content:
<system_instruction>
You are working inside Conductor, a Mac app that lets the user run many coding agents in parallel.
Your work should take place in the /Users/fcamblor/conductor/workspaces/mac-ai-trackers/santo-domingo directory (unless otherwise directed), which has been set up for you to work in.
Each workspace has a .context directory (gitignored) where you can save files to collaborate with other agents.
The target branch for this workspace is origin/main. Use this for actions like diffing (`git diff origin/main...`) or creating PRs (`gh pr create --base main`).

Do not rename the current branch unless the user explicitly tells you to do so.

By default, the user will only see the last message that you send before stopping. Include all essential information in the last message. The intermediate messages will be collapsed and accessible by the user but not displayed by default.

If the user asks you to work on several unrelated tasks, you can suggest they start new workspaces.
Sometimes the user might send you a message they meant to send in a different workspace or a different chat. If something doesn't make sense in the context of your work, just ask. 
If the user asks for help with Conductor, you can ask them to go to "Help -> Send Feedback" to get in touch with our team.
</system_instruction>

J'aimerais qu'on se place à niveau un peu plus meta sur le projet, pour permettre d'industrialiser le fait d'ajouter de nouvelles implémentations d'AI Assistants sur le projet.
S'inspirer notamment de la branche récente qui introduit copilot (copilot-cli-usage-api-research)

Les sujets que j'aimerais qu'on traite (il faut peut-être faire 1 fichier plan par problématique car il y a de la recherche profonde à faire dessus à chaque fois) :
1/ comment faire en sorte de "plugin-ifier" la codebase : fournir un cadre a toutes les problématiques/contrats que doivent implémenter un nouvel assistant sur le projet (récupération de l'auth, récupération des stats d'usage, conversion dans le format "standard" persisté (usages.json), configuration de l'icone de l'assistant, hypothèses et recherches documentaires sur les appels d'APIs, documentation claire des APIs appelées et des différents cas de payloads retournées (time windows, pay as you go, per-request...) etc..)

2/ créer un skill ou des guidelines pour guider un LLM sur l'implémentation qui suit ce cadre, puis formatlise le workflow à suivre au sein d'une PR :
- ouverture d'une PR
- review de cette dernière, s'assurer que la documentation est claire sur le support de l'assistant
- génération d'un build nightly avec le nouvel assistant et attachment de ce build sur la PR (+ guidelines sur comment reconstruire facilement ce build nightly pour les gens qui ne trusteraient pas le dmg fourni)
- attente de testeurs qui ont un abonnement pour le nouvel assistant, et qui permettent de donner du feedback sur l'implémentation avec leur abonnement propre
- une fois qu'on a 2-3 personnes qui ont ✅ l'implémentation, on merge la PR et on officialise le support de l'assistant en question

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena
CRITICAL: Before starting to work on a coding task, call the `initial_instructions` tool to read the 'Serena Instructions Manual'.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits détectés, langue détectée, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- typescript-standards:enforce-ts-standards: Enforce type-safe TypeScript patterns with Zod validation, pattern matching, and discriminated unions to prevent runtime errors. Use when writing TypeScript code, reviewing for type safety, designing data validation flows, or refactoring to eliminate impossible states.
- oss-security-audit:audit-oss-package: Security audit of a third-party package (npm, pip, homebrew, cargo, gem…) before
using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the
package manifest.
Use when the user says "is this package safe", "audit this dependency",
"check this npm/pip/brew package", "can I trust this library", or asks
whether a package is trustworthy before adding it to a project.
- impeccable:animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- impeccable:polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.

Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.

content.content:
[33mcommit 1fadbe51da22614ae6e5751a4e67702d068946af[m
Author: Frédéric Camblor <fcamblor@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed May 6 15:16:14 2026 +0200

    feat(copilot): implement GitHub Copilot CLI usage tracker with auth and monitoring
    
    Adds complete GitHub Copilot CLI usage tracking to AIUsagesTrackers, enabling
    users to monitor premium requests and usage across free/paid subscription plans.
    
    ## Implementation
    
    - CopilotAuth: Three-tier token resolution (env → keychain → hosts.yml) matching
      gh CLI conventions, with go-keyring base64 decoding and minimal YAML parsing
    - CopilotConnector: Actor conforming to UsageConnector protocol, parsing both
      quota_snapshots (paid plans: percent-remaining) and limited_user_quotas (free
      tier: absolute counts), with monthly 30-day window synthesis for UI progress
    - CopilotActiveAccountMonitor: Polls gh hosts.yml every 15s to detect account
      switches (gh auth switch) and invalidate cached login
    - Payload masking in logs to strip token, key, secret, password, email, credential
    - 454 tests covering auth token cascade, hosts.yml parsing variants, API response
      parsing, HTTP error codes, metric calculation, date normalization
    
    ## Bug fixes
    
    - Remove unlimited:true filter that was suppressing all premium request metrics.
      The unlimited field indicates a soft allowance (overage billed separately),
      not "no quota" — percent_remaining remains the meaningful signal per openusage.
    - GitHub's quota_reset_date and limited_user_reset_date are calendar dates
      (yyyy-MM-dd), not full ISO 8601 datetimes. Now recognized and normalized to
      UTC midnight (T00:00:00Z) via ISODate.parsingFlexibleDate.
    
    ## Core improvements
    
    - ISODate.parsing(_:) and isStrictlyValid: Strict factory and contract property
      for boundary validation (connectors must produce strict ISO 8601 datetimes)
    - ISODate.parsingFlexibleDate(_:): Flexible factory accepting strict datetime or
      yyyy-MM-dd calendar date (promoted to UTC midnight)
    - DEBUG assertion in UsageMetric.encode(to:) for .timeWindow resetAt: Enforces
      the connector contract at the core data model boundary; production stays
      permissive for round-trip of pre-existing legacy data
    - Numeric value bridge for API responses: Handles Int/Double/NSNumber for
      percent_remaining (JSONSerialization may wrap scalars as NSNumber)
    
    ## Assets and wiring
    
    - VendorBranding: Added Copilot with displayName "Copilot CLI", tint color
      GitHub dark gray (24292F), and generated copilot-mark.pdf asset
    - AppDelegate: Wire CopilotConnector into UsagePoller and start
      CopilotActiveAccountMonitor on startup
    - Logger: Added copilot-usages-connector.log stream for debug/diagnostic output
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
    Entire-Checkpoint: eb86658456fb

      UTC midnight (T00:00:00Z) via ISODate.parsingFlexibleDate.
    
    ## Core improvements
    
    - ISODate.parsing(_:) and isStrictlyValid: Strict factory and contract property
      for boundary validation (connectors must produce strict ISO 8601 datetimes)
    - ISODate.parsingFlexibleDate(_:): Flexible factory accepting strict datetime or
      yyyy-MM-dd calendar date (promoted to UTC midnight)
    - DEBUG assertion in UsageMetric.encode(to:) for .timeWindow resetAt: Enforces
      the connector contract at the core data model boundary; production stays
      permissive for round-trip of pre-existing legacy data
    - Numeric value bridge for API responses: Handles Int/Double/NSNumber for
      percent_remaining (JSONSerialization may wrap scalars as NSNumber)
    
    ## Assets and wiring
    
    - VendorBranding: Added Copilot with displayName "Copilot CLI", tint color
      GitHub dark gray (24292F), and generated copilot-mark.pdf asset
    - AppDelegate: Wire CopilotConnector into UsagePoller and start
      CopilotActiveAccountMonitor on startup
    - Logger: Added copilot-usages-connector.log stream for debug/diagnostic output

1	import Foundation
2	
3	// MARK: - Root
4	
5	/// The top-level file model: `{ usages: [...], outages?: [...] }`.
6	///
7	/// `outages` is an optional sibling written by an upstream status-fetching process.
8	/// When no incident is ongoing the upstream writer is expected to drop the key
9	/// entirely; the encoder here honours that convention by emitting nothing when
10	/// the in-memory array is empty.
11	public struct UsagesFile: Codable, Equatable, Sendable {
12	    public var usages: [VendorUsageEntry]
13	    public var outages: [Outage]
14	
15	    public init(usages: [VendorUsageEntry] = [], outages: [Outage] = []) {
16	        self.usages = usages
17	        self.outages = outages
18	    }
19	
20	    /// Grouped view for the UI — vendors without outages are omitted.
21	    public var outagesByVendor: [Vendor: [Outage]] {
22	        Dictionary(grouping: outages, by: \.vendor)
23	    }
24	
25	    private enum CodingKeys: String, CodingKey {
26	        case usages, outages
27	    }
28	
29	    public init(from decoder: Decoder) throws {
30	        let container = try decoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
31	        usages = try container.decodeIfPresent([VendorUsageEntry].self, forKey: .usages) ?? []
32	        outages = try container.decodeIfPresent([Outage].self, forKey: .outages) ?? []
33	    }
34	
35	    public func encode(to encoder: Encoder) throws {
36	        var container = encoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
37	        try container.encode(usages, forKey: .usages)
38	        // Upstream convention: absent key when no ongoing outage — mirror that on write
39	        // so the file never carries a stale empty array between incidents.
40	        if !outages.isEmpty {
41	            try container.encode(outages, forKey: .outages)
42	        }
43	    }
44	}
45

48	public struct VendorUsageEntry: Codable, Equatable, Sendable, Identifiable {
49	    public var id: String { "\(vendor.rawValue):\(account.rawValue)" }
50	
51	    public let vendor: Vendor
52	    public let account: AccountEmail
53	    public var isActive: Bool
54	    public var lastAcquiredOn: ISODate?
55	    public var lastError: UsageError?
56	    public var metrics: [UsageMetric]
57	
58	    public init(
59	        vendor: Vendor,
60	        account: AccountEmail,
61	        isActive: Bool = false,
62	        lastAcquiredOn: ISODate? = nil,
63	        lastError: UsageError? = nil,
64	        metrics: [UsageMetric] = []
65	    ) {
66	        self.vendor = vendor
67	        self.account = account
68	        self.isActive = isActive
69	        self.lastAcquiredOn = lastAcquiredOn
70	        self.lastError = lastError
71	        self.metrics = metrics
72	    }
73	}

75	// MARK: - Error
76	
77	public struct UsageError: Codable, Equatable, Sendable {
78	    public let timestamp: ISODate
79	    public let type: String
80	
81	    public init(timestamp: ISODate, type: String) {
82	        self.timestamp = timestamp
83	        self.type = type
84	    }
85	}
86	
87	// MARK: - Outage
88	
89	/// An active incident on a vendor's platform, written by an upstream status-fetching process.
90	/// The upstream writer removes entries as incidents resolve, so a missing or empty
91	/// `outages` array means "all clear".
92	public struct Outage: Codable, Equatable, Sendable, Identifiable {
93	    public let vendor: Vendor
94	    public let errorMessage: String
95	    public let severity: OutageSeverity
96	    public let since: ISODate
97	    public let href: URL?
98	
99	    /// Composite id — outages have no stable upstream id in this shape, so we
100	    /// derive one from the fields that together identify a single incident row.
101	    public var id: String {
102	        "\(vendor.rawValue)|\(since.rawValue)|\(href?.absoluteString ?? "")"
103	    }
104	
105	    public init(
106	        vendor: Vendor,
107	        errorMessage: String,
108	        severity: OutageSeverity,
109	        since: ISODate,
110	        href: URL? = nil
111	    ) {
112	        self.vendor = vendor
113	        self.errorMessage = errorMessage
114	        self.severity = severity
115	        self.since = since
116	        self.href = href
117	    }
118	
119	    private enum CodingKeys: String, CodingKey {
120	        case vendor, errorMessage, severity, since, href
121	    }
122	
123	    public init(from decoder: Decoder) throws {
124	        let container = try decoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
125	        vendor = try container.decode(Vendor.self, forKey: .vendor)
126	        errorMessage = try container.decode(String.self, forKey: .errorMessage)
127	        severity = try container.decode(OutageSeverity.self, forKey: .severity)
128	        since = try container.decode(ISODate.self, forKey: .since)
129	        if let hrefString = try container.decodeIfPresent(String.self, forKey: .href) {
130	            guard let parsed = URL(string: hrefString) else {
131	                throw DecodingError.dataCorruptedError(
132	                    forKey: .href, in: container,
133	                    debugDescription: "Invalid URL string: \(hrefString)")
134	            }
135	            href = parsed
136	        } else {
137	            href = nil
138	        }
139	    }
140	}
141	
142	// MARK: - Metric (polymorphic on "type" discriminator)
143	
144	public enum UsageMetric: Codable, Equatable, Sendable {
145	    case timeWindow(name: String, resetAt: ISODate?, windowDuration: DurationMinutes, usagePercent: UsagePercent)
146	    case payAsYouGo(name: String, currentAmount: Double, currency: String)
147	    /// A metric type not yet known to this client; retained for round-trip fidelity.
148	    case unknown(String)
149	
150	    // MARK: Kind
151	
152	    public var kind: MetricKind {
153	        switch self {
154	        case .timeWindow:       .timeWindow
155	        case .payAsYouGo:       .payAsYouGo
156	        case .unknown(let t):   .unknown(t)
157	        }
158	    }
159	
160	    // MARK: Coding
161	
162	    private enum CodingKeys: String, CodingKey {
163	        case type, name, resetAt
164	        case windowDuration = "windowDurationMinutes"
165	        case usagePercent, currentAmount, currency
166	    }
167	
168	    public init(from decoder: Decoder) throws {
169	        let container = try decoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
170	        let metricKind = try container.decode(MetricKind.self, forKey: .type)
171	        switch metricKind {
172	        case .timeWindow:
173	            self = .timeWindow(
174	                name: try container.decode(String.self, forKey: .name),
175	                resetAt: try container.decodeIfPresent(ISODate.self, forKey: .resetAt),
176	                windowDuration: try container.decode(DurationMinutes.self, forKey: .windowDuration),
177	                usagePercent: try container.decode(UsagePercent.self, forKey: .usagePercent)
178	            )
179	        case .payAsYouGo:
180	            self = .payAsYouGo(
181	                name: try container.decode(String.self, forKey: .name),
182	                currentAmount: try container.decode(Double.self, forKey: .currentAmount),
183	                currency: try container.decode(String.self, forKey: .currency)
184	            )
185	        case .unknown(let t):
186	            self = .unknown(t)
187	        }
188	    }
189	
190	    public func encode(to encoder: Encoder) throws {
191	        var container = encoder.container(keyedBy: CodingKeys.self)
192	        switch self {
193	        case let .timeWindow(name, resetAt, windowDuration, usagePercent):
194	            try container.encode(MetricKind.timeWindow, forKey: .type)
195	            try container.encode(name, forKey: .name)
196	            try container.encodeIfPresent(resetAt, forKey: .resetAt)
197	            try container.encode(windowDuration, forKey: .windowDuration)
198	            try container.encode(usagePercent, forKey: .usagePercent)
199	        case let .payAsYouGo(name, currentAmount, currency):
200	            try container.encode(MetricKind.payAsYouGo, forKey: .type)
201	            try container.encode(name, forKey: .name)
202	            try container.encode(currentAmount, forKey: .currentAmount)
203	            try container.encode(currency, forKey: .currency)
204	        case let .unknown(t):
205	            // Only the type discriminator round-trips; unknown fields are not preserved
206	            try container.encode(MetricKind.unknown(t), forKey: .type)
207	        }
208	    }
209	}

5	enum VendorBranding {
6	    struct Brand: Equatable, Sendable {
7	        let assetName: String
8	        let displayName: String
9	        let tintHex: String
10	    }
11	
12	    static func brand(for vendor: Vendor) -> Brand? {
13	        switch vendor {
14	        case .claude:
15	            Brand(
16	                assetName: "claude-mark",
17	                displayName: "Claude Code",
18	                tintHex: "DA7756"
19	            )
20	        case .codex:
21	            Brand(
22	                assetName: "codex-mark",
23	                displayName: "Codex",
24	                tintHex: "10A37F"
25	            )
26	        default:
27	            nil
28	        }
29	    }
30	
31	    static func displayName(for vendor: Vendor) -> String {
32	        brand(for: vendor)?.displayName ?? vendor.rawValue
33	    }
34	
35	    static func tint(for vendor: Vendor) -> Color? {
36	        guard let tintHex = brand(for: vendor)?.tintHex else { return nil }
37	        return Color(hex: tintHex)
38	    }
39	
40	    static func icon(for vendor: Vendor) -> NSImage? {
41	        guard let assetName = brand(for: vendor)?.assetName else { return nil }
42	        return loadTemplateImage(named: assetName)
43	    }
44	
45	    /// Pixels of white halo added on each side around the vendor icon for legibility.
46	    static let iconGlowPadding: CGFloat = 3.0
47	
48	    /// Returns a tinted, non-template copy of the vendor icon at the requested height.
49	    /// Uses the brand's `tintHex` as fill color and the template PDF as an alpha mask.
50	    /// A crisp white halo is produced by drawing a white silhouette at pixel offsets
51	    /// in all directions — no blur, so the icon stays sharp on any menu bar tint color.
52	    @MainActor
53	    static func tintedNSImage(for vendor: Vendor, height: CGFloat) -> NSImage? {
54	        guard let template = icon(for: vendor),
55	              let brand = brand(for: vendor),
56	              let tintColor = NSColor(hex: brand.tintHex) else { return nil }
57	        let aspectRatio = template.size.height > 0
58	            ? template.size.width / template.size.height : 1
59	        let iconSize = NSSize(width: ceil(height * aspectRatio), height: ceil(height))
60	        let pad = Self.iconGlowPadding
61	        let canvasSize = NSSize(width: iconSize.width + pad * 2, height: iconSize.height + pad * 2)
62	        let iconRect = NSRect(x: pad, y: pad, width: iconSize.width, height: iconSize.height)
63	
64	        let tinted = NSImage(size: iconSize)
65	        tinted.lockFocus()
66	        tintColor.setFill()
67	        NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize).fill()
68	        template.draw(
69	            in: NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize),
70	            from: .zero,
71	            operation: .destinationIn,
72	            fraction: 1.0
73	        )
74	        tinted.unlockFocus()
75	
76	        let white = NSImage(size: iconSize)
77	        white.lockFocus()
78	        NSColor.white.setFill()
79	        NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize).fill()
80	        template.draw(
81	            in: NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize),
82	            from: .zero,
83	            operation: .destinationIn,
84	            fraction: 1.0
85	        )
86	        white.unlockFocus()
87	
88	        let result = NSImage(size: canvasSize)
89	        result.lockFocus()
90	        // Draw the white silhouette at every surrounding pixel offset to build a crisp halo.
91	        for dx: CGFloat in [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2] {
92	            for dy: CGFloat in [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2] where !(dx == 0 && dy == 0) {
93	                white.draw(in: NSRect(
94	                    x: iconRect.minX + dx, y: iconRect.minY + dy,
95	                    width: iconRect.width, height: iconRect.height
96	                ))
97	            }
98	        }
99	        tinted.draw(in: iconRect)
100	        result.unlockFocus()
101	        result.isTemplate = false
102	        return result
103	    }
104	
105	    /// Resolves the SwiftPM resource bundle without ever touching `Bundle.module`,
106	    /// whose synthesized accessor traps with a precondition failure when the
107	    /// expected `.bundle` is missing next to the executable. We probe every
108	    /// location the bundle may legitimately land in across `swift run`,
109	    /// `swift build`, the assembled `.app`, and merged-into-main-bundle layouts —
110	    /// returning `nil` instead of crashing the app when none match.
111	    private static let resourceBundleName = "AIUsagesTrackers_AIUsagesTrackers"
112	
113	    private static let resourceBundle: Bundle? = {
114	        let bundleFile = "\(resourceBundleName).bundle"
115	        let mainURL = Bundle.main.bundleURL
116	        let tokenBundle = Bundle(for: BundleToken.self)
117	        let candidates: [URL?] = [
118	            Bundle.main.resourceURL,
119	            mainURL,
120	            mainURL.appendingPathComponent("Contents/MacOS"),
121	            mainURL.appendingPathComponent("Contents/Resources"),
122	            tokenBundle.resourceURL,
123	            tokenBundle.bundleURL,
124	        ]
125	        for candidate in candidates.compactMap({ $0 }) {
126	            let bundleURL = candidate.appendingPathComponent(bundleFile)
127	            if let bundle = Bundle(url: bundleURL) {
128	                return bundle
129	            }
130	        }
131	        return nil
132	    }()
133	
134	    private static func loadTemplateImage(named assetName: String) -> NSImage? {
135	        let url = locateAsset(named: assetName, fileExtension: "pdf", subdirectory: "VendorBranding")
136	        guard let url, let image = NSImage(contentsOf: url) else { return nil }
137	        image.isTemplate = true
138	        return image
139	    }
140	
141	    private static func locateAsset(
142	        named assetName: String,
143	        fileExtension: String,
144	        subdirectory: String
145	    ) -> URL? {
146	        if let url = Bundle.main.url(
147	            forResource: assetName,
148	            withExtension: fileExtension,
149	            subdirectory: subdirectory
150	        ) {
151	            return url
152	        }
153	        if let url = resourceBundle?.url(
154	            forResource: assetName,
155	            withExtension: fileExtension,
156	            subdirectory: subdirectory
157	        ) {
158	            return url
159	        }
160	        return nil
161	    }
162	}
163	
164	private final class BundleToken {}
165	
166	struct VendorIconView: View {
167	    let vendor: Vendor
168	    var size: CGFloat = 14
169	
170	    var body: some View {
171	        if let image = VendorBranding.icon(for: vendor),
172	           let tint = VendorBranding.tint(for: vendor) {
173	            Image(nsImage: image)
174	                .renderingMode(.template)
175	                .resizable()

179	                .accessibilityHidden(true)
180	        } else {

182	            // letter so the rest of the UI stays informative instead of blank.
183	            Text(VendorBranding.displayName(for: vendor).prefix(1).uppercased())
184	                .font(.system(size: size * 0.8, weight: .semibold))
185	                .foregroundStyle(VendorBranding.tint(for: vendor) ?? .primary)
186	                .frame(width: size, height: size)

204	private extension Color {
205	    init?(hex: String) {
206	        guard hex.count == 6, let value = Int(hex, radix: 16) else { return nil }
207	
208	        let red = Double((value >> 16) & 0xFF) / 255.0
209	        let green = Double((value >> 8) & 0xFF) / 255.0
210	        let blue = Double(value & 0xFF) / 255.0
211	
212	        self.init(red: red, green: green, blue: blue)
213	    }
214	}
215	
216	private extension NSColor {
217	    convenience init?(hex: String) {
218	        guard hex.count == 6, let value = Int(hex, radix: 16) else { return nil }
219	
220	        let red = CGFloat((value >> 16) & 0xFF) / 255.0
221	        let green = CGFloat((value >> 8) & 0xFF) / 255.0
222	        let blue = CGFloat(value & 0xFF) / 255.0
223	
224	        self.init(red: red, green: green, blue: blue, alpha: 1.0)
225	    }

5	
6	@MainActor
7	final class AppDelegate: NSObject, NSApplicationDelegate {
8	    private var poller: UsagePoller?
9	    private var snapshotScheduler: SnapshotScheduler?
10	    private var accountMonitor: ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor?
11	    private var codexMonitor: CodexActiveAccountMonitor?
12	    private var pidGuard: AppPidGuard?
13	    private var usageStore: UsageStore?
14	    private var refreshState: RefreshState?
15	    private var statusItem: NSStatusItem?
16	    private var popover: NSPopover?
17	    private var appearanceObserver: NSKeyValueObservation?
18	    private var settingsWindowObserver: NSObjectProtocol?
19	
20	    /// Shared preferences store — exposed as static so the SwiftUI Settings scene
21	    /// (constructed before applicationDidFinishLaunching) can access it.
22	    static let sharedPreferences: UserDefaultsAppPreferences = UserDefaultsAppPreferences()
23	
24	    /// Shared usage store — populated in applicationDidFinishLaunching. The Settings
25	    /// scene is instantiated before the store exists, so it must read through this
26	    /// mutable holder. Nil until the first launch callback has run.
27	    static var sharedStore: UsageStore?
28	
29	    func applicationDidFinishLaunching(_ notification: Notification) {
30	        NSApplication.shared.setActivationPolicy(.accessory)
31	
32	        // No .app bundle ships with this SwiftPM executable, so AppKit has no
33	        // static icon slot — set the Dock / Cmd+Tab icon programmatically.
34	        if let icon = AppIconRenderer.makeImage(pixelSize: 1024) {
35	            NSApplication.shared.applicationIconImage = icon
36	        }
37	
38	        let home = FileManager.default.homeDirectoryForCurrentUser.path
39	        let guard_ = AppPidGuard(cacheDir: "\(home)/.cache/ai-usages-tracker")
40	        do {
41	            try guard_.acquire()
42	        } catch AppPidGuardError.alreadyRunning(let pid, _) {
43	            let alert = NSAlert()
44	            alert.messageText = "AI Usages Tracker is already running"
45	            alert.informativeText = "Another instance is active (PID \(pid)). Use the menu bar icon to quit it before launching a new one."
46	            alert.runModal()
47	            NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
48	            return
49	        } catch {
50	            let alert = NSAlert()
51	            alert.messageText = "Failed to start AI Usages Tracker"
52	            alert.informativeText = error.localizedDescription
53	            alert.runModal()
54	            NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
55	            return
56	        }
57	        pidGuard = guard_
58	
59	        Loggers.setPreferences(Self.sharedPreferences)
60	
61	        MenuBarSegmentsSeeder.seedIfNeeded(preferences: Self.sharedPreferences)
62	        ChartConfigurationsSeeder.seedIfNeeded(preferences: Self.sharedPreferences)
63	
64	        // Reconcile launch-at-login preference with system state — the user may
65	        // have disabled the entry in System Settings > Login Items directly.
66	        let launchService = LaunchAtLoginService.shared
67	        if Self.sharedPreferences.launchAtLogin != launchService.isEnabled {
68	            Self.sharedPreferences.launchAtLogin = launchService.isEnabled
69	        }
70	
71	        let fileManager = UsagesFileManager.shared
72	        let refreshState = RefreshState()
73	        let codexConnector = CodexConnector()
74	        let poller = UsagePoller(
75	            connectors: [ClaudeCodeConnector(), codexConnector],
76	            statusConnectors: [ClaudeStatusConnector(), CodexStatusConnector()],
77	            fileManager: fileManager,
78	            refreshState: refreshState,
79	            preferences: Self.sharedPreferences
80	        )
81	        let accountMonitor = ClaudeActiveAccountMonitor(
82	            fileManager: fileManager,
83	            onActiveAccountChanged: { [weak poller] _ in
84	                await poller?.pollOnce(force: true)
85	            }
86	        )
87	        let codexMonitor = CodexActiveAccountMonitor(
88	            onActiveAccountChanged: { [weak codexConnector, weak poller] _ in
89	                await codexConnector?.invalidateEmailCache()
90	                await poller?.pollOnce(force: true)
91	            }
92	        )
93	        let snapshotRecorder = SnapshotRecorder()
94	        let historyReader = UsageHistoryReader(rootPath: snapshotRecorder.rootPath)
95	        let snapshotScheduler = SnapshotScheduler(
96	            fileManager: fileManager,
97	            recorder: snapshotRecorder
98	        )
99	        self.poller = poller
100	        self.snapshotScheduler = snapshotScheduler
101	        self.accountMonitor = accountMonitor
102	        self.codexMonitor = codexMonitor
103	        self.refreshState = refreshState
104	
105	        let usagesPath = fileManager.filePath
106	        let fileWatcher = UsagesFileWatcher(path: usagesPath)
107	        let store = UsageStore(fileWatcher: fileWatcher, preferences: Self.sharedPreferences)
108	        self.usageStore = store
109	        Self.sharedStore = store
110	
111	        setupStatusItem(store: store, refreshState: refreshState, historyReader: historyReader)
112	        trackStoreChanges(store: store)
113	
114	        Task {
115	            await StartupMigrationRunner(fileManager: fileManager).run()
116	            store.start()
117	            await poller.start()
118	            await snapshotScheduler.start()
119	            await accountMonitor.start()
120	            await codexMonitor.start()
121	        }
122	    }
123	
124	    func applicationWillTerminate(_ notification: Notification) {
125	        quit()
126	    }
127	
128	    // MARK: - Status item
129	
130	    private func setupStatusItem(
131	        store: UsageStore,
132	        refreshState: RefreshState,
133	        historyReader: UsageHistoryReader
134	    ) {
135	        let item = NSStatusBar.system.statusItem(withLength: NSStatusItem.variableLength)
136	        statusItem = item
137	
138	        let popover = NSPopover()
139	        popover.behavior = .transient
140	        let hosting = NSHostingController(
141	            rootView: UsageDetailsView(
142	                store: store,
143	                refreshState: refreshState,
144	                historyReader: historyReader,
145	                onRefresh: { [weak self] in
146	                    await self?.poller?.pollOnce(now: Date(), force: true)
147	                },
148	                onOpenSettings: { [weak self] in
149	                    self?.openSettings()
150	                },
151	                onQuit: { [weak self] in
152	                    self?.quit()
153	                }
154	            )
155	        )
156	        // Let SwiftUI's intrinsic size drive the popover so it grows with content
157	        // up to the screen-ratio cap enforced inside UsageDetailsView.
158	        hosting.sizingOptions = [.preferredContentSize]
159	        popover.contentViewController = hosting
160	        self.popover = popover
161	
162	        if let button = item.button {
163	            button.target = self
164	            button.action = #selector(togglePopover(_:))
165	            // The menu bar appearance can change (wallpaper-driven tinting) without
166	            // the app's effectiveAppearance changing, so we observe the button itself.
167	            appearanceObserver = button.observe(\.effectiveAppearance, options: [.new]) { [weak self] _, _ in
168	                Task { @MainActor in self?.refreshStatusItemImage() }
169	            }
170	        }
171	
172	        refreshStatusItemImage()
173	    }
174	
175	    private func refreshStatusItemImage() {
176	        guard let store = usageStore, let button = statusItem?.button else { return }
177	        let isDark = button.effectiveAppearance.bestMatch(from: [.darkAqua, .aqua]) == .darkAqua
178	        let isUnconfigured = Self.sharedPreferences.menuBarSegments.isEmpty
179	        let image = MenuBarLabelRenderer.render(
180	            segments: store.menuBarSegments,
181	            separator: Self.sharedPreferences.menuBarSeparator,
182	            fallbackText: store.menuBarText,
183	            isDarkMenuBar: isDark,
184	            isUnconfigured: isUnconfigured
185	        )
186	        button.image = image
187	    }
188	
189	    @objc private func togglePopover(_ sender: Any?) {
190	        guard let popover, let button = statusItem?.button else { return }
191	        if popover.isShown {
192	            popover.performClose(sender)
193	        } else {
194	            popover.show(relativeTo: button.bounds, of: button, preferredEdge: .minY)
195	            popover.contentViewController?.view.window?.makeKey()
196	        }
197	    }
198	
199	    // MARK: - Observation
200	
201	    /// Re-arms `withObservationTracking` after every change so the status item
202	    /// keeps mirroring the store. Tracking fires exactly once per registration.
203	    private func trackStoreChanges(store: UsageStore) {
204	        withObservationTracking {
205	            _ = store.menuBarSegments
206	            _ = store.menuBarText
207	        } onChange: { [weak self] in
208	            Task { @MainActor in
209	                guard let self, let store = self.usageStore else { return }
210	                self.refreshStatusItemImage()
211	                self.trackStoreChanges(store: store)
212	            }
213	        }
214	    }
215	
216	    // MARK: - Settings
217	
218	    private func openSettings() {
219	        popover?.performClose(nil)
220	        // .accessory apps have no dock presence; showSettingsWindow: silently fails
221	        // unless we temporarily switch to .regular so AppKit can make the window key.
222	        NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.regular)
223	        NSApp.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true)
224	
225	        // SwiftUI creates the Settings window lazily on first showSettingsWindow: —
226	        // if it already exists, reuse it directly to avoid responder-chain races
227	        // that occur during the popover-dismissal + activation-policy transition.
228	        if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
229	            settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
230	        } else {
231	            // First-time path: AppKit needs a runloop tick after the .accessory →
232	            // .regular transition to install the standard App menu (which is where
233	            // SwiftUI wires the Settings… action). sendAction with a nil target
234	            // relies on that wiring, so we trigger the menu item directly once
235	            // AppKit has had a chance to build it.
236	            DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.15) {
237	                Self.triggerSettingsMenuItem()
238	                if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
239	                    settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
240	                }
241	            }
242	        }
243	
244	        // Restore .accessory policy once the settings window is closed.
245	        if settingsWindowObserver == nil {
246	            // Use selector-based observer so the callback runs on @MainActor naturally,
247	            // avoiding Sendable-crossing issues with block-based addObserver(queue:).
248	            settingsWindowObserver = self
249	            NotificationCenter.default.addObserver(
250	                self,
251	                selector: #selector(handleSettingsWindowWillClose(_:)),
252	                name: NSWindow.willCloseNotification,
253	                object: nil
254	            )
255	        }
256	    }
257	
258	    @objc private func handleSettingsWindowWillClose(_ notification: Notification) {
259	        // Only react to the Settings window closing — popovers and other
260	        // transient windows also emit willClose.
261	        guard let closingWindow = notification.object as? NSWindow else { return }
262	        let windowIdentifier = closingWindow.identifier?.rawValue ?? ""
263	        guard windowIdentifier.contains("Settings") || windowIdentifier.contains("Preferences") else { return }
264	        // willClose fires while the window is still visible and before
265	        // AppKit tears it down; defer to the next runloop tick so the
266	        // activation-policy change isn't overridden by pending AppKit work.
267	        DispatchQueue.main.async { [weak self] in
268	            NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.accessory)
269	            // Accessory transition doesn't always remove the app from the
270	            // Cmd+Tab / app-switcher list until another app is activated.
271	            if let frontmost = NSWorkspace.shared.runningApplications.first(where: {
272	                $0.isActive && $0.processIdentifier != ProcessInfo.processInfo.processIdentifier
273	            }) {
274	                frontmost.activate()
275	            } else {
276	                NSApp.hide(nil)
277	            }
278	            guard let self else { return }
279	            NotificationCenter.default.removeObserver(self, name: NSWindow.willCloseNotification, object: nil)
280	            self.settingsWindowObserver = nil
281	        }
282	    }
283	
284	    /// SwiftUI tags its Settings window with an identifier containing "Settings"
285	    /// (or "Preferences" on older SDKs). Match on the stable identifier rather
286	    /// than localized titles.
287	    private static func findSettingsWindow() -> NSWindow? {
288	        NSApp.windows.first { window in
289	            let identifier = window.identifier?.rawValue ?? ""
290	            return identifier.contains("Settings") || identifier.contains("Preferences")
291	        }
292	    }
293	
294	    /// Walk the main menu looking for the App menu's Settings… (or Preferences…)
295	    /// item and invoke it. Going through the menu guarantees the action is
296	    /// routed to whichever responder SwiftUI wired up, instead of relying on
297	    /// `sendAction(_:to:from:)` to resolve a nil target in the responder chain
298	    /// — which is unreliable right after an activation-policy switch.
299	    private static func triggerSettingsMenuItem() {
300	        guard let mainMenu = NSApp.mainMenu else { return }
301	        for topItem in mainMenu.items {
302	            guard let submenu = topItem.submenu else { continue }
303	            for (index, item) in submenu.items.enumerated() {
304	                let title = item.title
305	                if title.hasPrefix("Settings") || title.hasPrefix("Preferences") {
306	                    submenu.performActionForItem(at: index)
307	                    return
308	                }
309	            }
310	        }
311	    }
312	
313	    // MARK: - Quit
314	
315	    private func quit() {
316	        let pollerRef = poller
317	        let schedulerRef = snapshotScheduler
318	        let monitorRef = accountMonitor
319	        let codexMonitorRef = codexMonitor
320	        Task {
321	            await pollerRef?.stop()
322	            await schedulerRef?.stop()
323	            await monitorRef?.stop()
324	            await codexMonitorRef?.stop()
325	        }
326	        usageStore?.stop()
327	        pidGuard?.release()
328	        NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
329	    }
330	}
331

1	# Architecture
2	
3	An executable Swift Package produces a **menubar-only** macOS application: no Dock icon, no main window, and all interaction flows through a single menu bar item.
4	
5	Invariants worth preserving when modifying the codebase:
6	
7	- The app sets its activation policy to `.accessory` at startup. Removing this call makes macOS show a Dock icon and breaks the menubar-only contract.
8	- The UI is exposed through an AppKit `NSStatusItem` owned by `AppDelegate`, not SwiftUI's `MenuBarExtra`. `MenuBarExtra` was dropped after it proved unable to render per-segment colored indicators (template tinting strips colors, mixed HStacks silently truncate). See `docs/SWIFT-MENUBAR.md` before changing anything menu-bar related. User-visible features belong inside the popover's root SwiftUI view — there is intentionally no main `WindowGroup`.
9	
10	## Package structure
11	
12	The Swift package is split into a library target and an executable target, plus a test target that exercises the library. The library contains all domain logic; the executable is a thin SwiftUI entry point.
13	
14	## Usage-fetching pipeline
15	
16	A `UsageConnector` protocol abstracts vendor-specific API access. Each connector resolves an active account and fetches usage data asynchronously. A connector may also emit additional optional per-model `timeWindow` entries when the upstream API includes them — these travel through the same persistence and display pipeline as the mandatory session and weekly aggregates, and are silently omitted when the API does not provide them. The first concrete implementation targets the Claude API via OAuth tokens stored in the macOS Keychain.
17	
18	A polling actor periodically invokes all registered connectors in parallel and merges results into a shared JSON file.
19	
20	## Persistence
21	
22	Usage data is persisted as a JSON file at `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usages.json`. The schema is a top-level `usages` array where each entry is keyed by `(vendor, account)`. A dedicated actor (`UsagesFileManager`) serializes all reads and writes for internal callers. Writes use the system's atomic write facility to prevent partial-file corruption. All public methods also acquire an advisory POSIX `flock` on a dedicated lock file (`usages.json.lock`) before touching the data file, so external readers (widgets, scripts) that do the same can safely coordinate access.
23	
24	History is persisted separately as append-only JSONL snapshots under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usage-history/YYYY/MM/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl`, partitioned by UTC date. `SnapshotRecorder` writes one `TickSnapshot` line only when the flattened account/metric payload changes. Expired time-window metrics are recorded as null values, which lets chart rendering break lines across reset or missing-data boundaries instead of interpolating through them.
25	
26	## Display pipeline
27	
28	A file watcher observes `usages.json` using a hybrid strategy: a kernel `DispatchSource` fires on `.write`/`.delete`/`.rename` events, backed by a polling timer that re-checks on a configurable polling interval to handle atomic replaces and network-mount edge cases. Events are debounced to coalesce rapid successive writes into a single read.
29	
30	An `@Observable` store (running on `@MainActor`) receives each new file snapshot, decodes it, tracks active accounts, and resolves the user's configured menu bar segments. A secondary countdown timer periodically refreshes the remaining-time values in the display so they stay current between file changes. When data is unavailable or malformed the label falls back to `"--"`.
31	
32	When the user clicks the menu bar item, `AppDelegate` toggles an `NSPopover` hosting the root SwiftUI view via `NSHostingController`. The popover renders one card per vendor/account entry, sorted alphabetically by vendor then by account (active accounts first within a vendor). Each card shows time-window metrics (gauge bar, theoretical pace marker, remaining time, next reset date) and pay-as-you-go metrics (amount with currency). An empty state is shown when no entries are available. A footer provides the app name and a Quit shortcut.
33	
34	The menu bar label itself is rasterised into a non-template `NSImage` by `MenuBarLabelRenderer`, so per-tier dot colors survive macOS's template tinting. Text color is picked from the status button's `effectiveAppearance` (which tracks the menu bar, not the app) and re-rendered whenever that appearance changes.
35	
36	## Usage history charts
37	
38	`UsageHistoryReader` recursively reads the JSONL history, filters points to the selected window, skips malformed lines, ignores future points, and reports whether older or newer data exists outside the current window for chart navigation. Decoded files are cached by modification date and size to keep repeated chart refreshes cheap.
39	
40	The history tab renders the user's `ChartConfiguration` list. Each chart is resolved through `ChartSeriesResolver`: `allAvailable` groups every history series with at least one non-null point, while `custom` follows the configured vendor/account/metric series and applies per-series label, color, and line-style preferences. A custom series targeting "currently active account" is resolved against the live store entries at render time.
41	
42	Null history points are kept until rendering. The chart panel omits them from `LineMark` values but uses them to increment segment identifiers, so Swift Charts draws separate line segments around reset gaps. Hover tooltips use pre-grouped series data and a nearest-point binary search rather than scanning every point on every hover event.
43	
44	## Account monitoring
45	
46	A separate monitoring actor polls the vendor's local config file at a short fixed interval to detect account switches in real time. When a switch is detected, it updates the `isActive` flag on the corresponding persistence entry without waiting for the next usage fetch. This separation keeps account-status latency low without coupling it to the (slower) API polling cadence.
47	
48	## Preferences
49	
50	An injectable preferences protocol backed by `UserDefaults` is the single source of truth for user-adjustable runtime behaviour. Consumers include the polling actor (refresh cadence), logging subsystem (verbosity), menu bar segment resolver, and history chart renderer. Changes made in the settings window take effect immediately — the poller re-reads the interval on every tick, the logger resolves its effective level on every log call, and SwiftUI observes the menu bar and chart configuration lists.
51	
52	The settings window is a standard SwiftUI `Settings` scene (tabbed, HIG chrome, `Cmd+,`). Because the app uses `.accessory` activation policy, opening the window requires explicitly activating the app before sending the `showSettingsWindow:` action.
53	
54	Launch-at-login state is reconciled at startup: if the user toggled the entry in System Settings > Login Items, the preferences store is updated to match the system state rather than overriding it.
55	
56	## Logging
57	
58	Two log files live under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/`: one for the app lifecycle and poller events, another for connector-specific activity. Log level is configurable via the `AI_TRACKER_LOG_LEVEL` environment variable; when that variable is absent, the level falls back to the user's setting in the preferences window (default: info). Size-based rotation keeps each file under 5 MB with one backup. A retention purge removes entries older than 7 days from all managed log files at startup and every 24 hours, using atomic rewrites so a crash mid-cleanup leaves the previous file intact.
59	
60	For authoritative details (Swift tools version, platform minimums, target layout), read the package manifest rather than mirroring them here.
61

# Architecture

An executable Swift Package produces a **menubar-only** macOS application: no Dock icon, no main window, and all interaction flows through a single menu bar item.

Invariants worth preserving when modifying the codebase:

- The app sets its activation policy to `.accessory` at startup. Removing this call makes macOS show a Dock icon and breaks the menubar-only contract.
- The UI is exposed through an AppKit `NSStatusItem` owned by `AppDelegate`, not SwiftUI's `MenuBarExtra`. `MenuBarExtra` was dropped after it proved unable to render per-segment colored indicators (template tinting strips colors, mixed HStacks silently truncate). See `docs/SWIFT-MENUBAR.md` before changing anything menu-bar related. User-visible features belong inside the popover's root SwiftUI view — there is intentionally no main `WindowGroup`.

## Package structure

The Swift package is split into a library target and an executable target, plus a test target that exercises the library. The library contains all domain logic; the executable is a thin SwiftUI entry point.

## Usage-fetching pipeline

A `UsageConnector` protocol abstracts vendor-specific API access. Each connector resolves an active account and fetches usage data asynchronously. A connector may also emit additional optional per-model `timeWindow` entries when the upstream API includes them — these travel through the same persistence and display pipeline as the mandatory session and weekly aggregates, and are silently omitted when the API does not provide them. The first concrete implementation targets the Claude API via OAuth tokens stored in the macOS Keychain.

A polling actor periodically invokes all registered connectors in parallel and merges results into a shared JSON file.

## Persistence

Usage data is persisted as a JSON file at `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usages.json`. The schema is a top-level `usages` array where each entry is keyed by `(vendor, account)`. A dedicated actor (`UsagesFileManager`) serializes all reads and writes for internal callers. Writes use the system's atomic write facility to prevent partial-file corruption. All public methods also acquire an advisory POSIX `flock` on a dedicated lock file (`usages.json.lock`) before touching the data file, so external readers (widgets, scripts) that do the same can safely coordinate access.

History is persisted separately as append-only JSONL snapshots under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usage-history/YYYY/MM/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl`, partitioned by UTC date. `SnapshotRecorder` writes one `TickSnapshot` line only when the flattened account/metric payload changes. Expired time-window metrics are recorded as null values, which lets chart rendering break lines across reset or missing-data boundaries instead of interpolating through them.

## Display pipeline

A file watcher observes `usages.json` using a hybrid strategy: a kernel `DispatchSource` fires on `.write`/`.delete`/`.rename` events, backed by a polling timer that re-checks on a configurable polling interval to handle atomic replaces and network-mount edge cases. Events are debounced to coalesce rapid successive writes into a single read.

An `@Observable` store (running on `@MainActor`) receives each new file snapshot, decodes it, tracks active accounts, and resolves the user's configured menu bar segments. A secondary countdown timer periodically refreshes the remaining-time values in the display so they stay current between file changes. When data is unavailable or malformed the label falls back to `"--"`.

When the user clicks the menu bar item, `AppDelegate` toggles an `NSPopover` hosting the root SwiftUI view via `NSHostingController`. The popover renders one card per vendor/account entry, sorted alphabetically by vendor then by account (active accounts first within a vendor). Each card shows time-window metrics (gauge bar, theoretical pace marker, remaining time, next reset date) and pay-as-you-go metrics (amount with currency). An empty state is shown when no entries are available. A footer provides the app name and a Quit shortcut.

The menu bar label itself is rasterised into a non-template `NSImage` by `MenuBarLabelRenderer`, so per-tier dot colors survive macOS's template tinting. Text color is picked from the status button's `effectiveAppearance` (which tracks the menu bar, not the app) and re-rendered whenever that appearance changes.

## Usage history charts

`UsageHistoryReader` recursively reads the JSONL history, filters points to the selected window, skips malformed lines, ignores future points, and reports whether older or newer data exists outside the current window for chart navigation. Decoded files are cached by modification date and size to keep repeated chart refreshes cheap.

The history tab renders the user's `ChartConfiguration` list. Each chart is resolved through `ChartSeriesResolver`: `allAvailable` groups every history series with at least one non-null point, while `custom` follows the configured vendor/account/metric series and applies per-series label, color, and line-style preferences. A custom series targeting "currently active account" is resolved against the live store entries at render time.

Null history points are kept until rendering. The chart panel omits them from `LineMark` values but uses them to increment segment identifiers, so Swift Charts draws separate line segments around reset gaps. Hover tooltips use pre-grouped series data and a nearest-point binary search rather than scanning every point on every hover event.

## Account monitoring

A separate monitoring actor polls the vendor's local config file at a short fixed interval to detect account switches in real time. When a switch is detected, it updates the `isActive` flag on the corresponding persistence entry without waiting for the next usage fetch. This separation keeps account-status latency low without coupling it to the (slower) API polling cadence.

## Preferences

An injectable preferences protocol backed by `UserDefaults` is the single source of truth for user-adjustable runtime behaviour. Consumers include the polling actor (refresh cadence), logging subsystem (verbosity), menu bar segment resolver, and history chart renderer. Changes made in the settings window take effect immediately — the poller re-reads the interval on every tick, the logger resolves its effective level on every log call, and SwiftUI observes the menu bar and chart configuration lists.

The settings window is a standard SwiftUI `Settings` scene (tabbed, HIG chrome, `Cmd+,`). Because the app uses `.accessory` activation policy, opening the window requires explicitly activating the app before sending the `showSettingsWindow:` action.

Launch-at-login state is reconciled at startup: if the user toggled the entry in System Settings > Login Items, the preferences store is updated to match the system state rather than overriding it.

## Logging

Two log files live under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/`: one for the app lifecycle and poller events, another for connector-specific activity. Log level is configurable via the `AI_TRACKER_LOG_LEVEL` environment variable; when that variable is absent, the level falls back to the user's setting in the preferences window (default: info). Size-based rotation keeps each file under 5 MB with one backup. A retention purge removes entries older than 7 days from all managed log files at startup and every 24 hours, using atomic rewrites so a crash mid-cleanup leaves the previous file intact.

For authoritative details (Swift tools version, platform minimums, target layout), read the package manifest rather than mirroring them here.

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

---
paths: **/*.md
---

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

file.content:
# Roadmap process

Planned work lives under `roadmap/` at the repository root. Unlike files in `docs/`, roadmap entries intentionally describe future, transient state — they are exempt from the "write for longevity" clauses of `.claude/rules/markdown-authoring.md`.

## Layout

- `roadmap/index.md` — open epics with their status and a Mermaid dependency graph. Single source of truth for ordering, dependencies, and status.
- `roadmap/<slug>.md` — one file per epic. Slug is kebab-case, English, concise.

When a feature ships, **both its epic file and its index entry are deleted**. The index only ever lists features that remain to be implemented.

## Granularity

One file equals one epic: a user-visible capability large enough to warrant a named milestone, small enough to be described on a single page. Sub-tasks of an epic stay inside its file and do not get their own roadmap entry.

## Feature file template

Each epic file follows this exact structure (omit `Notes` when empty):

```markdown
# <Epic name>

## Goal

<One or two sentences: the user problem solved.>

## Dependencies

<Bulleted list of links to other roadmap files, or the single word `None.`.>

## Scope

<Bulleted list of what is included.>

**Out of scope**

<Bulleted list of explicit boundaries.>

## Acceptance criteria

<Bulleted list of verifiable conditions.>

## Notes

<Optional. Technical constraints or architectural intent.>
```

Status does NOT appear in feature files — it lives only in `index.md` to avoid drift.

## Index format

`roadmap/index.md` contains two sections:

### Dependency graph

A Mermaid `graph TD` block listing every open epic as a node and every inter-dependency as a directed edge. Node IDs match epic slugs; labels are the epic display names. Edges point from dependency to dependent (`A --> B` means B depends on A). Isolated nodes (no dependencies among open epics) appear without edges.

```markdown
## Dependency graph

\`\`\`mermaid
graph TD
    epic-a["Epic A"]
    epic-b["Epic B"]
    epic-a --> epic-b
\`\`\`
```

### Epics list

An ordered list in topological order (dependencies first). Each entry uses this line format:

```markdown
1. `<status>` — [<Epic name>](<slug>.md) — <one-line summary>
```

Status values: `planned` | `in-progress`. Reordering or renumbering the list is how the plan is revised. The list must stay contiguous and in strict topological order.

## Creating a new epic

Use the `roadmap-feature` skill: it gathers context, checks for duplicates, writes the feature file, inserts it at the right position in the Epics list, and adds its node (and edges) to the Mermaid dependency graph.

## Closing a shipped epic

When a feature's acceptance criteria are all verified:

1. Remove the epic's line from the `## Epics` list and renumber.
2. Remove the epic's node and any edges involving it from the Mermaid graph.
3. Delete `roadmap/<slug>.md`.
4. Delete `roadmap/<slug>.plan.md` if it exists.

The `implement-roadmap-feature` Archon workflow handles all four steps automatically in its `close-feature` phase.

Only ask for what is genuinely missing — one question per missing section of the template defined in `docs/ROADMAP.md` (typically: epic name/slug, goal, dependencies, scope + out-of-scope, acceptance criteria, optional notes).

30

If the placeholder "_No epics yet…_" is present, replace it with a proper ordered list. Renumber so the list stays contiguous and in strict topological order. The status of a newly created epic is `planned`.
51

53	
54	## Phase 5: Confirm
55

57	
58	- the new file path,
59	- the chosen position in `index.md` and which dependency it sits after,
60	- a short diff-like summary of what was added.
61	
62	Do NOT commit the changes.
63
