# Plan — New assistant onboarding workflow

This file captures the deep research backing the `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow` epic: the phased process, the LLM skill, the build pipeline, and the validation protocol.

## 1. End-to-end phases

```
[scaffold] → [implement] → [open PR] → [review docs + code]
   → [nightly build attached to PR] → [recruit testers]
   → [tester ✅ × ≥ 2] → [merge] → [officialize]
```

Each phase has explicit entry conditions, deliverables, and gates.

### 1.1 Scaffold

- Trigger: maintainer or contributor invokes the `new-assistant` skill (or runs `scripts/scaffold-vendor.sh` directly).
- Inputs: vendor slug, display name, tint hex, asset SVG source (Octicons / Simple-Icons / vendor-provided).
- Outputs: connector / auth / status / monitor stubs, PDF mark, `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` skeleton, `VendorRegistry` line, test fixtures.
- Exit: `swift build` succeeds with a feature-flagged stub bundle that returns "not implemented" errors.

### 1.2 Implement

The skill walks the contributor through the contract phase by phase: auth cascade → usage fetch → metric mapping → status connector → active-account monitor → branding. Each step references the corresponding section of `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`. The skill explicitly forbids producing `MetricKind.unknown` and reminds the contributor of the strict-ISO-8601 contract on `resetAt`.

API research documentation (`docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`) is filled in **before** the connector is written, not after — so the implementation is a transcription of documented behavior, not a guess.

### 1.3 Open PR

- The PR title follows `feat(<vendor>): support <Display Name> usage tracking`.
- The PR body uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`.
- The contributor adds the `assistant:<vendor>` label (or the workflow auto-labels based on changed paths under `Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Connectors/<Vendor>*` plus `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`).

### 1.4 Review

Two-track review:

- **Code review** — contract compliance, test coverage, Swift quality rules, no `MetricKind.unknown`.
- **Documentation review** — the reviewer reads `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` standalone (without the PR diff) and asks: "Could a third-party developer reproduce this connector from this doc alone?". If no, request more detail in the doc, not just the code comments.

A reviewer checklist (see §3) ensures the second track is not skipped — historically the easiest to skip and the costliest to skip.

### 1.5 Nightly build attached to PR

The release workflow today only fires on `v*.*.*` tags. We add a sibling workflow `new-assistant-build.yml` triggered by:

- `pull_request: types: [labeled, synchronize]` — re-runs whenever the PR carries `assistant:<vendor>` and gets a new commit.
- `workflow_dispatch` — manual rebuild knob for maintainers.

Steps:

1. Checkout the PR head.
2. Reuse the existing `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` (already used by `release.yml`) — the SwiftLint-strip step is identical.
3. Produce both `AI-Usages-Tracker.zip` and an unsigned `.dmg` for casual download (DMG is friendlier to non-technical testers).
4. Compute SHA-256 for both.
5. Post or update a sticky PR comment with:
   - Direct download links to the workflow artifact.
   - Both SHA-256 hashes.
   - Reproducible build instructions: exact commit SHA, Xcode version selection, `./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` invocation. The instructions explicitly state Apple notarization is not performed and explain the right-click → Open Gatekeeper bypass.
   - A link to `docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md` (a new short doc that mirrors the GH Actions step list for human runners).

The sticky comment is updated in place on every push so the PR has a single source of truth for "where is the build". The GitHub Actions concurrency group cancels in-flight runs on new pushes to avoid stacking artifacts.

### 1.6 Tester recruitment

The PR description contains a recruitment block templated by `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`:

> 🧪 **Testers wanted.** This PR adds support for `<Display Name>`. We need ≥ 2 testers with their own `<Display Name>` subscription to validate that the connector reads their real usage correctly. Download the build attached above, run the app, and post your feedback below using the template.

Recruitment channels are off-platform (any community channel the maintainer chooses); the PR comment is the system of record.

### 1.7 Validation — tester sign-off protocol

Testers post a single comment using a fenced template:

```
✅ tester-confirm

Plan: <Free | Pro | Team | Enterprise | Other: …>
macOS: <14.x | 15.x | 26.x>
Build SHA: <first 8 chars from the PR build comment>
Verified:
- [x] Active account is detected correctly
- [x] At least one usage metric matches the vendor's own dashboard within reasonable tolerance
- [x] Reset date displayed in the popover matches the vendor's reported reset
- [ ] Optional: outage banner appears when the vendor reports an incident
Notes: <free-form>
```

Counting rules:

- The PR author's own confirmation does **not** count toward the threshold.
- A confirmation is valid only if the build SHA matches the latest build comment (re-confirmation required after a rebase that changes the build).
- "Tester ✅" is detected by a maintainer eyeballing the comments. A future iteration could add a GitHub Action that scans comments for the `✅ tester-confirm` sentinel and posts a `Tester confirmations: 2/2 ✅` summary, but this is not required for the first version.

Threshold for merge: **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** with valid confirmations on the latest build.

### 1.8 Merge

When all gates are green:

1. Maintainer merges (squash) with the standard commit message convention.
2. Post-merge automation (or a checklist item in the PR template) updates:
   - `README.md` — add the new assistant to the supported list.
   - `docs/vendors/index.md` — add a row to the vendor table.
3. The next tagged release (`v*.*.*`) ships the support; the existing `release.yml` workflow is unchanged.

### 1.9 Officialize

A short announcement note (changelog line in the GitHub release notes auto-generated by `release.yml`) calls out the new vendor with credit to testers (linking their GitHub handles from the validation comments).

## 2. Skill structure (`.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md`)

Following `.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md` — operational artifact, not a spec.

```
---
name: new-assistant
description: Implement support for a new AI assistant against the vendor plugin contract, then open a PR ready for tester validation. Use when the user wants to add a new vendor to AI Usages Tracker.
model: opus
---

# New assistant onboarding

## Prerequisites

Read these specs first; this skill only orchestrates.

- `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` — the technical contract every assistant must satisfy.
- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` — phase-by-phase workflow this skill executes.
- `docs/vendors/_TEMPLATE.md` — the vendor research doc layout.
- The Swift quality docs listed in `CLAUDE.md`.

If this skill and any of those specs disagree, the spec wins; flag it to the user.

## Phase 1 — Gather context
Mine the conversation. Ask only what is missing: vendor slug, display name, tint hex,
asset source (URL or inline SVG), known auth sources (env / keychain / file),
known plan variants (free / paid / enterprise).

## Phase 2 — Duplicate check
List `docs/vendors/`. If the vendor already exists, stop and propose extending it.

## Phase 3 — API research write-up
Before any code: produce `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` from the template. Source every
claim. The user must approve this draft before phase 4.

## Phase 4 — Scaffold
Run `scripts/scaffold-vendor.sh <slug> "<display>" <tintHex>` (or call the equivalent
internal helper). Verify the build still compiles.

## Phase 5 — Implement against the contract
For each plugin point, read the contract section, then implement. After each
implementation step, run the targeted XCTest suite for that connector.

## Phase 6 — Tests
At minimum: auth cascade, payload parsing happy path, every documented plan variant,
HTTP error codes, metric calculation, date normalization. No silent `try?` per
SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.

## Phase 7 — Open the PR
Use `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`. Pre-fill the sections from the
research doc. Add the `assistant:<vendor>` label. Do not request review yet.

## Phase 8 — Self-review checklist
Walk through `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`. Fix every finding before
flipping the PR to ready-for-review.

## Phase 9 — Hand off
Tell the user the PR is ready, list any open questions, and remind them of the
≥ 2 tester ✅ threshold.
```

The skill must reference (not duplicate) the contract and onboarding spec. Templates and grammars stay in `docs/`.

## 3. Reviewer checklist (sketch)

To live in `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md` — referenced by the PR template.

```
## Contract
- [ ] Bundle registered in VendorRegistry; AppDelegate untouched.
- [ ] Auth provider implements VendorAuthProviding with injectable env/keychain/file sources.
- [ ] No MetricKind.unknown in the connector output (contract test passes).
- [ ] resetAt values are strict ISO 8601 datetimes (DEBUG assertion holds).
- [ ] Active-account monitor implements ActiveAccountMonitoring.start/stop.

## Documentation
- [ ] docs/vendors/<vendor>.md is complete: endpoints, auth cascade, plan variants,
      metric semantics, time semantics, error catalog, source references.
- [ ] A reader unfamiliar with the codebase could reproduce the connector from
      this doc alone.

## Tests
- [ ] Auth cascade covered (env / keychain / file each individually + cascade).
- [ ] Each documented plan variant has a representative response fixture.
- [ ] HTTP error codes seen in the wild are tested (4xx / 5xx).
- [ ] Date normalization tested (calendar date → UTC midnight if applicable).

## Build & validation
- [ ] Nightly build artifact present in the sticky PR comment, SHA-256 listed.
- [ ] At least 2 non-author tester confirmations on the latest build SHA.
- [ ] Reproducible-build instructions verified by the reviewer (optional spot check).

## Branding
- [ ] PDF mark renders cleanly in light + dark menu bars.
- [ ] tintHex matches vendor brand reasonably; readable on both bars.

## Post-merge follow-ups
- [ ] README "Supported assistants" updated in the same PR.
```

## 4. Build pipeline detail

### 4.1 Workflow file `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`

```yaml
name: New-assistant nightly build

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [labeled, synchronize, opened, reopened]
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: assistant-build-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  build:
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'assistant')  # any assistant:* label
    runs-on: macos-15
    timeout-minutes: 30
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      # Reuse the steps from release.yml: select Xcode 26, strip SwiftLint plugin,
      # run ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh, produce ZIP + DMG, compute SHA-256.
      # Then upload as workflow artifact AND post a sticky PR comment with links
      # and reproducible-build instructions.
```

The matching label uses `assistant` (any `assistant:*` label triggers it). DMG packaging is added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` or wrapped by a sibling script.

### 4.2 Sticky comment shape

Maintained by an action that creates or edits a comment marked with an HTML sentinel:

```html
<!-- new-assistant-build:sticky -->
🛠 Build for commit <SHA>

- [Download ZIP](…) — SHA-256 `…`
- [Download DMG](…) — SHA-256 `…`

Reproducible build:

```sh
git checkout <SHA>
./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
# Verify: shasum -a 256 dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip
```

Builds are ad-hoc signed only. First launch: right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper.
```

### 4.3 Reproducible-build doc

`docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md` — short, stable: prerequisites (Xcode 26+, macOS 15+, the SwiftLint-plugin caveat for CI), the exact command sequence, and the expected SHA-256 verification flow.

## 5. Risks and open questions

- **Tester recruitment latency.** Some vendors have small audiences; getting two confirmations may take days/weeks. The workflow does not pressure-merge — PRs simply wait. The README should call this out so contributors know the timeline up front.
- **Plan-variant coverage gap.** A tester on Free plan does not validate Paid behavior. The threshold of ≥ 2 testers is a floor, not a ceiling — the reviewer checklist asks the maintainer to weigh whether all documented plan variants are covered before merging.
- **Sticky-comment race conditions.** Two pushes in quick succession may produce two comments. Mitigated by `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true` and by the sentinel-driven find-or-create pattern.
- **DMG vs ZIP tradeoff.** DMG is more familiar to casual users but slightly larger. We ship both — testers download whichever they prefer.
- **PR template visibility.** GitHub picks the template based on the URL parameter; without the parameter, contributors get the default template. The skill must either set `&template=new-assistant.md` in the URL it suggests or explicitly instruct the contributor to use it.
- **Self-approval.** The author's own subscription is the most convenient way to validate — but it does not count toward the threshold. The PR template explicitly states this.
- **Skipped reviews.** Documentation review is the easiest to skip. Mitigation: the reviewer checklist is mandatory and the PR template includes a `Reviewer signed off on docs/vendors/<vendor>.md ☐` checkbox.

## 6. Deliverables checklist

- [ ] `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
- [ ] `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`
- [ ] `docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md`
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`
- [ ] `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`
- [ ] DMG packaging step added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` (or sibling script)
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` lazy-loaded context update (mention `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`)
- [ ] README "Supported assistants" section restructured to be append-friendly

content.text:
J'ai créé deux épics roadmap, chacun avec son fichier épic + son fichier plan détaillé, et mis à jour `roadmap/index.md`.

## Fichiers créés

**Épic 1 — `roadmap/vendor-plugin-framework.md`** + `vendor-plugin-framework.plan.md`
- Définit le contrat plugin pour ajouter un nouvel assistant : `VendorBundle`, `UsageConnector`, `StatusConnector`, `ActiveAccountMonitoring`, `VendorAuthProviding`, `VendorBranding`, `VendorRegistry`.
- Le plan contient : inventaire de l'existant (Claude / Codex / Copilot avec divergences identifiées — auth couplé chez Claude, sémantique des comptes, génération des metrics, registration des loggers), proposition de contrat avec types Swift précis, plan de migration en 8 étapes (refactor Claude en premier car le plus divergent), test de conformité de contrat, scaffolding script vs skill, risques et open questions.
- Exigence-clé : un `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` par assistant qui formalise la recherche d'API (endpoints, plans observés, sémantique des metrics, dates ISO vs calendaires, error catalog, références sources).

**Épic 2 — `roadmap/new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.md`** + `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` (dépend de l'épic 1)
- Formalise les 9 phases : scaffold → implement → open PR → review (code + doc) → nightly build attaché à la PR → tester recruitment → ✅ × ≥ 2 → merge → officialize.
- Le plan détaille : structure du skill `.claude/skills/new-assistant/` (en suivant `skill-authoring.md` : opérationnel, référence les specs), workflow GitHub Actions `new-assistant-build.yml` (déclenché par label `assistant:<vendor>`, sticky comment avec ZIP + DMG + SHA-256 + instructions de rebuild reproductible), protocole de sign-off testeurs (template `✅ tester-confirm` avec plan / build SHA / checklist, l'auteur ne compte pas dans le seuil), checklist reviewer (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`), risques (latence recrutement, plan-variant coverage, race conditions sticky comment).

**`roadmap/index.md`** mis à jour avec le graphe Mermaid de dépendance et la liste topologique des épics (et au passage la coquille `1\`planned\`` → `1. \`planned\`` corrigée pour la nouvelle entrée).

## Points à valider avant implémentation

- Le seuil **≥ 2 testeurs non-auteurs** est-il bon, ou tu préfères 3 ?
- **DMG + ZIP** ou seulement ZIP comme aujourd'hui ? (le plan suggère les deux pour faciliter les testeurs non-techniques mais c'est discutable)
- Préférence entre **scaffolding shell script** (`scripts/scaffold-vendor.sh`) vs uniquement la skill ? J'ai recommandé les deux (script d'abord, skill l'orchestre) mais un seul peut suffire.
- Le contrat propose un `VendorAuthProviding` générique avec `associatedtype Credentials` — accepter cette généricité, ou imposer un type credential commun ?

quelques remarques

# epic 1

OK avec tout ce que tu dis
Dans le <vendor>.md il est absolument crucial de mettre l'accent sur la documentation de comment les choses se passent à une date donnée (il faut dater ce fichier) car ça sera un point très important pour comparer, quelques mois plus tard, si on détecte un changement dans l'API, comment les choses ont pu évoluer (et quels sont donc les impacts)

# epic 2

il faut mettre l'accent sur la temporalité sur ce skill. Car toutes les étapes vont se dérouler dans une fenêtre de temps plus ou moins longue (plusieurs jours)
=> ce n'est pas dans un seul prompt que tout ça va se jouer

Il faut peut-être prévoir plusieurs skills (ou un meta skill qui se repose sur plusieurs "sub skills") pour pouvoir invoquer le bon skill lorsqu'un évènement survient dans le process.

# tes questions

- 2 testeurs mini
- DMG uniquement. Le zip ne sert à rien, il faut plutôt pointer sur une doc qui permette de cloner (ou télécharger) les sources d'une branche donnée + les builder en local + les run
- je vois pas l'intéret du script sh de scaffold ... pour moi faut faire un skill pour aider à implémenter (et à dérouler toutes les autres étapes de l'epic 2)
- je n'ai pas tous les tenants et aboutissant par rapport au VendorAuthProviding. Explicite. Ce qui est important, c'est que ce soit compatible avec les 3 impléms d'assistants actuellement supportés (claude code, codex et copilot) et je pense que ce n'est pas à l'app de gérer les credentials (et leur securisation). Le job de l'app, c'est uniquement de fournir des connecteurs pour récupérer les credentials là où ils se trouvent.

- DMG uniquement. Le zip ne sert à rien, il faut plutôt pointer sur une doc qui permette de cloner (ou télécharger) les sources d'une branche donnée + les builder en local + les run
- je vois pas l'intéret du script sh de scaffold ... pour moi faut faire un skill pour aider à implémenter (et à dérouler toutes les autres étapes de l'epic 2)
- je n'ai pas tous les tenants et aboutissant par rapport au VendorAuthProviding. Explicite. Ce qui est important, c'est que ce soit compatible avec les 3 impléms d'assistants actuellement supportés (claude code, codex et copilot) et je pense que ce n'est pas à l'app de gérer les credentials (et leur securisation). Le job de l'app, c'est uniquement de fournir des connecteurs pour récupérer les credentials là où ils se trouvent.

content.thinking:
The user is pointing out that I need to strengthen a couple of key aspects of these epics. For the vendor plugin framework, I should emphasize that the `<vendor>.md` document needs to be dated and function as a snapshot—this matters because API changes require historical reference points to track how things have diverged over time. For the onboarding workflow, I need to make clear that this is a multi-day process, not something that happens instantaneously.

Looking at the feedback on implementation details: the distribution should be DMG only, with the rebuild path documented as a checkout-and-build process rather than a shell script. The sticky comment just links to the DMG and the build instructions. I also need to reconsider the `VendorAuthProviding` abstraction entirely—the user's point is that the app doesn't manage credentials at all, it just locates them in their existing homes (environment variables, keychains, config files). Looking at how the three existing assistants handle this, they all follow a pattern of reading from external sources in some cascade order. So the real abstraction here isn't an "auth provider" but a "credentials locator" that resolves from wherever the vendor's own tooling already stores them.

Now I'm planning the concrete updates across the four files: adding mandatory dating requirements to the vendor documentation (with a `Last verified` header and dated payloads), renaming and reframing the auth protocol to reflect this read-only, source-aware nature, dropping the ZIP distribution and scaffold script entirely, and restructuring the onboarding workflow around a meta-skill that delegates to sub-skills mapped to specific phases and events. I'm leaning toward the sub-skills approach since it aligns better with how other skills in this repo are structured—each one handles a single responsibility. I'll create separate skills for implementation, review, tester follow-up, and officialization, tied together by a meta-doc that walks through the whole workflow. The build pipeline stays as a GitHub Action triggered by labels, and the implementation skill will remind users to add that label. For the DMG, I'm dropping ZIP entirely and making the reproducible build path (clone, build locally) the trust anchor, documented in a new reproducible builds guide that the sticky comment will reference. The scaffolding happens directly in the implementation skill using the edit tools rather than a separate bash script.

Now I'm going through the edits systematically. For the epic 1 plan, I need to reframe the VendorAuthProviding section around credential locators, rename and reframe section 2.3, add dating requirements to 2.6, remove the shell scaffolding mention, and update the risks. The epic 1 file gets tweaks to the scope and notes. Epic 2 needs the scaffold script removed, temporal and multi-skill emphasis added, and DMG-only callouts. In the epic 2 plan, I'm restructuring the phases to highlight trigger events, replacing the single-skill section with a meta-skill plus sub-skills mapped to those events, updating the build pipeline section for DMG-only with no ZIP and revised sticky comments, and adding or merging the reproducible builds documentation. Let me start making these changes now.

# Vendor plugin framework

## Goal

Turn the codebase into a plugin-ready foundation so adding a new AI assistant follows a single, well-defined contract — auth retrieval, usage fetching, payload normalization to the persisted JSON shape, vendor branding, status fetching, and active-account monitoring — instead of being copy-pasted off the previous connector and drifting.

## Dependencies

None.

## Scope

- A `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` spec describing every required plugin point (protocols, value objects, wiring) with cross-references to the existing Swift quality docs.
- A `VendorBundle` (or equivalent) value type that ties together a vendor's connector, status connector, active-account monitor, branding, and metadata so `AppDelegate` composes assistants from a registry rather than naming each one explicitly.
- A standardized API research document per assistant under `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` covering: endpoints called, plan variants observed (free / paid / enterprise), payload shapes, time-window vs pay-as-you-go vs per-request semantics, reset cadences, error codes, and source references (community write-ups, official docs).
- Refactor of the three existing connectors (Claude Code, Codex, Copilot CLI) onto the new contract without behavioral changes or test-coverage loss.
- A scaffolding helper (script or skill input) that emits the boilerplate skeleton for a new assistant: connector actor, auth provider, status connector, active-account monitor, branding entry, vendor mark asset stub, test fixtures.

**Out of scope**

- Runtime dynamic plugin loading (dlopen, external dylibs). The framework stays compile-time.
- Breaking the public JSON schema of `usages.json` or `usage-history/*.jsonl` — the contract must reuse the existing `UsageMetric` discriminator.
- Implementing any new vendor as part of this epic — it is purely a refactor + documentation effort.
- Generalizing storage, logging, or scheduler beyond what is needed to express the contract.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` exists, lists every plugin point, and is referenced from `CLAUDE.md`'s lazy-loaded context section.
- All existing assistants are exposed through the registry; `AppDelegate` no longer references concrete connector or monitor types one by one.
- Each existing assistant has a corresponding `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` populated.
- A new assistant can be onboarded by adding files under predictable paths (connector, monitor, vendor doc, branding asset) plus one registry line — no edits to `AppDelegate`, `Loggers`, or other shared subsystems beyond their declared plugin points.
- Test coverage for existing connectors is unchanged or higher after the refactor.

## Notes

- See `vendor-plugin-framework.plan.md` for the deep research (current-state inventory, proposed contract, migration plan, open questions).
- The contract must compose with `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md`, `docs/SWIFT-CONCURRENCY.md`, `docs/SWIFT-TESTABILITY.md`, `docs/SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.md`, and `docs/SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS.md` — none of those rules are relaxed for plugin code.
- The `Vendor` value object is the natural primary key; it stays string-backed for forward compatibility (unknown vendors decode without throwing).

- The credential-locator abstraction never stores, encrypts, or persists tokens — it only reads them from external sources owned by the vendor's own CLI (env var, Keychain entry written by the vendor's CLI, or on-disk config file written by the vendor's CLI).
- Test coverage for existing connectors is unchanged or higher after the refactor.

## Notes

- See `vendor-plugin-framework.plan.md` for the deep research (current-state inventory, proposed contract, migration plan, open questions).
- The contract must compose with `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md`, `docs/SWIFT-CONCURRENCY.md`, `docs/SWIFT-TESTABILITY.md`, `docs/SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.md`, and `docs/SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS.md` — none of those rules are relaxed for plugin code.
- The `Vendor` value object is the natural primary key; it stays string-backed for forward compatibility (unknown vendors decode without throwing).

input.old_string:
### 2.3 Auth abstraction

Generalize the current per-vendor `*AuthProviding` protocols behind:

```swift
public protocol VendorAuthProviding: Sendable {
    associatedtype Credentials: Sendable
    func load() async throws -> Credentials
}
```

Connectors continue to consume their own concrete credentials type. The associated type avoids forcing all vendors into a common `Credentials` shape (Claude has access tokens, Codex has API keys + org IDs, Copilot has GitHub tokens with provenance metadata). The contract additionally requires that:

- Auth resolution must support a cascade of injectable sources (env vars, Keychain, on-disk config). Direct calls to `SecKeychain*` or `ProcessInfo` outside the auth provider are a contract violation.
- Errors must be enum cases with associated values, per `docs/SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.md`.
input.new_string:
### 2.3 Credential locator (not "auth provider")

The naming "auth provider" is misleading because the app never owns or manages credentials. The app's job is purely to **locate** credentials in storage that the vendor's own CLI already owns and secures (`claude` writes its keychain entry, `codex` writes `~/.config/codex/`, `gh` writes `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml` and the system keychain). The plugin contract reflects this read-only stance:

```swift
public protocol CredentialLocator: Sendable {
    associatedtype Credentials: Sendable
    /// Reads credentials from external sources owned by the vendor's CLI.
    /// The locator MUST NOT write, refresh, rotate, or persist tokens — those
    /// operations belong to the vendor's own tooling.
    func locate() async throws -> Credentials
}
```

Why an associated type instead of a concrete `Credentials` shape:

- Claude Code: OAuth access token from the macOS Keychain.
- Codex: API key + org id from `~/.config/codex/auth.json` or env.
- Copilot CLI: GitHub OAuth token with three-source cascade (`$GITHUB_TOKEN` → `gh` keychain entry decoded from `go-keyring-base64:` → `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml`), plus `tokenSource` provenance for diagnostic logs.

Forcing those into a common struct would either lose information or balloon into a lowest-common-denominator bag of optionals. The associated type keeps each connector's domain credential shape, while the contract ensures uniform invocation and testability.

The contract additionally requires:

- Sources must be injectable: `environment: [String: String]`, `fileManager: FileManager`, `processRunner: ProcessRunning`, `keychainAccessor: KeychainReading` — never direct calls to `ProcessInfo.processInfo` or `SecKeychain*` from the locator implementation.
- Locators MUST NOT call `SecItemAdd`, `SecItemUpdate`, `SecItemDelete`, write to vendor config files, or shell out to `<vendor> auth login` — read paths only. A SwiftLint custom rule should flag these symbols inside any `*CredentialLocator.swift` file.
- Errors are enum cases with associated values per `docs/SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.md`, and they distinguish "not logged in" (vendor's CLI never wrote credentials) from "found but unreadable" (file exists but malformed) — the first is a normal user state, the second is a bug or a vendor schema change worth surfacing.

This contract is already compatible with all three existing assistants:

| Vendor | Today's type | Migration |
|---|---|---|
| Claude Code | inline keychain reads inside `ClaudeCodeConnector` | Extract `ClaudeCredentialLocator` actor; connector consumes it. |
| Codex | `CodexAuthProviding` actor | Rename to `CodexCredentialLocator`; signature already matches. |
| Copilot CLI | `CopilotAuthProviding` actor | Rename to `CopilotCredentialLocator`; signature already matches. |

input.old_string:
### 2.6 Required vendor documentation

`docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` is a mandatory sibling deliverable. It captures the API research that today only survives in a single PR commit message. Required sections:

- **Endpoints.** Full URL(s), HTTP method, headers (auth, user-agent, plan-specific), timeout we use.
- **Auth sources.** Cascade order (env var → keychain → file), exact paths and keys, fallback ordering rationale.
- **Plan variants observed.** Free / Pro / Team / Enterprise — each with a representative sanitized payload and the fields the connector reads.
- **Metric semantics.** For each `UsageMetric` the connector emits, mapping from raw payload fields to the Swift case (timeWindow / payAsYouGo). Includes reset cadence (rolling window vs calendar boundary), unit (requests, dollars, percent remaining vs consumed), and edge cases (free-tier overage, "unlimited" flags, monthly vs weekly billing).
- **Time semantics.** Whether the API returns ISO 8601 datetimes or calendar dates — and whether the connector promotes calendar dates to UTC midnight.
- **Error catalog.** HTTP status codes seen in the wild and the connector's response (degrade, retry, surface).
- **Source references.** Community write-ups, official docs, GitHub gists used as evidence — links + retrieval date in case they rot.

A skeleton template at `docs/vendors/_TEMPLATE.md` enforces the section list.
input.new_string:
### 2.6 Required vendor documentation — a dated snapshot

`docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` is a mandatory sibling deliverable. It captures the API research that today only survives in a single PR commit message. **Critically, this doc is treated as a dated snapshot of how the vendor's API behaves at a given moment, not as a forever-true reference.** Vendor APIs drift silently — endpoints change shape, "unlimited" flags get re-purposed, calendar dates become full ISO datetimes. When that drift breaks the connector months later, the historical snapshot is the only way to compare what the API used to look like against what it does now, and to scope the impact of the change. Without it, every drift becomes archeology.

#### Dating discipline

- **Top-of-file header**, immediately under the H1, machine-parseable:
  ```markdown
  > **Last verified:** 2026-05-06 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan
  ```
  Updated whenever the doc is re-validated against the live API. The reviewer checklist for any PR touching the connector requires bumping this date; a stale doc is a contract violation.

- **Per-section verification dates.** Each section that captures a payload, plan variant, or behavior carries its own `_verified: YYYY-MM-DD_` line. This matters because some sections age faster than others — endpoints may stay stable for years while plan-variant payloads change every quarter.

- **Sample payloads are dated and tagged with their plan.** Every captured response includes a fenced block prefixed with `<!-- captured: YYYY-MM-DD, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->`. Sensitive fields are redacted but the structure, types, and field presence are preserved. Multiple snapshots over time can coexist — old ones get a `(superseded by YYYY-MM-DD)` annotation rather than being deleted, so the drift trail is visible.

- **External sources include retrieval dates.** Community gists, blog posts, GitHub issues — every link is followed by `(retrieved YYYY-MM-DD)`. URLs rot and content gets edited; the retrieval date pins what we actually read.

#### Required sections

- **Endpoints.** Full URL(s), HTTP method, headers (auth, user-agent, plan-specific), timeout we use, response content-type. Each entry dated.
- **Credential sources.** Cascade order (env var → keychain → file), exact paths and keys, fallback ordering rationale. Names of the vendor CLIs that own each source. Dated because vendor CLIs rename their files.
- **Plan variants observed.** Free / Pro / Team / Enterprise — each with a representative dated sanitized payload and the fields the connector reads. Explicit note when a variant is **assumed** but not yet **verified by a tester** — that distinction is the cornerstone of the onboarding workflow's tester gate.
- **Metric semantics.** For each `UsageMetric` the connector emits, mapping from raw payload fields to the Swift case (timeWindow / payAsYouGo). Includes reset cadence (rolling window vs calendar boundary), unit (requests, dollars, percent remaining vs consumed), and edge cases (free-tier overage, "unlimited" flags, monthly vs weekly billing). Each mapping dated.
- **Time semantics.** Whether the API returns ISO 8601 datetimes or calendar dates — and whether the connector promotes calendar dates to UTC midnight. This is exactly the kind of detail that flipped silently between Copilot's early days and today; the doc must capture which form was observed on which date.
- **Error catalog.** HTTP status codes seen in the wild and the connector's response (degrade, retry, surface). Dated.
- **Known unknowns.** Explicit list of behaviors the contributor could not verify directly (e.g., enterprise plans without a tester to confirm). The onboarding workflow's tester gate fills these in over time.
- **Source references.** Community write-ups, official docs, GitHub gists used as evidence — links + retrieval date.
- **Change log.** Append-only list of `YYYY-MM-DD — what changed in the API or in our understanding of it`. The first entry is "initial capture". Subsequent entries fill in as drift is discovered. This is the section a future contributor reads first when debugging an "it used to work" report.

A skeleton template at `docs/vendors/_TEMPLATE.md` enforces the section list and the dating placeholders.

#### Future re-verification

Re-dating the file is part of normal PR hygiene whenever the connector is touched, but the framework should also support a deliberate "I just re-verified the live API against the doc" pass — useful when the user reports a drift. A future skill (`new-assistant-reverify`, see the onboarding workflow plan) automates this, but the data shape lives in the spec defined here.

+Re-dating the file is part of normal PR hygiene whenever the connector is touched, but the framework should also support a deliberate "I just re-verified the live API against the doc" pass — useful when the user reports a drift. A future skill (`new-assistant-reverify`, see the onboarding workflow plan) automates this, but the data shape lives in the spec defined here.
## 3. Migration plan
The refactor must not regress behavior or tests. Suggested ordering:

lines: ### Divergences worth noting

-1. **Auth coupling.** Claude Code's auth lives inside `ClaudeCodeConnector`; Codex and Copilot extracted dedicated `*Auth` providers. The contract should mandate a separate provider so it can be mocked uniformly in tests.

+1. **Credential coupling.** Claude Code's credential lookup lives inside `ClaudeCodeConnector`; Codex and Copilot extracted dedicated locator actors. The contract mandates a separate locator so it can be mocked uniformly in tests, and so the read-only stance (no writes, no rotations) can be enforced at a single layer.

2. **Active-account semantics.** Claude monitors a single keychain-derived email, Codex monitors a config file emitting `AccountEmail`, Copilot monitors `gh hosts.yml` and emits a GitHub login wrapped in `AccountEmail`. The contract has to admit any local source — file, process, keychain — under one protocol.

3. **Branding switch.** `VendorBranding.brand(for:)` returns `nil` for unknown vendors, which the UI handles via fallback. A registry replaces the switch and removes the implicit "is this vendor known?" check from each call site.

4. **Logging registration.** Adding a new logger today means editing `Loggers` and `Logger.swift`. The contract should let a `VendorBundle` carry its own `FileLogger` factory so adding a vendor does not need cross-cutting edits.

## 4. Scaffolding helper

The scaffolding can be done two ways — they are not mutually exclusive:

- **Shell script** under `scripts/scaffold-vendor.sh <vendor-slug>` that copies a `templates/vendor/` tree, performs token replacement (`__VENDOR__`, `__Vendor__`, `__VENDOR_DISPLAY__`), and prints the next-step checklist. Cheap, deterministic, no LLM.
- **Skill** (`.claude/skills/new-assistant/`) that drives the entire onboarding workflow including scaffolding — covered by the `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow` epic.

Recommended sequence: ship the shell script first as part of this epic so the contract has a working artifact even before the workflow epic; the skill then orchestrates the script.
input.new_string:
## 4. Scaffolding

Scaffolding is **skill-only** — there is no shell script. The implementation skill defined in the [new-assistant-onboarding-workflow](new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.md) epic creates every required file directly via `Write` / `Edit` tools, using the contract spec as its template source. Reasons:

- A shell script duplicates the contract structure in a second place that drifts from `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`.
- The skill already needs to read the contract to perform code review later in the workflow; reusing it for scaffolding keeps a single source of truth.
- Token-replacement-based scaffolding produces dead-looking boilerplate that contributors then have to humanize anyway. Skill-driven scaffolding can write the right code from the start, informed by the live conversation context (which auth sources the vendor exposes, etc.).

This epic only **specifies the shape** the skill must emit (file layout, registry registration, branding entry). The mechanics of "how the skill drives it across multiple sessions" are owned by the workflow epic.

input.old_string:
## 5. Risks and open questions

- **Contract churn.** Locking in the contract now means future vendors that need exotic auth (OAuth device flow, refresh tokens, multi-tenant cookies) might require contract evolution. Mitigation: keep `VendorAuthProviding` generic via an associated type so each vendor can carry its own credential shape.
- **Active-account "absent" semantics.** Some vendors (e.g., Copilot) have a single global login; others (Claude) can have many accounts. The contract treats account discovery as a connector concern (`fetchUsages` returns `[VendorUsageEntry]`) and active-account-tracking as the optional monitor's job. Make sure the spec calls this out so a reader doesn't assume one-active-per-vendor.
- **Logger registration.** The per-vendor `FileLogger` opens a separate log file. Reading log retention behavior from `LogCleaner` must continue to discover bundle-owned loggers automatically — the registry is the natural source.
- **`MetricKind.unknown` slipping in.** A connector that produces `.unknown` accidentally (because its API changed) would silently round-trip. The contract conformance test catches this on CI.
- **Docs/VENDORS as living context.** API shapes drift. The maintenance burden is on each PR that touches a connector — the PR template (workflow epic) enforces "if you touch the connector, you touch the vendor doc".
input.new_string:
## 5. Risks and open questions

- **Contract churn.** Locking in the contract now means future vendors that need exotic credential storage (OAuth device flow, multi-tenant cookies) might require contract evolution. Mitigation: keep `CredentialLocator` generic via an associated `Credentials` type so each vendor carries its own shape; the read-only invariant remains regardless of the credential type.
- **Read-only invariant enforcement.** Nothing in the type system prevents a future locator from calling `SecItemAdd` or writing to a vendor's config file. Mitigation: a SwiftLint custom rule flags those symbols inside `*CredentialLocator.swift` files, and a contract conformance test inspects the file imports.
- **Active-account "absent" semantics.** Some vendors (e.g., Copilot) have a single global login; others (Claude) can have many accounts. The contract treats account discovery as a connector concern (`fetchUsages` returns `[VendorUsageEntry]`) and active-account-tracking as the optional monitor's job. Make sure the spec calls this out so a reader doesn't assume one-active-per-vendor.
- **Logger registration.** The per-vendor `FileLogger` opens a separate log file. Reading log retention behavior from `LogCleaner` must continue to discover bundle-owned loggers automatically — the registry is the natural source.
- **`MetricKind.unknown` slipping in.** A connector that produces `.unknown` accidentally (because its API changed) would silently round-trip. The contract conformance test catches this on CI.
- **Vendor doc drift.** API shapes drift silently and the doc is the only artifact that captures the historical baseline. The maintenance burden is on each PR that touches a connector — the PR template (workflow epic) enforces "if you touch the connector, you bump `Last verified` and append a Change log entry". A dated re-verification skill complements PR-time discipline for opportunistic spot checks.

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

# New assistant onboarding workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: scaffolding, implementation, PR opening, review, nightly build, tester recruitment, validation, merge, post-merge announcement.
- A `.claude/skills/new-assistant/` skill that walks an LLM through implementing a new assistant against the plugin contract — including duplicate checks, scaffolding invocation, API research write-up, and PR description generation.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` that enforces required deliverables: API research doc, branding asset, contract conformance, tester sign-off section.
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds a signed (ad-hoc) DMG/ZIP for any PR carrying an `assistant:<vendor>` label, attaches it to the PR as a build comment, and prints reproducible-build instructions so a skeptical reader can rebuild from source.
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md` or co-located in the spec) covering documentation clarity, contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: how testers post their results (GitHub PR comment with a magic sentinel), the threshold (≥ 2 distinct testers ✅), the rules for self-approval (the author's own subscription does not count toward the threshold).

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; rebuild instructions are the trust path.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens in the issue tracker and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- A `.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md` exists, points at both `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` and `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`, and includes the "operational artifact references the spec" pattern from `.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md`.
- Opening a PR labelled `assistant:<vendor>` triggers a workflow that builds, packages, and attaches a downloadable artifact (or a build comment with a download link) to the PR within the workflow's normal latency.
- Every `assistant:<vendor>` PR uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and includes a "Tester sign-off" section pre-filled with the threshold rule.
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist that covers contract compliance, doc clarity, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated automatically (or by checklist requirement) when a new assistant is merged.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: phase breakdown, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, skill structure, risks.
- The skill orchestrates but does not bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate.

+This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. ## Scope

- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: implementation, PR opening, review, nightly build, tester recruitment, validation, merge, post-merge announcement, and later re-verification of the vendor doc against API drift. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill and PR template references it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one trigger event in the workflow. A short meta-skill helps the user pick the right one when they're not sure where they are in the process. See the plan for the proposed split.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` that enforces required deliverables: dated API research doc, branding asset, contract conformance, tester sign-off section.
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR carrying an `assistant:<vendor>` label, attaches it to the PR as a sticky build comment, and points readers at `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` for the trust path (clone the branch, build locally, run). No ZIP — the trust escape hatch is "build from source yourself", not "download a different archive format".
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: how testers post their results (GitHub PR comment with a magic sentinel), the threshold (**≥ 2 distinct testers** ✅), the rules for self-approval (the author's own subscription does not count toward the threshold).

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: scaffolding, implementation, PR opening, review, nightly build, tester recruitment, validation, merge, post-merge announcement.
- A `.claude/skills/new-assistant/` skill that walks an LLM through implementing a new assistant against the plugin contract — including duplicate checks, scaffolding invocation, API research write-up, and PR description generation.

**Out of scope**

- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.

# New assistant onboarding workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: implementation, PR opening, review, nightly build, tester recruitment, validation, merge, post-merge announcement, and later re-verification of the vendor doc against API drift. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill and PR template references it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one trigger event in the workflow. A short meta-skill helps the user pick the right one when they're not sure where they are in the process. See the plan for the proposed split.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` that enforces required deliverables: dated API research doc, branding asset, contract conformance, tester sign-off section.
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR carrying an `assistant:<vendor>` label, attaches it to the PR as a sticky build comment, and points readers at `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` for the trust path (clone the branch, build locally, run). No ZIP — the trust escape hatch is "build from source yourself", not "download a different archive format".
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: how testers post their results (GitHub PR comment with a magic sentinel), the threshold (**≥ 2 distinct testers** ✅), the rules for self-approval (the author's own subscription does not count toward the threshold).

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens in the issue tracker and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- A `.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md` exists, points at both `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` and `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`, and includes the "operational artifact references the spec" pattern from `.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md`.
- Opening a PR labelled `assistant:<vendor>` triggers a workflow that builds, packages, and attaches a downloadable artifact (or a build comment with a download link) to the PR within the workflow's normal latency.
- Every `assistant:<vendor>` PR uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and includes a "Tester sign-off" section pre-filled with the threshold rule.
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist that covers contract compliance, doc clarity, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated automatically (or by checklist requirement) when a new assistant is merged.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: phase breakdown, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, skill structure, risks.
- The skill orchestrates but does not bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate.

input.new_string:
# Plan — New assistant onboarding workflow

This file captures the deep research backing the `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow` epic: the multi-day phased process, the per-event skill split, the build pipeline, and the validation protocol.

## 0. Why temporality is the central constraint

Adding a new assistant is not a single coding session. The phases below are separated by hours or days — sometimes weeks while waiting for testers. A monolithic "do everything" skill would either lose its place between sessions, or pretend it has state it doesn't have. The design therefore commits to two principles:

1. **Each phase is owned by exactly one skill.** A skill is invoked when a specific event happens (a PR is opened, a tester comments, the threshold is reached). The skill rebuilds context from the PR + repo state on each invocation.
2. **No skill assumes prior session state.** Every skill starts by reading `gh pr view`, the relevant files, and the spec, then decides what to do. There is no scratchpad passed between phases.

`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` is the human-readable map of which event triggers which skill. The skills themselves only describe their own phase.

## 1. End-to-end phases (with triggers and skills)

```
[implement: scaffold + code + docs + tests]
        ↓ (author finishes locally)
[open PR with assistant:<vendor> label]
        ↓ (GH Actions reacts to the label)
[CI builds DMG, posts sticky build comment]
        ↓ (maintainer reviews when convenient — could be hours or days later)
[review docs + code]
        ↓ (testers download the DMG, run it, comment back — wall-clock days)
[recruit + collect tester comments]
        ↓ (≥ 2 non-author tester ✅ on the latest build SHA)
[merge]
        ↓ (next tagged release, plus README + docs/vendors/index updates)
[officialize]
        ↓ (later, on demand or when API drift is suspected)
[re-verify vendor doc against live API]
```

Each phase has an explicit trigger event, a single owning skill (or no skill, when CI handles it), explicit inputs (always re-read from the repo / PR), and a clear exit condition.

### 1.1 Implement

- **Trigger**: contributor decides to add support for vendor X.
- **Skill**: `new-assistant-implement` (see §2.1).
- **Inputs**: vendor slug, display name, tint hex, asset SVG source (Octicons / Simple-Icons / vendor-provided), known credential sources, known plan variants. Skill mines the conversation first; asks only what's missing.
- **Outputs**: connector / credential locator / status / monitor implementations, PDF mark, dated `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` populated with everything the contributor knows up front, `VendorRegistry` line, tests passing.
- **Exit**: `swift build` and `swift test` both succeed; the connector returns real metrics for the contributor's own subscription. Scaffolding happens inline — no separate shell script.

+- **Exit**: `swift build` and `swift test` both succeed; the connector returns real metrics for the contributor's own subscription. Scaffolding happens inline — no separate shell script.

### 1.2 Implement

The skill walks the contributor through the contract phase by phase: auth cascade → usage fetch → metric mapping → status connector → active-account monitor → branding. Each step references the corresponding section of `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`. The skill explicitly forbids producing `MetricKind.unknown` and reminds the contributor of the strict-ISO-8601 contract on `resetAt`.

API research documentation (`docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`) is filled in **before** the connector is written, not after — so the implementation is a transcription of documented behavior, not a guess.

### 1.3 Open PR

- The PR title follows `feat(<vendor>): support <Display Name> usage tracking`.
- The PR body uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`.
- The contributor adds the `assistant:<vendor>` label (or the workflow auto-labels based on changed paths under `Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Connectors/<Vendor>*` plus `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`).

### 1.4 Review

Two-track review:

- **Code review** — contract compliance, test coverage, Swift quality rules, no `MetricKind.unknown`.
- **Documentation review** — the reviewer reads `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` standalone (without the PR diff) and asks: "Could a third-party developer reproduce this connector from this doc alone?". If no, request more detail in the doc, not just the code comments.

A reviewer checklist (see §3) ensures the second track is not skipped — historically the easiest to skip and the costliest to skip.

### 1.5 Nightly build attached to PR

The release workflow today only fires on `v*.*.*` tags. We add a sibling workflow `new-assistant-build.yml` triggered by:

- `pull_request: types: [labeled, synchronize]` — re-runs whenever the PR carries `assistant:<vendor>` and gets a new commit.
- `workflow_dispatch` — manual rebuild knob for maintainers.

Steps:

1. Checkout the PR head.
2. Reuse the existing `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` (already used by `release.yml`) — the SwiftLint-strip step is identical.
3. Produce both `AI-Usages-Tracker.zip` and an unsigned `.dmg` for casual download (DMG is friendlier to non-technical testers).
4. Compute SHA-256 for both.
5. Post or update a sticky PR comment with:
   - Direct download links to the workflow artifact.
   - Both SHA-256 hashes.
   - Reproducible build instructions: exact commit SHA, Xcode version selection, `./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` invocation. The instructions explicitly state Apple notarization is not performed and explain the right-click → Open Gatekeeper bypass.
   - A link to `docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md` (a new short doc that mirrors the GH Actions step list for human runners).

The sticky comment is updated in place on every push so the PR has a single source of truth for "where is the build". The GitHub Actions concurrency group cancels in-flight runs on new pushes to avoid stacking artifacts.

### 1.6 Tester recruitment

- **Inputs**: the local branch state.
- **Outputs**: PR opened with title `feat(<vendor>): support <Display Name> usage tracking`, body filled from `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`, label `assistant:<vendor>` applied.
- **Exit**: PR exists with the label. CI workflow takes over from here — no skill is needed for the build itself.

### 1.3 CI builds the DMG and posts a sticky comment

- **Trigger**: GH Actions reacts to the `assistant:*` label or to a push on a labelled PR. No skill involvement.
- **Inputs**: the PR head commit.

Notes:

- The sticky comment is updated in place on every push so the PR has a single source of truth for "where is the latest build". `concurrency: cancel-in-progress` on the workflow prevents stacking obsolete artifacts.

Could a third-party developer reproduce this connector from the doc alone? If no, request more detail in the doc, not just the code comments).
- **Exit**: every checklist box is either ✅ or has a corresponding review comment.

The documentation track is historically the easiest to skip and the costliest to skip. The skill's job is to make skipping it unergonomic — it always reads the vendor doc top-to-bottom before reading any code.

### 1.5 Recruit testers

# New assistant onboarding

## Prerequisites

Read these specs first; this skill only orchestrates.

- `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` — the technical contract every assistant must satisfy.
- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` — phase-by-phase workflow this skill executes.

- The Swift quality docs listed in `CLAUDE.md`.

## Phase 1 — Gather context
Mine the conversation. Ask only what is missing: vendor slug, display name, tint hex,
asset source (URL or inline SVG), known auth sources (env / keychain / file),
known plan variants (free / paid / enterprise).

## Phase 2 — Duplicate check
List `docs/vendors/`. If the vendor already exists, stop and propose extending it.

## Phase 3 — API research write-up
Before any code: produce `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` from the template. Source every
claim. The user must approve this draft before phase 4.

## Phase 4 — Scaffold
Run `scripts/scaffold-vendor.sh <slug> "<display>" <tintHex>` (or call the equivalent
internal helper). Verify the build still compiles.

## Phase 5 — Implement against the contract
For each plugin point, read the contract section, then implement. After each
implementation step, run the targeted XCTest suite for that connector.

## Phase 6 — Tests
At minimum: auth cascade, payload parsing happy path, every documented plan variant,
HTTP error codes, metric calculation, date normalization. No silent `try?` per

## Contract
- [ ] Bundle registered in VendorRegistry; AppDelegate untouched.

- [ ] resetAt values are strict ISO 8601 datetimes (DEBUG assertion holds).
- [ ] Active-account monitor implements ActiveAccountMonitoring.start/stop.

## Documentation

- [ ] A reader unfamiliar with the codebase could reproduce the connector from

## Tests

# New assistant onboarding

## Prerequisites

input.new_string:
### 1.5 Recruit testers (continued)

Recruitment is partly off-platform (community channels) but the system of record is the PR comment thread.

- **Trigger**: PR is opened with the build comment present.
- **Skill**: none required for recruitment itself; the PR template's pre-filled "Testers wanted" block does the visible work.
- **Outputs over time**: tester comments using the sign-off template (see §1.6).

### 1.6 Tester sign-off

- **Trigger**: a tester downloads the DMG, runs it, and posts a comment.
- **Skill**: `new-assistant-tester-followup` (see §2.3) — invoked by the maintainer when a comment arrives, especially if the comment is incomplete (missing build SHA, missing plan, ambiguous result) and the maintainer wants help triaging or replying.
- **Inputs**: the PR comments, the latest build SHA from the sticky comment.
- **Outputs**: a tally of valid confirmations on the **latest** build SHA, a list of follow-up questions for incomplete comments, an explicit "ready to merge" / "not yet" verdict.
- **Exit**: tally is up to date and posted as a comment or report to the maintainer.

Tester sign-off comment template:

```
✅ tester-confirm

Plan: <Free | Pro | Team | Enterprise | Other: …>
macOS: <14.x | 15.x | 26.x>
Build SHA: <first 8 chars from the PR build comment>
Verified:
- [x] Active account is detected correctly
- [x] At least one usage metric matches the vendor's own dashboard within reasonable tolerance
- [x] Reset date displayed in the popover matches the vendor's reported reset
- [ ] Optional: outage banner appears when the vendor reports an incident
Notes: <free-form>
```

Counting rules:

- The PR author's own confirmation does **not** count toward the threshold.
- A confirmation is valid only if the build SHA matches the latest build comment (re-confirmation required after a rebase that changes the build).
- The follow-up skill scans comments for the `✅ tester-confirm` sentinel and produces a `Tester confirmations: N/2 ✅` summary. The summary is informational; the actual merge decision stays with the maintainer.

Threshold for merge: **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** with valid confirmations on the latest build.

### 1.7 Merge

- **Trigger**: maintainer judges the threshold reached and the review clean.
- **Skill**: `new-assistant-merge` (see §2.4) — performs all post-merge updates that would otherwise be forgotten.
- **Inputs**: the PR number, the merged commit, the list of testers who confirmed.
- **Outputs**: PR squash-merged, `README.md` "Supported assistants" section updated, `docs/vendors/index.md` row added (if the vendor index exists), GitHub release notes prepped to credit testers by handle on the next tag.
- **Exit**: every post-merge artifact is in place; nothing is left to "remember to do later".

The next tagged release (`v*.*.*`) ships the support; the existing `release.yml` workflow is unchanged.

### 1.8 Re-verify (later, on demand)

- **Trigger**: contributor or maintainer suspects API drift (e.g., a metric value looks wrong, the connector starts erroring), or simply opens the doc and notices `Last verified` is months old.
- **Skill**: `new-assistant-reverify` (see §2.5).
- **Inputs**: the existing `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`, a fresh request against the vendor's API.
- **Outputs**: an updated doc with bumped `Last verified` date, new sample payloads tagged with today's date (old ones marked superseded but kept), an appended Change log entry summarizing what changed since the last verification, and any code adjustments forced by the drift in a follow-up PR.
- **Exit**: doc accurately reflects the current API behavior; the change log captures the delta.

This phase is decoupled from the onboarding because it happens long after merge — sometimes years later. It belongs in the same skill family because it operates on the same vendor doc structure.

## 2. Skill family

`.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md` mandates that operational artifacts reference specs rather than duplicating them. Each skill below points at `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` and `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`, and includes the conflict-resolution clause.

The skills are deliberately split per trigger event so that:

- The right skill is invoked when the right thing happens, even days apart.
- Each skill is self-contained — it reads PR/repo state on every invocation and never assumes prior session memory.
- A user uncertain which phase they're in can run `new-assistant` (the meta-skill) which asks one question and routes to the right sub-skill.

### 2.0 `new-assistant` (meta / router)

Entry-point skill for users who don't know which sub-skill to invoke.

```
---
name: new-assistant
description: Routes the user to the correct sub-skill of the new-assistant family. Use when the user mentions adding, reviewing, validating, merging, or re-verifying a vendor and is unsure which workflow step they're at.
model: sonnet
---

# New assistant — meta router

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` first; it owns the phase ↔ skill map.

Ask one question: "Where are you in the new-assistant workflow?"

- "Just starting / writing code" → suggest `/new-assistant-implement`
- "PR is open, you want a review" → suggest `/new-assistant-review <pr#>`
- "Tester comments arrived, you want to triage" → suggest `/new-assistant-tester-followup <pr#>`
- "Threshold reached, you want to merge" → suggest `/new-assistant-merge <pr#>`
- "API drift suspected on an existing vendor" → suggest `/new-assistant-reverify <vendor>`

Do not run sub-skills automatically — name the right one and stop, so the user invokes it explicitly with the right context.
```

### 2.1 `new-assistant-implement`

Owns phases 1.1 and 1.2.

```
---
name: new-assistant-implement
description: Scaffold, implement, document, test, and open a PR for a new AI assistant against the vendor plugin contract. Use when starting a new assistant.
model: opus
---

# Implementation skill

Prerequisite: read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`, `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`,
`docs/vendors/_TEMPLATE.md`, and the Swift quality docs listed in CLAUDE.md.
If this skill and any spec disagree, the spec wins; flag to the user.

## Phase A — Gather context
Mine the conversation. Ask only what's missing: vendor slug, display name, tint hex,
asset source, known credential sources, known plan variants.

## Phase B — Duplicate check
List `docs/vendors/`. If the vendor already exists, stop and propose extending it.

## Phase C — API research write-up
Before any code, produce `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` from the template. Stamp
`Last verified: <today>` and per-section dates. Source every claim with retrieval
date. The user must approve this draft before phase D.

## Phase D — Scaffold
Create the connector / locator / status / monitor / branding files inline using
Write/Edit. There is no shell script. Verify `swift build` compiles.

## Phase E — Implement against the contract
For each plugin point, read the contract section, then implement. After each step,
run the targeted XCTest suite. Forbidden: `MetricKind.unknown` in connector output;
`try?` on operations whose failure affects correctness; calls to SecItemAdd/Update/Delete
in the credential locator.

## Phase F — Tests
At minimum: credential cascade, payload parsing happy path, every documented plan
variant, HTTP error codes, metric calculation, date normalization.

## Phase G — Open the PR
Use `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`. Pre-fill from the research doc.
Add the `assistant:<vendor>` label. Hand off to `new-assistant-review` when the
maintainer is ready.
```

### 2.2 `new-assistant-review`

Owns phase 1.4. Invoked at PR review time, possibly days after the PR opened.

```
---
name: new-assistant-review
description: Review an open new-assistant PR against the dual-track checklist (code + dated documentation). Use when reviewing an `assistant:<vendor>` PR.
model: opus
---

# Review skill

Argument: PR number.

Prerequisite: read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`, `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`,
`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`.

## Phase A — Reconstruct state
`gh pr view <pr#>`, fetch the diff, read `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` and the
connector under review.

## Phase B — Documentation track first
Read `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` standalone, before opening the diff. Verify:
- `Last verified` header present and recent.
- Per-section dates present where required by the template.
- Sample payloads tagged with their plan and capture date.
- Change log seeded.
- A reader unfamiliar with the codebase could reproduce the connector from this doc.

## Phase C — Code track
Run the contract conformance test. Walk the reviewer checklist on the diff.

## Phase D — Post review
Post a single PR review comment with all findings, grouped by checklist section.
Approve only if every box is green.
```

### 2.3 `new-assistant-tester-followup`

Owns phase 1.6. Invoked when tester comments arrive.

```
---
name: new-assistant-tester-followup
description: Triage tester sign-off comments on a new-assistant PR, count valid confirmations, and draft follow-up questions for incomplete ones. Use when tester feedback comes in.
model: sonnet
---

# Tester follow-up skill

Argument: PR number.

## Phase A — Reconstruct state
`gh pr view <pr#> --comments`. Find the latest sticky build comment to get the
current build SHA.

## Phase B — Tally
Scan comments for the `✅ tester-confirm` sentinel. For each, validate:
- Author ≠ PR author.
- Build SHA matches the latest build.
- All required checklist boxes filled.

## Phase C — Report
Post (or update) a comment summarizing `Tester confirmations: N/2 ✅`, listing
valid confirmations, and listing incomplete comments with the exact follow-up
question to ask each tester. Do not merge.
```

### 2.4 `new-assistant-merge`

Owns phase 1.7. Invoked when the maintainer is ready to merge.

```
---
name: new-assistant-merge
description: Run the post-merge updates for a new-assistant PR (README, docs/vendors/index, release-notes draft). Use when the threshold is reached and the maintainer is ready to merge.
model: sonnet
---

# Merge skill

Argument: PR number.

## Phase A — Verify gates
Re-check the tester tally and the reviewer checklist. Refuse to proceed if either
gate is not green.

## Phase B — Squash-merge
Use the standard commit message convention.

## Phase C — Post-merge updates
- Append the new vendor to README's "Supported assistants" section.
- Add a row to `docs/vendors/index.md` if it exists.
- Stage a release-notes line crediting tester GitHub handles.

## Phase D — Hand-off
Tell the user the merge is complete and what release will ship the support.
```

### 2.5 `new-assistant-reverify`

Owns phase 1.8. Invoked weeks or months later when API drift is suspected or
when proactively refreshing the vendor doc.

```
---
name: new-assistant-reverify
description: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, and append a Change log entry. Use when API drift is suspected or for proactive refresh.
model: opus
---

# Re-verify skill

Argument: vendor slug.

## Phase A — Read the existing snapshot
Open `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`. Note `Last verified` and per-section dates.

## Phase B — Live capture
Run the connector against the vendor's live API (or guide the user to do so).
Capture sanitized payloads, dated today.

## Phase C — Diff
Compare each captured payload against the dated samples in the doc. Identify
every drift: new fields, removed fields, changed types, changed semantics.

## Phase D — Update the doc
Bump `Last verified` to today. Add new dated samples; mark old ones as superseded
(do NOT delete them — the historical trail is the value). Append a Change log
entry summarizing what drifted.

## Phase E — Code follow-up
If any drift breaks the connector or warrants new behavior, open a follow-up PR
with the doc update + the code change. Do not bundle re-verification with the
original new-assistant PR.
```

All skills reference the specs (contract + onboarding) instead of duplicating templates and grammars.
