- Sample payloads tagged with their plan and capture date.
- Change log seeded.
- A reader unfamiliar with the codebase could reproduce the connector from this doc.

## Phase C — Code track
Run the contract conformance test. Walk the reviewer checklist on the diff.

## Phase D — Post review
Post a single PR review comment with all findings, grouped by checklist section.
Approve only if every box is green.
```

### 2.3 `new-assistant-tester-followup`

Owns phase 1.6. Invoked when tester comments arrive.

```
---
name: new-assistant-tester-followup
description: Triage tester sign-off comments on a new-assistant PR, count valid confirmations, and draft follow-up questions for incomplete ones. Use when tester feedback comes in.
model: sonnet
---

# Tester follow-up skill

Argument: PR number.

## Phase A — Reconstruct state
`gh pr view <pr#> --comments`. Find the latest sticky build comment to get the
current build SHA.

## Phase B — Tally
Scan comments for the `✅ tester-confirm` sentinel. For each, validate:
- Author ≠ PR author.
- Build SHA matches the latest build.
- All required checklist boxes filled.

## Phase C — Report
Post (or update) a comment summarizing `Tester confirmations: N/2 ✅`, listing
valid confirmations, and listing incomplete comments with the exact follow-up
question to ask each tester. Do not merge.
```

### 2.4 `new-assistant-merge`

Owns phase 1.7. Invoked when the maintainer is ready to merge.

```
---
name: new-assistant-merge
description: Run the post-merge updates for a new-assistant PR (README, docs/vendors/index, release-notes draft). Use when the threshold is reached and the maintainer is ready to merge.
model: sonnet
---

# Merge skill

Argument: PR number.

## Phase A — Verify gates
Re-check the tester tally and the reviewer checklist. Refuse to proceed if either
gate is not green.

## Phase B — Squash-merge
Use the standard commit message convention.

## Phase C — Post-merge updates
- Append the new vendor to README's "Supported assistants" section.
- Add a row to `docs/vendors/index.md` if it exists.
- Stage a release-notes line crediting tester GitHub handles.

## Phase D — Hand-off
Tell the user the merge is complete and what release will ship the support.
```

### 2.5 `new-assistant-reverify`

Owns phase 1.8. Invoked weeks or months later when API drift is suspected or
when proactively refreshing the vendor doc.

```
---
name: new-assistant-reverify
description: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, and append a Change log entry. Use when API drift is suspected or for proactive refresh.
model: opus
---

# Re-verify skill

Argument: vendor slug.

## Phase A — Read the existing snapshot
Open `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`. Note `Last verified` and per-section dates.

## Phase B — Live capture
Run the connector against the vendor's live API (or guide the user to do so).
Capture sanitized payloads, dated today.

## Phase C — Diff
Compare each captured payload against the dated samples in the doc. Identify
every drift: new fields, removed fields, changed types, changed semantics.

## Phase D — Update the doc
Bump `Last verified` to today. Add new dated samples; mark old ones as superseded
(do NOT delete them — the historical trail is the value). Append a Change log
entry summarizing what drifted.

## Phase E — Code follow-up
If any drift breaks the connector or warrants new behavior, open a follow-up PR
with the doc update + the code change. Do not bundle re-verification with the
original new-assistant PR.
```

All skills reference the specs (contract + onboarding) instead of duplicating templates and grammars.

lines: Threshold for merge: **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** with valid confirmations on the latest build.
-### 1.8 Merge
+### 1.5 Recruit testers (continued)
-When all gates are green:
+Recruitment is partly off-platform (community channels) but the system of record is the PR comment thread.
-1. Maintainer merges (squash) with the standard commit message convention.
-2. Post-merge automation (or a checklist item in the PR template) updates:
-   - `README.md` — add the new assistant to the supported list.
-   - `docs/vendors/index.md` — add a row to the vendor table.
-3. The next tagged release (`v*.*.*`) ships the support; the existing `release.yml` workflow is unchanged.
+- **Trigger**: PR is opened with the build comment present.
+- **Skill**: none required for recruitment itself; the PR template's pre-filled "Testers wanted" block does the visible work.
+- **Outputs over time**: tester comments using the sign-off template (see §1.6).
-### 1.9 Officialize
+### 1.6 Tester sign-off

-A short announcement note (changelog line in the GitHub release notes auto-generated by `release.yml`) calls out the new vendor with credit to testers (linking their GitHub handles from the validation comments).
+- **Trigger**: a tester downloads the DMG, runs it, and posts a comment.

+- **Skill**: `new-assistant-tester-followup` (see §2.3) — invoked by the maintainer when a comment arrives, especially if the comment is incomplete (missing build SHA, missing plan, ambiguous result) and the maintainer wants help triaging or replying.
+- **Inputs**: the PR comments, the latest build SHA from the sticky comment.
+- **Outputs**: a tally of valid confirmations on the **latest** build SHA, a list of follow-up questions for incomplete comments, an explicit "ready to merge" / "not yet" verdict.
+- **Exit**: tally is up to date and posted as a comment or report to the maintainer.
-## 2. Skill structure (`.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md`)
+Tester sign-off comment template:
-Following `.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md` — operational artifact, not a spec.
+✅ tester-confirm
+Plan: <Free | Pro | Team | Enterprise | Other: …>
+Build SHA: <first 8 chars from the PR build comment>
+Verified:
+- [x] Active account is detected correctly
+- [x] At least one usage metric matches the vendor's own dashboard within reasonable tolerance
+- [x] Reset date displayed in the popover matches the vendor's reported reset
+- [ ] Optional: outage banner appears when the vendor reports an incident
+Notes: <free-form>
+Counting rules:
+- The PR author's own confirmation does **not** count toward the threshold.
+- A confirmation is valid only if the build SHA matches the latest build comment (re-confirmation required after a rebase that changes the build).

+- The follow-up skill scans comments for the `✅ tester-confirm` sentinel and produces a `Tester confirmations: N/2 ✅` summary. The summary is informational; the actual merge decision stays with the maintainer.
+Threshold for merge: **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** with valid confirmations on the latest build.
+### 1.7 Merge
+- **Trigger**: maintainer judges the threshold reached and the review clean.
+- **Skill**: `new-assistant-merge` (see §2.4) — performs all post-merge updates that would otherwise be forgotten.
+- **Inputs**: the PR number, the merged commit, the list of testers who confirmed.

+- **Outputs**: PR squash-merged, `README.md` "Supported assistants" section updated, `docs/vendors/index.md` row added (if the vendor index exists), GitHub release notes prepped to credit testers by handle on the next tag.
+- **Exit**: every post-merge artifact is in place; nothing is left to "remember to do later".
+The next tagged release (`v*.*.*`) ships the support; the existing `release.yml` workflow is unchanged.
+### 1.8 Re-verify (later, on demand)
+- **Trigger**: contributor or maintainer suspects API drift (e.g., a metric value looks wrong, the connector starts erroring), or simply opens the doc and notices `Last verified` is months old.
+- **Skill**: `new-assistant-reverify` (see §2.5).
+- **Inputs**: the existing `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`, a fresh request against the vendor's API.

+- **Outputs**: an updated doc with bumped `Last verified` date, new sample payloads tagged with today's date (old ones marked superseded but kept), an appended Change log entry summarizing what changed since the last verification, and any code adjustments forced by the drift in a follow-up PR.
+- **Exit**: doc accurately reflects the current API behavior; the change log captures the delta.
+This phase is decoupled from the onboarding because it happens long after merge — sometimes years later. It belongs in the same skill family because it operates on the same vendor doc structure.
+## 2. Skill family

+`.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md` mandates that operational artifacts reference specs rather than duplicating them. Each skill below points at `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` and `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md`, and includes the conflict-resolution clause.
+The skills are deliberately split per trigger event so that:
+- The right skill is invoked when the right thing happens, even days apart.
+- Each skill is self-contained — it reads PR/repo state on every invocation and never assumes prior session memory.
+- A user uncertain which phase they're in can run `new-assistant` (the meta-skill) which asks one question and routes to the right sub-skill.
+### 2.0 `new-assistant` (meta / router)
+Entry-point skill for users who don't know which sub-skill to invoke.

397	## 3. Reviewer checklist (sketch)
398	
399	To live in `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md` — referenced by the PR template.
400	
401	```
402	## Contract
403	- [ ] Bundle registered in VendorRegistry; AppDelegate untouched.
404	- [ ] Auth provider implements VendorAuthProviding with injectable env/keychain/file sources.
405	- [ ] No MetricKind.unknown in the connector output (contract test passes).
406	- [ ] resetAt values are strict ISO 8601 datetimes (DEBUG assertion holds).
407	- [ ] Active-account monitor implements ActiveAccountMonitoring.start/stop.
408	
409	## Documentation
410	- [ ] docs/vendors/<vendor>.md is complete: endpoints, auth cascade, plan variants,
411	      metric semantics, time semantics, error catalog, source references.
412	- [ ] A reader unfamiliar with the codebase could reproduce the connector from
413	      this doc alone.
414	
415	## Tests
416	- [ ] Auth cascade covered (env / keychain / file each individually + cascade).
417	- [ ] Each documented plan variant has a representative response fixture.
418	- [ ] HTTP error codes seen in the wild are tested (4xx / 5xx).
419	- [ ] Date normalization tested (calendar date → UTC midnight if applicable).
420	
421	## Build & validation
422	- [ ] Nightly build artifact present in the sticky PR comment, SHA-256 listed.
423	- [ ] At least 2 non-author tester confirmations on the latest build SHA.
424	- [ ] Reproducible-build instructions verified by the reviewer (optional spot check).
425	
426	## Branding
427	- [ ] PDF mark renders cleanly in light + dark menu bars.
428	- [ ] tintHex matches vendor brand reasonably; readable on both bars.
429	
430	## Post-merge follow-ups
431	- [ ] README "Supported assistants" updated in the same PR.
432	```
433	
434	## 4. Build pipeline detail
435	
436	### 4.1 Workflow file `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`
437	
438	```yaml
439	name: New-assistant nightly build
440	
441	on:
442	  pull_request:
443	    types: [labeled, synchronize, opened, reopened]
444	  workflow_dispatch:
445	
446	concurrency:
447	  group: assistant-build-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
448	  cancel-in-progress: true
449	
450	jobs:
451	  build:
452	    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'assistant')  # any assistant:* label
453	    runs-on: macos-15
454	    timeout-minutes: 30
455	    permissions:
456	      contents: read
457	      pull-requests: write
458	    steps:
459	      # Reuse the steps from release.yml: select Xcode 26, strip SwiftLint plugin,
460	      # run ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh, produce ZIP + DMG, compute SHA-256.
461	      # Then upload as workflow artifact AND post a sticky PR comment with links
462	      # and reproducible-build instructions.
463	```
464	
465	The matching label uses `assistant` (any `assistant:*` label triggers it). DMG packaging is added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` or wrapped by a sibling script.
466	
467	### 4.2 Sticky comment shape
468	
469	Maintained by an action that creates or edits a comment marked with an HTML sentinel:
470	
471	```html
472	<!-- new-assistant-build:sticky -->
473	🛠 Build for commit <SHA>
474	
475	- [Download ZIP](…) — SHA-256 `…`
476	- [Download DMG](…) — SHA-256 `…`
477	
478	Reproducible build:
479	
480	```sh
481	git checkout <SHA>
482	./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
483	# Verify: shasum -a 256 dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip
484	```
485	
486	Builds are ad-hoc signed only. First launch: right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper.
487	```
488	
489	### 4.3 Reproducible-build doc
490	
491	`docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md` — short, stable: prerequisites (Xcode 26+, macOS 15+, the SwiftLint-plugin caveat for CI), the exact command sequence, and the expected SHA-256 verification flow.
492	
493	## 5. Risks and open questions
494	
495	- **Tester recruitment latency.** Some vendors have small audiences; getting two confirmations may take days/weeks. The workflow does not pressure-merge — PRs simply wait. The README should call this out so contributors know the timeline up front.
496	- **Plan-variant coverage gap.** A tester on Free plan does not validate Paid behavior. The threshold of ≥ 2 testers is a floor, not a ceiling — the reviewer checklist asks the maintainer to weigh whether all documented plan variants are covered before merging.
497	- **Sticky-comment race conditions.** Two pushes in quick succession may produce two comments. Mitigated by `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true` and by the sentinel-driven find-or-create pattern.
498	- **DMG vs ZIP tradeoff.** DMG is more familiar to casual users but slightly larger. We ship both — testers download whichever they prefer.
499	- **PR template visibility.** GitHub picks the template based on the URL parameter; without the parameter, contributors get the default template. The skill must either set `&template=new-assistant.md` in the URL it suggests or explicitly instruct the contributor to use it.
500	- **Self-approval.** The author's own subscription is the most convenient way to validate — but it does not count toward the threshold. The PR template explicitly states this.
501	- **Skipped reviews.** Documentation review is the easiest to skip. Mitigation: the reviewer checklist is mandatory and the PR template includes a `Reviewer signed off on docs/vendors/<vendor>.md ☐` checkbox.
502

file.content:
## Phase C — Report
Post (or update) a comment summarizing `Tester confirmations: N/2 ✅`, listing
valid confirmations, and listing incomplete comments with the exact follow-up
question to ask each tester. Do not merge.
```

### 2.4 `new-assistant-merge`

Owns phase 1.7. Invoked when the maintainer is ready to merge.

```
---
name: new-assistant-merge
description: Run the post-merge updates for a new-assistant PR (README, docs/vendors/index, release-notes draft). Use when the threshold is reached and the maintainer is ready to merge.
model: sonnet
---

# Merge skill

Argument: PR number.

## Phase A — Verify gates
Re-check the tester tally and the reviewer checklist. Refuse to proceed if either
gate is not green.

## Phase B — Squash-merge
Use the standard commit message convention.

## Phase C — Post-merge updates
- Append the new vendor to README's "Supported assistants" section.
- Add a row to `docs/vendors/index.md` if it exists.
- Stage a release-notes line crediting tester GitHub handles.

## Phase D — Hand-off
Tell the user the merge is complete and what release will ship the support.
```

### 2.5 `new-assistant-reverify`

Owns phase 1.8. Invoked weeks or months later when API drift is suspected or
when proactively refreshing the vendor doc.

```
---
name: new-assistant-reverify
description: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, and append a Change log entry. Use when API drift is suspected or for proactive refresh.
model: opus
---

# Re-verify skill

Argument: vendor slug.

## Phase A — Read the existing snapshot
Open `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md`. Note `Last verified` and per-section dates.

## Phase B — Live capture
Run the connector against the vendor's live API (or guide the user to do so).
Capture sanitized payloads, dated today.

## Phase C — Diff
Compare each captured payload against the dated samples in the doc. Identify
every drift: new fields, removed fields, changed types, changed semantics.

## Phase D — Update the doc
Bump `Last verified` to today. Add new dated samples; mark old ones as superseded
(do NOT delete them — the historical trail is the value). Append a Change log
entry summarizing what drifted.

## Phase E — Code follow-up
If any drift breaks the connector or warrants new behavior, open a follow-up PR
with the doc update + the code change. Do not bundle re-verification with the
original new-assistant PR.
```

All skills reference the specs (contract + onboarding) instead of duplicating templates and grammars.

## 3. Reviewer checklist (sketch)

To live in `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md` — referenced by the PR template.

```
## Contract
- [ ] Bundle registered in VendorRegistry; AppDelegate untouched.
- [ ] Auth provider implements VendorAuthProviding with injectable env/keychain/file sources.
- [ ] No MetricKind.unknown in the connector output (contract test passes).
- [ ] resetAt values are strict ISO 8601 datetimes (DEBUG assertion holds).
- [ ] Active-account monitor implements ActiveAccountMonitoring.start/stop.

## Documentation
- [ ] docs/vendors/<vendor>.md is complete: endpoints, auth cascade, plan variants,
      metric semantics, time semantics, error catalog, source references.
- [ ] A reader unfamiliar with the codebase could reproduce the connector from
      this doc alone.

## Tests
- [ ] Auth cascade covered (env / keychain / file each individually + cascade).
- [ ] Each documented plan variant has a representative response fixture.
- [ ] HTTP error codes seen in the wild are tested (4xx / 5xx).
- [ ] Date normalization tested (calendar date → UTC midnight if applicable).

## Build & validation
- [ ] Nightly build artifact present in the sticky PR comment, SHA-256 listed.
- [ ] At least 2 non-author tester confirmations on the latest build SHA.
- [ ] Reproducible-build instructions verified by the reviewer (optional spot check).

## Branding
- [ ] PDF mark renders cleanly in light + dark menu bars.
- [ ] tintHex matches vendor brand reasonably; readable on both bars.

## Post-merge follow-ups
- [ ] README "Supported assistants" updated in the same PR.
```

## 4. Build pipeline detail

### 4.1 Workflow file `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`

```yaml
name: New-assistant nightly build

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [labeled, synchronize, opened, reopened]
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: assistant-build-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  build:
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'assistant')  # any assistant:* label
    runs-on: macos-15
    timeout-minutes: 30
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      # Reuse the steps from release.yml: select Xcode 26, strip SwiftLint plugin,
      # run ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh, produce ZIP + DMG, compute SHA-256.
      # Then upload as workflow artifact AND post a sticky PR comment with links
      # and reproducible-build instructions.
```

The matching label uses `assistant` (any `assistant:*` label triggers it). DMG packaging is added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` or wrapped by a sibling script.

### 4.2 Sticky comment shape

Maintained by an action that creates or edits a comment marked with an HTML sentinel:

```html
<!-- new-assistant-build:sticky -->
🛠 Build for commit <SHA>

- [Download ZIP](…) — SHA-256 `…`
- [Download DMG](…) — SHA-256 `…`

Reproducible build:

```sh
git checkout <SHA>
./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
# Verify: shasum -a 256 dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip
```

Builds are ad-hoc signed only. First launch: right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper.
```

### 4.3 Reproducible-build doc

`docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md` — short, stable: prerequisites (Xcode 26+, macOS 15+, the SwiftLint-plugin caveat for CI), the exact command sequence, and the expected SHA-256 verification flow.

## 5. Risks and open questions

- **Tester recruitment latency.** Some vendors have small audiences; getting two confirmations may take days/weeks. The workflow does not pressure-merge — PRs simply wait. The README should call this out so contributors know the timeline up front.
- **Plan-variant coverage gap.** A tester on Free plan does not validate Paid behavior. The threshold of ≥ 2 testers is a floor, not a ceiling — the reviewer checklist asks the maintainer to weigh whether all documented plan variants are covered before merging.
- **Sticky-comment race conditions.** Two pushes in quick succession may produce two comments. Mitigated by `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true` and by the sentinel-driven find-or-create pattern.
- **DMG vs ZIP tradeoff.** DMG is more familiar to casual users but slightly larger. We ship both — testers download whichever they prefer.
- **PR template visibility.** GitHub picks the template based on the URL parameter; without the parameter, contributors get the default template. The skill must either set `&template=new-assistant.md` in the URL it suggests or explicitly instruct the contributor to use it.
- **Self-approval.** The author's own subscription is the most convenient way to validate — but it does not count toward the threshold. The PR template explicitly states this.
- **Skipped reviews.** Documentation review is the easiest to skip. Mitigation: the reviewer checklist is mandatory and the PR template includes a `Reviewer signed off on docs/vendors/<vendor>.md ☐` checkbox.

## 6. Deliverables checklist

- [ ] `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
- [ ] `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`
- [ ] `docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md`
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`
- [ ] `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`
- [ ] DMG packaging step added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` (or sibling script)
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` lazy-loaded context update (mention `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`)
- [ ] README "Supported assistants" section restructured to be append-friendly

## 4. Build pipeline detail

The build pipeline is **CI-driven and skill-free** — no skill should manage it. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; the trust escape hatch for skeptical readers is "clone the branch and build it yourself", documented in `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`.

### 4.1 Workflow file `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`

```yaml
name: New-assistant nightly build

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [labeled, synchronize, opened, reopened]
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: assistant-build-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  build:
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'assistant')  # any assistant:* label
    runs-on: macos-15
    timeout-minutes: 30
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      # Reuse the prep steps from release.yml: select Xcode 26, strip SwiftLint
      # plugin, run ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh.
      # Package the .app into a DMG (no ZIP), compute SHA-256, upload as workflow
      # artifact AND post or update a sticky PR comment.
```

The matching label uses `assistant` (any `assistant:*` label triggers it). DMG packaging is added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` or wrapped by a sibling script — the implementation choice is left to the actual implementation PR.

### 4.2 Sticky comment shape

Maintained by an action that creates or edits a comment marked with an HTML sentinel so updates land in the same comment instead of stacking:

```
<!-- new-assistant-build:sticky -->
🛠 Build for commit `<SHA>`

- [Download DMG](…) — SHA-256 `…`

Builds are ad-hoc signed only. First launch: right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper.

Don't trust this prebuilt DMG? Build it yourself in 5 minutes — see
[`docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`](…).
```

The sticky comment is intentionally minimal — DMG link, SHA, and a pointer to the build-from-source doc. Long instructions belong in the doc, not in a comment that has to be re-read on every push.

### 4.3 The "build it yourself" doc

`docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` is the trust anchor. It targets a non-developer reader who has Xcode but no Swift experience, and walks through:

1. **Prerequisites** — Xcode 26 (or whichever version the package manifest pins), macOS 15+, `git` available in PATH. Pointers to install Xcode for readers who don't have it.
2. **Get the source** — two paths: `gh pr checkout <pr#>` for users who have `gh`, or "Code → Download ZIP" from the GitHub PR page for users who don't.
3. **Build** — exact command sequence (`./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` or the explicit `swift build` invocation, whichever ends up being the canonical entry point).
4. **Run** — how to launch the resulting `.app` from the `dist/` folder, including the right-click → Open Gatekeeper bypass.
5. **Compare** — optional `shasum -a 256` against the SHA in the sticky comment, so the reader can confirm their local build matches the CI build (when build is reproducible) or notes when it does not.

The doc is short by design — long enough to follow, short enough that a tester reads it in full. Anything CI-specific (SwiftLint plugin stripping, GH Actions step list) stays in the workflow file or in `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`.

3. **Build** — exact command sequence (`./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` or the explicit `swift build` invocation, whichever ends up being the canonical entry point).
4. **Run** — how to launch the resulting `.app` from the `dist/` folder, including the right-click → Open Gatekeeper bypass.
5. **Compare** — optional `shasum -a 256` against the SHA in the sticky comment, so the reader can confirm their local build matches the CI build (when build is reproducible) or notes when it does not.

The doc is short by design — long enough to follow, short enough that a tester reads it in full. Anything CI-specific (SwiftLint plugin stripping, GH Actions step list) stays in the workflow file or in `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`.

+The build pipeline is **CI-driven and skill-free** — no skill should manage it. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; the trust escape hatch for skeptical readers is "clone the branch and build it yourself", documented in `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`.
### 4.1 Workflow file `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`

+`docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` is the trust anchor. It targets a non-developer reader who has Xcode but no Swift experience, and walks through:

input.old_string:
## 5. Risks and open questions

- **Tester recruitment latency.** Some vendors have small audiences; getting two confirmations may take days/weeks. The workflow does not pressure-merge — PRs simply wait. The README should call this out so contributors know the timeline up front.
- **Plan-variant coverage gap.** A tester on Free plan does not validate Paid behavior. The threshold of ≥ 2 testers is a floor, not a ceiling — the reviewer checklist asks the maintainer to weigh whether all documented plan variants are covered before merging.
- **Sticky-comment race conditions.** Two pushes in quick succession may produce two comments. Mitigated by `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true` and by the sentinel-driven find-or-create pattern.
- **DMG vs ZIP tradeoff.** DMG is more familiar to casual users but slightly larger. We ship both — testers download whichever they prefer.
- **PR template visibility.** GitHub picks the template based on the URL parameter; without the parameter, contributors get the default template. The skill must either set `&template=new-assistant.md` in the URL it suggests or explicitly instruct the contributor to use it.
- **Self-approval.** The author's own subscription is the most convenient way to validate — but it does not count toward the threshold. The PR template explicitly states this.
- **Skipped reviews.** Documentation review is the easiest to skip. Mitigation: the reviewer checklist is mandatory and the PR template includes a `Reviewer signed off on docs/vendors/<vendor>.md ☐` checkbox.

## 6. Deliverables checklist

- [ ] `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
- [ ] `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`
- [ ] `docs/REPRODUCIBLE-BUILDS.md`
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`
- [ ] `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml`
- [ ] DMG packaging step added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` (or sibling script)
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` lazy-loaded context update (mention `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`)
- [ ] README "Supported assistants" section restructured to be append-friendly

## 5. Risks and open questions

- **Multi-day temporality.** The defining constraint. Each skill must be invocable cold and rebuild context from PR + repo state — there is no continuity between sessions. Risk: a skill author drifts toward "assume the implementation skill ran 5 minutes ago". Mitigation: the meta-skill explicitly tells the user to invoke the right sub-skill with the right argument; sub-skills always start with `gh pr view` / file reads.
- **Tester recruitment latency.** Some vendors have small audiences; getting two confirmations may take days/weeks. The workflow does not pressure-merge — PRs simply wait. The README should call this out so contributors know the timeline up front.
- **Plan-variant coverage gap.** A tester on Free plan does not validate Paid behavior. The threshold of ≥ 2 testers is a floor, not a ceiling — the reviewer checklist asks the maintainer to weigh whether all documented plan variants are covered before merging. The "Known unknowns" section of the vendor doc explicitly lists unverified variants so they can be filled in by later testers.
- **Sticky-comment race conditions.** Two pushes in quick succession may produce two comments. Mitigated by `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true` and by the sentinel-driven find-or-create pattern.
- **PR template visibility.** GitHub picks the template based on the URL parameter; without the parameter, contributors get the default template. The implementation skill must either set `&template=new-assistant.md` in the URL it suggests, run `gh pr create --template`, or explicitly instruct the contributor to copy the template body manually.
- **Self-approval.** The author's own subscription is the most convenient way to validate — but it does not count toward the threshold. The PR template and the follow-up skill both enforce this.
- **Skipped reviews.** Documentation review is the easiest to skip. Mitigation: the review skill reads the vendor doc *before* the diff, and the reviewer checklist treats doc dating as a first-class section. The PR template includes a "Reviewer signed off on `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` ☐" checkbox that gates approval.
- **Vendor doc maintenance over time.** A perfectly dated snapshot from launch is worthless if nobody updates it. Mitigation: the contract-side rule "if you touch the connector, you bump `Last verified` and append a Change log entry" + the optional `new-assistant-reverify` skill for proactive refreshes.
- **Build reproducibility.** Swift release builds aren't strictly bit-for-bit reproducible (timestamps in binaries, optimization non-determinism). The "verify SHA-256" step in `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` is therefore optional and the doc says so. The trust path is "you read the source and you built it" — the SHA comparison is a bonus when builds happen to match.

## 6. Deliverables checklist

- [ ] `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` — phase ↔ skill ↔ trigger event map
- [ ] `docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`
- [ ] `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` — non-developer-friendly clone + build path
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant/SKILL.md` (meta router)
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant-implement/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant-review/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant-tester-followup/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant-merge/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.claude/skills/new-assistant-reverify/SKILL.md`
- [ ] `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md`
- [ ] `.github/workflows/new-assistant-build.yml` — DMG only, sticky comment
- [ ] DMG packaging step added to `scripts/build-app-bundle.sh` (or sibling script)
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` lazy-loaded context update (mention `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`)
- [ ] README "Supported assistants" section restructured to be append-friendly

content.thinking:
Je vérifie qu'il n'y a aucune trace des anciens éléments comme `VendorAuthProviding` ou les fichiers temporaires, et que les seules références à `new-assistant` sont légitimes. Les modifications sont cohérentes à travers les épics, notamment le remplacement de `VendorAuthProviding` par `CredentialLocator` dans l'Epic 1.

Pour l'Epic 2, j'ai restructuré la temporalité multi-jours avec cinq sous-compétences plus un meta-skill routeur, limité à DMG, renommé le document de confiance en `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`, et supprimé le ZIP ainsi que le script de scaffold.

content.text:
Tout est aligné. Récapitulatif des changements appliqués :

## Epic 1 — `vendor-plugin-framework`

**Vendor doc daté (snapshot crucial pour drift detection)** — la section §2.6 du plan est maintenant entièrement orientée autour de la discipline de datation :
- Header `Last verified: YYYY-MM-DD by @<handle> on <plan>` obligatoire
- Dates par section
- Sample payloads taggés `<!-- captured: YYYY-MM-DD, plan: Pro -->`, anciens samples conservés en "superseded" plutôt que supprimés (pour préserver la trace historique)
- Liens externes datés avec retrieval date
- Section "Known unknowns" listant ce qui n'est pas vérifié
- **Change log** append-only que le futur contributeur lit en premier face à un "ça marchait avant"

**Credential locator** — renommage `VendorAuthProviding` → `CredentialLocator` avec stance read-only explicite : l'app ne produit, refresh, ou rotate jamais de tokens. Chaque vendor CLI (`claude`, `codex`, `gh`) reste seul responsable de la sécurisation. SwiftLint custom rule prévue pour bloquer `SecItemAdd/Update/Delete` dans tout `*CredentialLocator.swift`. Tableau de migration explicite pour les 3 vendors existants.

**Suppression du scaffold shell** — la skill d'implémentation fait le scaffolding directement via Write/Edit (justification ajoutée : éviter la duplication contract / template, garder une source de vérité unique).

## Epic 2 — `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow`

**Temporalité multi-jours** — section §0 dédiée qui pose les deux principes : une skill par événement déclencheur, aucune skill ne suppose d'état de session précédent (toutes rebuildent leur contexte via `gh pr view` + lecture des fichiers).

**Famille de skills** au lieu d'une seule (§2) :
- `new-assistant` (meta router, sonnet) — pose une question, oriente vers la bonne sub-skill
- `new-assistant-implement` (opus) — phases A→G : context, dup-check, doc daté, scaffold, implem, tests, ouverture PR
- `new-assistant-review` (opus) — invoquée à la review, lit le vendor doc **avant** le diff
- `new-assistant-tester-followup` (sonnet) — tally des `✅ tester-confirm` sur le **latest build SHA**, drafts de relances pour les commentaires incomplets
- `new-assistant-merge` (sonnet) — vérifie les gates, squash-merge, met à jour README + `docs/vendors/index.md` + release notes
- `new-assistant-reverify` (opus) — phase 1.8, invocable des mois plus tard quand on suspecte un drift API

**DMG only** — ZIP supprimé partout. Le sticky comment ne contient que le DMG + SHA-256 + lien vers `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. Cette nouvelle doc cible un lecteur non-développeur (Xcode + git seulement) et est le vrai "trust anchor" — clone, build, run.

**Seuil testeurs** — confirmé à ≥ 2 non-auteurs distincts sur le latest build SHA.

content.thinking:
The user is proposing using a dedicated GitHub issue type instead of PRs for tracking this workflow, where only maintainers can move things through each gate. That's a clever approach—GitHub's custom issue types and templated forms could handle this, though I'd need to think through whether that's actually cleaner than the PR model for this use case.

The main appeal is that issues are designed for long-running conversations and visibility, and the issue itself becomes the historical record after merge. But there's real friction: two artifacts (issue + PR) means potential drift, maintainer-only gating is hard to enforce technically without a GitHub Action, and custom issue types aren't available on all GitHub plans. I'm sketching out what this would look like concretely—opening the issue first with a structured form, then linking the PR to it as work happens, with the issue closure marking the official step. Now I'm weighing the different ways to enforce maintainer-only gate advancement. A GitHub Action that reverts unauthorized label changes would work, but it adds operational overhead. The cleanest approach feels like using a skill that only the maintainer can invoke—it keeps the logic centralized and makes the audit trail clear. I could use a single `phase:<step>` label to track progress, with the skill handling transitions, and optionally add a validation Action as a safety net. Before I lock this in, I should check if the user is aligned on the overall approach. Je vois que l'utilisateur propose une bonne approche — créer un type d'issue dédié dans GitHub pour chaque nouvel assistant avec un workflow structuré où seul le mainteneur peut faire progresser les étapes. C'est pertinent parce que les issues sont conçues pour les workflows longs qui s'étalent sur plusieurs jours, contrairement aux PRs. L'issue devient le système de référence qui porte l'état du workflow sur la durée, tandis que la PR n'est qu'un artefact au sein de ce processus. Le compromis, c'est de gérer deux artefacts (issue + PR) et un peu plus de configuration, mais on gagne en visibilité, on peut recruter naturellement des testeurs, et on garde une trace persistante après la fusion.

content.text:
Excellente piste. Oui ça améliore nettement le design parce qu'une PR n'est pas le bon support pour un workflow qui s'étale sur plusieurs jours/semaines avec recrutement de testeurs en amont — l'issue est faite pour ça.

**Recommandation rapide :**

L'issue devient le **système de référence** sur tout le cycle "vendor X demandé → support officialisé" ; la PR n'est qu'un artefact intermédiaire référencé par l'issue. Avantages :
- Les testeurs peuvent s'abonner à l'issue **avant** que le code existe (recrutement plus naturel qu'un PR comment thread).
- L'issue persiste post-merge comme trace historique de "qui a contribué, qui a testé, sur quel build".
- Les gates deviennent un seul label mutex `phase:<étape>` (`proposed` → `approved` → `implementing` → `review` → `testing` → `merge-ready` → `merged` → `officialized`), beaucoup plus lisible qu'un patchwork de checkboxes dans la PR.
- Un type d'issue dédié (issue form avec champs structurés : vendor, proposer, plans connus, sources docs API) cadre dès le départ ce qu'on attend.

**Mainteneur-only sur les transitions :**

Le plus propre est une GH Action qui valide chaque changement de label `phase:*` et le rollback si l'auteur n'est pas dans une whitelist (CODEOWNERS-like). En complément, les skills `new-assistant-*` font les transitions via `gh issue edit --add-label` plutôt qu'à la main — donc seul le mainteneur qui invoque la skill peut déclencher une transition.

**Tradeoff principal :** double tracking issue + PR avec risque de drift entre les deux. Mitigé en fixant clairement la responsabilité : l'issue porte l'état macro (phase courante, tally testeurs, lien PR), la PR porte les détails techniques (diff, review, build artefacts).

**Impact sur l'épic 2 :** non négligeable — ça touche le PR template (devient issue template + PR template plus léger), le GH Actions workflow (déclencheur étendu aux issues + label mutex), le découpage des skills (chacune sait lire/écrire l'issue, pas seulement la PR), et `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` (la map phase ↔ skill devient phase ↔ label ↔ skill).

Tu veux que je refactor le plan de l'épic 2 dans cette direction (issue comme system of record + label mutex + GH Action de gating) ? Ou tu préfères d'abord qu'on stabilise quelques détails — par exemple la liste exacte des phases-labels, ou si on utilise un **custom issue type** GitHub natif (qui dépend du plan du repo) vs simplement un **issue form** standard (qui marche partout) ?

content.thinking:
I should address their question about creating a dedicated GitHub issue type first, then move into refactoring Epic 2. Looking at the options here—custom issue types are a newer GitHub feature that require Enterprise Cloud, so they have limited availability. Let me think through whether that's the right approach versus using issue forms or labels for this workflow.

Issue forms are the better choice for a public repo like this one. They're available on all plans, fully version-controlled in the repo, and give you the same structured input experience without needing Enterprise features. I'd recommend creating a dedicated issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` paired with a specific label like `type:new-assistant` to mark and query these issues. This approach is forward-compatible too—if GitHub makes custom issue types widely available later, the label system still works and you can migrate without losing anything.

Now I'm ready to refactor the plan itself. The core changes involve restructuring the phases around the issue lifecycle, using phase labels as gates, and having each skill read and write to the issue while advancing those labels. I'll also need to split out the reviewer checklist section.

The build pipeline stays DMG-only, but the build comment should appear on both the PR and the issue. I'll add a new GitHub Action to enforce that only maintainers can change `phase:*` labels, and I'm thinking through the relationship between the issue and PR—the issue tracks the macro phase while the PR carries the technical work. Once the PR merges, the issue moves to `phase:merged`, and after officialization it closes with `phase:officialized`.

The phase progression flows from `phase:proposed` when the issue opens, through `phase:approved` once the maintainer greenlights it, then into implementation and review stages.

Now I'm refining the phase labels themselves—I think `phase:released` is clearer than `phase:officialized` for the final state, so the full sequence is proposed → approved → implementing → review → testing → merge-ready → merged → released. Each skill will read the current phase from the issue's labels and transition to the next phase as work progresses: the implement skill moves from approved to implementing to review, the review skill transitions to testing, and a new merge skill handles the final stages.

For the proposed-to-approved transition, I'm thinking a manual maintainer review is probably sufficient rather than automating it with a triage skill, though we could add one later if needed. Now I'm sketching out a GitHub Action that gates phase label changes by checking the actor's permissions—only repo owners and listed maintainers can modify phase labels, and the action would revert unauthorized changes...

Writing GitHub Actions workflow...

Now I'm reconsidering the overall structure of the plan document. Instead of keeping the current organization, I should restructure it to flow around the phase model itself: start with why temporality and issues matter, then lay out the phase labels and transitions, walk through what happens at each phase, describe the skill family, add the issue form and PR template, explain the maintainer gating action, cover the build pipeline with issue cross-references, include the reviewer checklist, and finish with risks and deliverables. This should keep the document manageable while making the phase-driven workflow much clearer.

Given how extensive these changes are, I think it's better to rewrite the entire plan file from scratch rather than patch it incrementally. I'll start with the epic file since it's smaller, then tackle the full plan rewrite. Let me update the epic to emphasize the issue-as-system-of-record framing and the phase label approach. I need to decide between incremental edits or a full rewrite of the plan file. Given how substantial the restructuring is—adding the issue-as-system-of-record framing, reworking the phases with label-gating, integrating the GitHub Action and issue form into the deliverables—a complete rewrite will be cleaner than patching. Let me draft the new version from scratch.

content.text:
**Réponse rapide à ta question :** non, pas de custom issue type. Les **issue forms** (`.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*.yml`) couvrent le besoin (champs structurés, dropdowns, validation), marchent sur tous les plans GitHub, sont versionnés dans le repo, et un simple label `type:new-assistant` suffit comme discriminant pour les requêtes (`gh issue list -l type:new-assistant`). Les custom issue types natifs sont sur GitHub Enterprise Cloud avec un rollout limité — overkill ici, et migrable plus tard si besoin (un re-tag d'issues existantes).

Maintenant je refactor le plan de l'épic 2.

- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: implementation, PR opening, review, nightly build, tester recruitment, validation, merge, post-merge announcement, and later re-verification of the vendor doc against API drift. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill and PR template references it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one trigger event in the workflow. A short meta-skill helps the user pick the right one when they're not sure where they are in the process. See the plan for the proposed split.

No ZIP — the trust escape hatch is "build from source yourself", not "download a different archive format".
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: how testers post their results (GitHub PR comment with a magic sentinel), the threshold (**≥ 2 distinct testers** ✅), the rules for self-approval (the author's own subscription does not count toward the threshold).

## Scope

- A **dedicated GitHub issue** is the system of record for the entire vendor-onboarding lifecycle (request → approval → implementation → review → testing → merge → release). The PR is one artifact referenced by the issue; the issue persists before the PR exists and after it is merged. An issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` enforces the structured fields (vendor name, proposer, known credential sources, known plan variants, links to vendor's API references).
- **Phase progression is gated by a single mutex label** `phase:<step>` on the issue. Only the maintainer can transition phase labels — enforced by a GitHub Action that validates label changes and reverts unauthorized transitions.
- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: trigger event, current label, owning skill, inputs, outputs, exit condition. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill, the issue form, and the PR template reference it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one phase transition. A short meta-skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` (lighter than before — most of the structured information lives on the issue, the PR template only carries technical-review checkboxes and a "Closes #<issue>" link).
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill.

# New assistant onboarding workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: implementation, PR opening, review, nightly build, tester recruitment, validation, merge, post-merge announcement, and later re-verification of the vendor doc against API drift. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill and PR template references it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one trigger event in the workflow. A short meta-skill helps the user pick the right one when they're not sure where they are in the process. See the plan for the proposed split.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` that enforces required deliverables: dated API research doc, branding asset, contract conformance, tester sign-off section.
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR carrying an `assistant:<vendor>` label, attaches it to the PR as a sticky build comment, and points readers at `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` for the trust path (clone the branch, build locally, run). No ZIP — the trust escape hatch is "build from source yourself", not "download a different archive format".
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: how testers post their results (GitHub PR comment with a magic sentinel), the threshold (**≥ 2 distinct testers** ✅), the rules for self-approval (the author's own subscription does not count toward the threshold).

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens in the issue tracker and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists, describes every phase including its trigger event and the skill that handles it, and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- The skill family is in place under `.claude/skills/`, each skill scoped to one trigger event (implementation, review, tester follow-up, merge readiness, vendor doc re-verification). Each skill points at both `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` and `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`, and follows the "operational artifact references the spec" pattern from `.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md`.
- Opening a PR labelled `assistant:<vendor>` triggers a workflow that builds the DMG, posts (or updates) a sticky comment with a download link plus the SHA-256 and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push so a single source of truth exists for "where is the latest build".
- `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` exists and walks a non-developer reader through cloning the PR branch, building locally, and running the app — short enough that a skeptical tester can follow it without prior Swift experience.
- Every `assistant:<vendor>` PR uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and includes a "Tester sign-off" section pre-filled with the threshold rule (≥ 2 distinct non-author testers).
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist that covers contract compliance, doc clarity (including the dating discipline from the contract epic), test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated by the merge-readiness skill (or by checklist requirement) when a new assistant is merged.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: per-event skill split, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, risks.
- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate.
- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the PR / repository state.

- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill.

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens in the issue tracker and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists, describes every phase including its trigger event and the skill that handles it, and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- The skill family is in place under `.claude/skills/`, each skill scoped to one trigger event (implementation, review, tester follow-up, merge readiness, vendor doc re-verification). Each skill points at both `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` and `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`, and follows the "operational artifact references the spec" pattern from `.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md`.
- Opening a PR labelled `assistant:<vendor>` triggers a workflow that builds the DMG, posts (or updates) a sticky comment with a download link plus the SHA-256 and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push so a single source of truth exists for "where is the latest build".
- `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` exists and walks a non-developer reader through cloning the PR branch, building locally, and running the app — short enough that a skeptical tester can follow it without prior Swift experience.
- Every `assistant:<vendor>` PR uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and includes a "Tester sign-off" section pre-filled with the threshold rule (≥ 2 distinct non-author testers).
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist that covers contract compliance, doc clarity (including the dating discipline from the contract epic), test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated by the merge-readiness skill (or by checklist requirement) when a new assistant is merged.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: per-event skill split, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, risks.
- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate.
- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the PR / repository state.

# New assistant onboarding workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A **dedicated GitHub issue** is the system of record for the entire vendor-onboarding lifecycle (request → approval → implementation → review → testing → merge → release). The PR is one artifact referenced by the issue; the issue persists before the PR exists and after it is merged. An issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` enforces the structured fields (vendor name, proposer, known credential sources, known plan variants, links to vendor's API references).
- **Phase progression is gated by a single mutex label** `phase:<step>` on the issue. Only the maintainer can transition phase labels — enforced by a GitHub Action that validates label changes and reverts unauthorized transitions.
- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: trigger event, current label, owning skill, inputs, outputs, exit condition. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill, the issue form, and the PR template reference it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one phase transition. A short meta-skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` (lighter than before — most of the structured information lives on the issue, the PR template only carries technical-review checkboxes and a "Closes #<issue>" link).
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill.

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
- Custom GitHub issue types (Enterprise feature). The issue form + `type:new-assistant` label cover the same need on every plan.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens on the issue and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists, describes every phase including its trigger event and the skill that handles it, and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- The skill family is in place under `.claude/skills/`, each skill scoped to one trigger event (implementation, review, tester follow-up, merge readiness, vendor doc re-verification). Each skill points at both `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` and `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`, and follows the "operational artifact references the spec" pattern from `.claude/rules/skill-authoring.md`.
- Opening a PR labelled `assistant:<vendor>` triggers a workflow that builds the DMG, posts (or updates) a sticky comment with a download link plus the SHA-256 and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push so a single source of truth exists for "where is the latest build".
- `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` exists and walks a non-developer reader through cloning the PR branch, building locally, and running the app — short enough that a skeptical tester can follow it without prior Swift experience.
- Every `assistant:<vendor>` PR uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and includes a "Tester sign-off" section pre-filled with the threshold rule (≥ 2 distinct non-author testers).
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist that covers contract compliance, doc clarity (including the dating discipline from the contract epic), test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated by the merge-readiness skill (or by checklist requirement) when a new assistant is merged.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: per-event skill split, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, risks.
- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate.
- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the PR / repository state.

- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate.
- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the PR / repository state.
