input.old_string:
Issue body:

```
<!-- new-assistant-build:sticky -->
🛠 Build for commit `<SHA>` (PR #<n>) — vendor-debug enabled for `<vendor-slug>`

- [Download DMG](…) — SHA-256 `…`

Builds are ad-hoc signed only. First launch: right-click → Open to bypass Gatekeeper.

This build logs every API exchange with `<vendor-slug>` (sanitized) under:

    ~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/<vendor-slug>-usages-connector.log

Please attach that file to your `✅ tester-confirm` comment so the maintainer can
verify the integration end-to-end. The build sanitizes secrets (tokens, API keys,
emails) before writing to the log; if you spot anything that looks confidential,
flag it in your comment — it's a bug we want to fix before merge.

Don't trust this prebuilt DMG? Build it yourself in 5 minutes — see
[`docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`](…).
```

PR body (lighter):

```
<!-- new-assistant-build:sticky -->
🛠 Build for commit `<SHA>` (vendor-debug enabled for `<vendor-slug>`)

- [Download DMG](…) — SHA-256 `…`

For tester instructions see the linked issue.
```

input.old_string:
**Why this design**

- **Build-time activation, runtime override.** Putting the slug in `Info.plist` means a tester downloading the DMG just runs the app — no env vars, no flags. The env-var override exists for the maintainer reproducing a tester's bug locally without rebuilding.
- **Sanitization is mandatory, never optional.** Logs are the entire point of this mode, but the project doesn't trust contributors (or itself) to remember to redact each new field. The `LoggingProxy` is the choke point.
- **Per-vendor scoping.** A user happens to be running with three accounts on different vendors in the same DMG; only the vendor under test gets verbose logs. This both reduces noise and limits accidental data exposure to the vendor the tester opted into testing.
- **Reusing the existing connector log file.** No new file path to document, no new log rotation policy to configure.
input.new_string:
**Why this design**

- **Build-time activation, runtime override.** Putting the slug in `Info.plist` means a tester downloading the DMG just runs the app — no env vars, no flags. The env-var override exists for the maintainer reproducing a tester's bug locally without rebuilding.
- **Sanitization is mandatory, never optional.** Logs are the entire point of this mode, but the project doesn't trust contributors (or itself) to remember to redact each new field. The `LoggingProxy` is the choke point.
- **Per-vendor scoping.** A user happens to be running with three accounts on different vendors in the same DMG; only the vendor under test gets verbose logs. This both reduces noise and limits accidental data exposure to the vendor the tester opted into testing.
- **Reusing the existing connector log file.** No new file path to document, no new log rotation policy to configure.

### 6.5 In-app tester feedback

Active under the same conditions as tester-debug build mode. Reduces tester friction from "find the issue, copy the template, find the log file, drag-attach, submit" to "click banner, click submit, drag log".

#### Activation conditions

The feedback affordance is visible iff **all three** Info.plist keys are present in the running bundle:

- `AITrackerVendorDebug` — vendor slug under test
- `AITrackerBuildCommit` — full commit SHA (40 chars)
- `AITrackerOnboardingIssueURL` — fully-qualified GitHub issue URL

If any is missing, the feedback UI does not exist in the running app — there is no toggle, no settings entry, nothing. This guarantees that stable releases (none of the three keys baked) cannot expose the feature accidentally. The same triple is what the build script writes when invoked with `VENDOR_DEBUG`, `BUILD_COMMIT`, `ONBOARDING_ISSUE_URL` env vars.

#### UI surface

A small banner inside the popover, just above the vendor cards, when activation conditions are met:

```
🧪 You're running a tester build for <vendor-display>.
[Submit feedback →]
```

Visual styling subordinate to the usage content (it's a tester nudge, not a CTA competing with the metrics). Dismissible per-launch (a Close button on the banner only hides it for the current popover session — no persisted preference, since the build is short-lived by definition).

Clicking "Submit feedback" opens a sheet hosted by the popover (or a small detached window — implementation detail that fits whichever pattern the existing settings flow uses).

#### Form

Fields, top to bottom:

- **Build** (read-only): displays `<vendor-slug>` + the short SHA + a "Copy full SHA" affordance. Sourced from Info.plist; not editable.
- **Plan** (dropdown): Free / Pro / Team / Enterprise / Other (with a free-text field for Other).
- **macOS** (read-only, auto-detected): `ProcessInfo.operatingSystemVersionString` formatted to match the sign-off template.
- **Verified checklist**: matches the sign-off template's Verified items, each as a SwiftUI `Toggle`.
- **Connector log**: shows the log file path + size + a "Reveal in Finder" button. If the file is missing or empty, a non-blocking warning surfaces the situation but submission is still allowed (with the comment line `Connector log attached: no — file empty/missing`).
- **Notes** (free-form text, optional): translates to the `Notes:` line of the template.

A live-rendered preview of the resulting Markdown comment is shown below the form so the tester sees exactly what they're about to post.

#### Submit flow

The "Submit" button performs three actions in sequence (none of them GitHub-API-dependent):

1. Generates the comment body from the form, URL-encodes it, builds:
   `<AITrackerOnboardingIssueURL>?body=<encoded>` — bound by GitHub's URL length limit (~8 KB worst-case for query strings; the template stays well under).
2. `NSWorkspace.shared.open(url)` to open the user's default browser at the issue with the comment composer pre-filled.
3. `NSWorkspace.shared.activateFileViewerSelecting([logFileURL])` to reveal the log file in Finder, ready to drag into the GitHub composer.

A confirmation panel then displays the next step in plain language:
"Your browser opened the issue with the comment pre-filled. Drag the highlighted log file from Finder into the GitHub comment field, then click **Comment**."

The app does not track whether the comment was actually posted; the issue + the follow-up skill are the system of record for that.

#### Why no GitHub API integration in v1

- **No credential handling.** The app does not currently store any user credential it produces; introducing a GitHub PAT keychain entry just for tester-debug builds adds a security surface for marginal UX gain.
- **No auto-upload of the log.** GitHub's REST API does not expose a documented "attach a file to an issue comment" endpoint without going through gist + linking, which means an extra public artifact the tester didn't necessarily intend.
- **Browser handoff is ergonomic enough.** The hard parts (template formatting, SHA, log file location, encoding) are all handled by the app; the tester does the equivalent of "click Comment" in the browser, which is the same gesture they'd do anyway.

A v2 with optional API-based posting (PAT pasted once into Settings, kept in Keychain, used to post the comment + upload the log via gist) is plausible but explicitly out of scope here.

#### Privacy posture

The form re-asserts the same sanitization invariant the connector enforces: the log file is already sanitized at write time. The app does not re-scan the log before submission — that would be a second source of truth for what counts as confidential, and discrepancies between the connector's sanitizer and a UI-side scanner would be subtle and dangerous. The single source of truth stays the connector's `PayloadSanitizing` implementation.

A short reminder appears next to the "Reveal in Finder" button: "The log is sanitized — but if anything looks confidential, edit the file before drag-attaching, and flag it in your notes so we fix the sanitizer."

input.old_string:
## Sanitization
- [ ] `PayloadSanitizing` implementation present for the connector.
- [ ] `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` Sanitized fields section is exhaustive vs the
      observed payload (every field captured in the dated samples is either safe
      to log or listed as redacted).
- [ ] Leakage test fixture (`Tests/Fixtures/<vendor>-full-payload.json`) seeded
      with realistic-looking secrets; the test asserts none survive sanitization.
- [ ] No connector log call passes a raw payload — every payload-bearing log
      entry routes through `LoggingProxy`.

## Build & validation (after phase:testing)
- [ ] DMG present in the issue's sticky build comment, SHA-256 listed,
      vendor-debug slug shown.
- [ ] At least 2 non-author tester confirmations on the latest build SHA (issue tally).
- [ ] Each counted confirmation has an attached connector log audited clean of
      secret leakage.
- [ ] Build-from-source path verified by the reviewer (optional spot check).

input.old_string: - **Stable release accidentally ships with `AITrackerVendorDebug` set.** Mitigation: the release workflow (`release.yml`) explicitly does NOT pass `VENDOR_DEBUG`, and a unit test on the produced bundle's Info.plist asserts the key is absent. Tagged releases never carry tester-debug mode.
input.new_string:
- **Stable release accidentally ships with `AITrackerVendorDebug` set.** Mitigation: the release workflow (`release.yml`) explicitly does NOT pass `VENDOR_DEBUG` / `BUILD_COMMIT` / `ONBOARDING_ISSUE_URL`, and a unit test on the produced bundle's Info.plist asserts all three keys are absent. Tagged releases never carry tester-debug mode and never expose the in-app feedback banner.
- **In-app feedback banner shipped to the wrong issue.** If the build script is invoked with a stale or wrong `ONBOARDING_ISSUE_URL`, testers would post on an unrelated issue. Mitigation: the CI workflow derives the URL from the linked issue (the one referenced by `Closes #<n>` in the PR body) so the tooling is the source of truth, not human input. The build script refuses to bake the URL if it doesn't match the form `https://github.com/<repo>/issues/<n>`.
- **`?body=` URL too long for very chatty notes.** GitHub silently truncates URL-encoded comment bodies past ~8 KB. Mitigation: the form's free-form Notes field is character-capped (~2 KB) with a hint, the rest of the template is bounded by construction. If a tester really needs to share a long note, they fall back to the manual path or post a follow-up comment.
- **Tester clicks Submit but never finishes in the browser.** No way to detect this from the app — the issue simply doesn't get a comment. Mitigation: the meta-skill / follow-up skill remind the maintainer that "no comment = no confirmation"; the app does not pretend a submission was successful just because the browser opened.

lines: - **Build reproducibility.** Swift release builds aren't strictly bit-for-bit reproducible. The "verify SHA-256" step in `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` is documented as best-effort. The trust path is "you read the source and you built it"; SHA matching is a bonus.

- **Tester accidentally pastes raw log into the issue.** Even with sanitization at the source, a tester might quote a snippet that happens to contain a borderline value the sanitizer missed. The follow-up skill replies inline with `<redacted>` in any quoted secret pattern when posting its audit comment, but the original tester comment is still public. Mitigation: the issue template up front + the sticky build comment both explicitly tell the tester to attach the log as a *file* (not paste it), and to flag anything that looks confidential rather than quote it. If a leak does land in a comment, the maintainer edits or deletes the comment and bumps `Last verified` after fixing the sanitizer.

- **Verbose mode noise.** A connector logging every HTTP exchange in tester-debug mode produces large files quickly. Mitigated by per-vendor scoping (only the vendor under test) + existing 5 MB rotation + the short-lived nature of `phase:testing` (days, not months).

-- **Stable release accidentally ships with `AITrackerVendorDebug` set.** Mitigation: the release workflow (`release.yml`) explicitly does NOT pass `VENDOR_DEBUG`, and a unit test on the produced bundle's Info.plist asserts the key is absent. Tagged releases never carry tester-debug mode.

+- **Stable release accidentally ships with `AITrackerVendorDebug` set.** Mitigation: the release workflow (`release.yml`) explicitly does NOT pass `VENDOR_DEBUG` / `BUILD_COMMIT` / `ONBOARDING_ISSUE_URL`, and a unit test on the produced bundle's Info.plist asserts all three keys are absent. Tagged releases never carry tester-debug mode and never expose the in-app feedback banner.

+- **In-app feedback banner shipped to the wrong issue.** If the build script is invoked with a stale or wrong `ONBOARDING_ISSUE_URL`, testers would post on an unrelated issue. Mitigation: the CI workflow derives the URL from the linked issue (the one referenced by `Closes #<n>` in the PR body) so the tooling is the source of truth, not human input. The build script refuses to bake the URL if it doesn't match the form `https://github.com/<repo>/issues/<n>`.

+- **`?body=` URL too long for very chatty notes.** GitHub silently truncates URL-encoded comment bodies past ~8 KB. Mitigation: the form's free-form Notes field is character-capped (~2 KB) with a hint, the rest of the template is bounded by construction. If a tester really needs to share a long note, they fall back to the manual path or post a follow-up comment.

+- **Tester clicks Submit but never finishes in the browser.** No way to detect this from the app — the issue simply doesn't get a comment. Mitigation: the meta-skill / follow-up skill remind the maintainer that "no comment = no confirmation"; the app does not pretend a submission was successful just because the browser opened.
## 9. Deliverables checklist

content.text:
Récap des deux ajouts :

## Threading du SHA partout

- **DMG filename** : pattern `AI-Usages-Tracker-<vendor-slug>-<sha8>.dmg` (slug + 8 premiers chars du commit). Le tester peut distinguer ses téléchargements après rebase.
- **Info.plist** : nouvelle clé `AITrackerBuildCommit` (SHA full 40 chars), bakée par le build script via env var `BUILD_COMMIT`.
- **Sticky comment (issue + PR)** : SHA-8 dans le titre + SHA-40 en clair dans le body + filename DMG explicite.
- **Sign-off template** : `Build SHA: <8 chars> (full: <40 chars>)` avec ligne `Submission path: <in-app | manual>` pour distinguer les deux paths.
- **Reviewer checklist** : un item explicite que chaque confirmation comptée carry les deux formes du SHA.

## Feature in-app tester feedback (§6.5)

**Activation strictement liée à 3 clés Info.plist** (`AITrackerVendorDebug`, `AITrackerBuildCommit`, `AITrackerOnboardingIssueURL`) — toutes baked au build par le CI workflow ; releases stables ne les ont pas → banner inexistant par construction. Garde unit-test côté `release.yml` qui assert l'absence des 3.

**UI minimale dans le popover** — bandeau "🧪 You're running a tester build for `<vendor>`. [Submit feedback →]" subordonné aux cards d'usage, dismissible per-launch (pas persisté car le build est court-lived).

**Form** auto-rempli :
- Build SHA (read-only, depuis Info.plist)
- Plan (dropdown Free/Pro/Team/Enterprise/Other+free-text)
- macOS (auto-detected via `ProcessInfo`)
- Verified checklist (toggles SwiftUI matching le template)
- Connector log (path + size + "Reveal in Finder", warning non-bloquant si vide)
- Notes (capped ~2 KB pour rester sous la limite GitHub `?body=`)
- Live preview Markdown du commentaire généré

**Submit flow** sans aucune authentification GitHub :
1. URL-encode le body, ouvre `NSWorkspace.shared.open(<issue-url>?body=…)` → navigateur sur l'issue avec composer pré-rempli
2. `activateFileViewerSelecting([logFileURL])` → Finder révèle le fichier log, prêt pour drag-into-comment
3. Panneau de confirmation in-app explique en plain language : "drag le fichier highlighted depuis Finder dans le composer GitHub, click Comment"

L'app ne tente pas de tracker si le commentaire a vraiment été posté — le système de référence reste l'issue + le follow-up skill.

**Justification "no GitHub API"** : pas de credential handling à introduire, pas de risque de gist accidentel public, le browser handoff fait 90% de la valeur sans surface de sécurité. V2 avec PAT en Keychain explicitement out-of-scope (mentionnée comme évolution plausible).

**Posture privacy** : le log est déjà sanitisé à l'écriture par le `LoggingProxy` (contrat épic 1). L'app ne re-scanne pas le log à la submission — single source of truth = `PayloadSanitizing` côté connector. Reminder UX à côté du "Reveal in Finder" qui invite le testeur à éditer le fichier s'il voit quelque chose qui ressemble à un secret + flagger dans les notes pour qu'on fixe le sanitizer.

**Risques additionnels §8** : URL `?body=` truncation au-delà de ~8 KB (notes capées), banner pointant vers la mauvaise issue (mitigé par dérivation auto depuis `Closes #<n>` côté CI), tester clique submit mais ne finit jamais dans le navigateur (pas de tracking côté app, le maintainer voit juste qu'il n'y a pas de commentaire).

**Deliverables** ajoutés :
- DMG filename + 3 env vars + 3 Info.plist keys
- Garde release.yml (3 keys absent)
- Banner SwiftUI + form
- Submit flow (URL encode + NSWorkspace handoff + Finder reveal)

content.thinking:
I'm realizing the workflow needs to handle more than just new vendors—there's also the challenge of managing API evolution from existing ones. When a vendor adds new fields, we want to expose those improvements while staying compatible with older versions. But if they make breaking changes, we're stuck deciding whether to force an app update or leave older installations vulnerable.

Both scenarios share the same core steps as onboarding a new vendor—we need active testers, updated documentation with real examples, a debug build mode, and sign-offs before shipping. The key difference is that we're modifying existing infrastructure rather than building from scratch, and backward-incompatible changes create urgency since they affect all current users immediately.

I think the cleanest approach is to generalize the existing workflow rather than duplicate it. The lifecycle stays the same—triage, implement, review, test with a debug build—so I'd rather extend the current skills and issue type with a "kind" field to distinguish between new vendors and vendor evolution, keeping everything under one coherent system.

For vendor evolution specifically, I'd add a subkind to capture whether we're doing backward-compatible enrichment or introducing breaking changes, since those have different implications for testing and release notes.

Breaking changes require bumping the app version requirement and need explicit callouts in release notes so users know they need to update. For urgent fixes where the connector is currently broken, I'm thinking we could waive the two-tester threshold if at least one tester validates the fix on a real subscription—or the maintainer themselves if they have access to that vendor. That's an escape hatch for emergency situations where something is better than nothing.

For backward-compatible enrichments, the standard threshold still applies.

Now I'm looking at what needs updating in the documentation. The vendor plugin framework already covers API drift, and the evolution skill in the plan doc handles both initial capture and subsequent changes. The onboarding workflow is technically scoped to new vendors, but rather than rename the epic mid-flight, I'll extend it to cover evolution cases too—the existing structure already supports both scenarios.

Since nothing's implemented yet, I can cleanly rename the skill family from `new-assistant-*` to `assistant-*` for better clarity and future-proofing. Both new vendor onboarding and vendor evolution issues will flow through the same skills and phase labels, with the implement skill branching on issue type to determine the actual work. The reviewer checklist will have shared items plus type-specific addenda, keeping the process unified while accounting for the different contexts.

I also need to handle release notes that explicitly flag breaking changes, app version bumps for incompatible updates, and min app version annotations in vendor docs so we track which app version first supported a new API shape. On the epic file itself, I'm torn between keeping the existing slug for continuity or renaming it to reflect the expanded scope—the user's been adding to this throughout our conversation, so the epic has genuinely grown beyond just onboarding.

I'll keep the slug as-is but broaden the epic title and content to cover both new vendor onboarding and evolution of existing vendor APIs. I'll also rename the skill family from `new-assistant-*` to `assistant-*` since we're planning this from scratch anyway. That means updating the epic markdown file to reflect the wider scope, expanding the plan document to cover both issue types, and checking for any cross-references in other plan files that need updating. you happens when no actual code change is needed; "evolution" covers when code does change. So reverify is really just a lightweight case of evolution where the vendor's API stayed stable but the docs needed a refresh.

I'm thinking the cleanest split is to keep `assistant-reverify` for doc-only updates that don't need a PR, while `assistant-implement` handles both new vendors and actual evolution work. The implement skill can branch in phase A based on the issue type — if it's a new assistant, scaffold everything from scratch; if it's vendor evolution, find the existing files and apply the diff.

Now I need to actually write these changes. I'll keep it pragmatic: add a "Workflow scope" section explaining both new and evolution work, insert a section on how the two kinds branch through the same workflow, add the second issue form, and adjust the skills accordingly.

Actually, the meta-skill name matters here. If `new-assistant` now handles both new and evolution, that's misleading. I should rename the whole family to `assistant-*` — it's cleaner and the plan hasn't been implemented yet, so there's no installed base to worry about. The router reads the issue type and dispatches correctly either way, and the names will finally match what they actually do.

For labeling, I'm weighing two approaches: either separate type labels (`type:new-assistant` and `type:vendor-evolution`) with a `kind:*` qualifier on evolution issues, or collapse it to a single `type:assistant` with `kind:new` vs `kind:evolution-enrichment` etc. Two type labels feels simpler and clearer. The `phase:*` labels stay as-is since they don't reference the old naming.

But I should probably check with the user before writing this all out — they've been giving incremental feedback and asked me to update the epic plan, so maybe I should confirm these naming and labeling decisions first rather than just producing a full document.

Actually, looking back at what they said, they're trusting me to make the calls here. So I'll move forward: I'll briefly frame the changes, list the key decisions (especially the non-obvious ones like keeping the file slug but updating the title), then apply the edits across the three files that need updates.

I'm deciding to keep the epic as one workflow rather than split it into two, since the onboarding and evolution phases share most of the same structure. The main edits are renaming the skills from `new-assistant-*` to `assistant-*`, expanding the plan file with a new section on the two kinds of work, adding the second issue type, and updating the index and epic file descriptions to reflect the broader scope.

Let me start with the highest-impact changes: updating the epic file first to set the tone, then the index, then the plan file where I'll do the bulk of the work—inserting the new section, renaming skills throughout, and adding the missing issue form and risks.

content.text:
Très bon point. Le workflow actuel suppose implicitement "création d'un nouveau vendor". L'évolution d'un vendor existant partage 80% du process (même `phase:*` lifecycle, mêmes testeurs, même tester-debug build, même in-app feedback) mais diverge sur les phases d'implémentation et de release.

**Décisions de design que je propose :**

- **Un seul workflow, deux types d'issue.** `type:new-assistant` (existant) + `type:vendor-evolution` (nouveau), tous deux passant par le même `phase:*` lifecycle. Les skills lisent le type au début et adaptent leur comportement.
- **Sous-classification de l'évolution** via label `kind:enrichment` (additif backward-compat) / `kind:breaking` (incompatible, app version bump requis) / `kind:urgent-fix` (vendor a unilatéralement cassé la compat — escape hatch sur le seuil testeurs).
- **Renommage de la famille de skills** `new-assistant-*` → `assistant-*` puisque le scope n'est plus "new only". Pas encore implémenté → coût zéro.
- **`assistant-reverify` reste distinct** : c'est le cas "doc-only refresh, pas de PR" (pure mise à jour de `Last verified` quand la vérif passe sans drift). Toute évolution avec changement de code passe par le workflow normal via `type:vendor-evolution`.

J'applique tout ça.

input.old_string:
# New assistant onboarding workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.
input.new_string:
# Assistant onboarding and evolution workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of **shipping any vendor-scoped change** in AI Usages Tracker — both adding support for a new AI assistant **and** evolving the integration of an existing one when the vendor's API drifts (additive enrichments, backward-incompatible refactors, or urgent emergency fixes when the vendor unilaterally breaks compatibility).

The workflow covers, end-to-end:

- LLM-guided implementation against the vendor plugin contract.
- Auditable PR review checklist, including doc dating and sanitization compliance.
- Nightly DMG build attached to a dedicated GitHub issue for non-author testers, with verbose vendor-debug logging and an in-app feedback affordance.
- Community-validation gate before merge.
- Officialization on the next tagged release (and explicit breaking-change call-out when applicable).

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

Two kinds of work share this lifecycle:

- **`type:new-assistant`** — adding a vendor not yet supported by the app.
- **`type:vendor-evolution`** — modifying an already-supported vendor's connector when its API evolves (qualified by a `kind:enrichment` / `kind:breaking` / `kind:urgent-fix` sub-label).

Both go through the same `phase:*` labels, the same skill family, the same nightly-build pipeline, and the same tester sign-off threshold. The skills branch on issue type only where the work genuinely differs (scaffolding vs in-place refactor; README update on first ship vs none on evolution; release notes wording; whether the urgency escape hatch on the threshold applies).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# New assistant onboarding workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of adding support for a new AI assistant: from "I want to add vendor X" to "the PR is merged and X is officially supported", with an LLM-guided implementation, an auditable PR review checklist, a nightly build attached to the PR for non-author testers, and a community-validation gate before merge.

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A **dedicated GitHub issue** is the system of record for the entire vendor-onboarding lifecycle (request → approval → implementation → review → testing → merge → release). The PR is one artifact referenced by the issue; the issue persists before the PR exists and after it is merged. An issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` enforces the structured fields (vendor name, proposer, known credential sources, known plan variants, links to vendor's API references).
- **Phase progression is gated by a single mutex label** `phase:<step>` on the issue. Only the maintainer can transition phase labels — enforced by a GitHub Action that validates label changes and reverts unauthorized transitions.
- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: trigger event, current label, owning skill, inputs, outputs, exit condition. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill, the issue form, and the PR template reference it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one phase transition. A short meta-skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` (lighter than before — most of the structured information lives on the issue, the PR template only carries technical-review checkboxes and a "Closes #<issue>" link).
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill.
- **Tester-debug build mode**: the nightly DMG attached to a `type:new-assistant` PR is built with verbose logging enabled for the vendor under test (and only that vendor). Payloads are logged in full but sanitized — confidential fields stripped at the logger boundary per the contract from the [vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) epic. Testers attach the resulting log file to their sign-off comment so the maintainer can debug the implementation against real subscriptions without the testers having to hand-redact secrets.
- **In-app tester feedback**: the same nightly DMG ships with a tester-only banner in the popover that opens a feedback sheet, pre-fills the sign-off template (build SHA, vendor slug, macOS version, checklist), and on submission opens the GitHub issue in the browser with the comment body URL-encoded plus reveals the connector log in Finder so the tester can drag-attach it. No GitHub authentication required. The banner is invisible in stable releases by construction (it depends on Info.plist keys only present on tester-debug builds). The legacy manual-comment path stays documented as a fallback.
- **Build provenance everywhere**: the commit SHA is threaded through the DMG filename (`AI-Usages-Tracker-<vendor-slug>-<sha8>.dmg`), baked into the bundle's Info.plist (`AITrackerBuildCommit`, full SHA), called out in the sticky build comment, and required in the tester sign-off so a confirmation cannot be silently mis-attributed to a different build.

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
- Custom GitHub issue types (Enterprise feature). The issue form + `type:new-assistant` label cover the same need on every plan.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens on the issue and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists, describes every phase including its trigger event, the `phase:*` label transition, and the owning skill, and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- The issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` exists, applies `type:new-assistant` and `phase:proposed` automatically, and gates submission on the required fields.
- A GitHub Actions workflow validates every `phase:*` label change on `type:new-assistant` issues against a maintainers list (CODEOWNERS or an explicit whitelist). Unauthorized changes are reverted automatically.
- The skill family is in place under `.claude/skills/`, each skill scoped to one phase transition (triage, implementation, review, tester follow-up, merge, release/officialization, re-verification). Each skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label, performs its work, and applies the next phase label via `gh issue edit`.
- A meta-skill `new-assistant` reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill rather than acting itself.
- A push on a PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue triggers a workflow that builds the DMG **with the tester-debug flag set to the vendor's slug, the commit SHA baked into the bundle's Info.plist, and the issue URL baked alongside it for the in-app feedback banner**. The DMG filename includes both the vendor slug and the short SHA. The workflow posts (or updates) a sticky comment **on the issue** (and a sibling reviewer comment on the PR) with the download link, the SHA-256, the full commit SHA, the connector log path, and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push.
- `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` exists and walks a non-developer reader through cloning the PR branch, building locally, and running the app — short enough that a skeptical tester can follow it without prior Swift experience.
- Every PR for a new assistant uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and starts with `Closes #<issue>` so closing the PR feeds back into the issue lifecycle.
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) that covers contract compliance, doc clarity (including the dating discipline from the contract epic), test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness on the issue.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated by the release skill when a new assistant ships, transitioning the issue to `phase:released` and closing it.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: phase-label lifecycle, per-phase skill split, issue form + gating action, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, risks.
- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate enforced by the maintainer-only label transition.
- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the issue's current `phase:*` label and the linked PR state.
- The issue is the durable contract; the PR is a transient artifact that comes and goes within the issue's lifecycle.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Assistant onboarding and evolution workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of **shipping any vendor-scoped change** in AI Usages Tracker — both adding support for a new AI assistant **and** evolving the integration of an existing one when the vendor's API drifts (additive enrichments, backward-incompatible refactors, or urgent emergency fixes when the vendor unilaterally breaks compatibility).

The workflow covers, end-to-end:

- LLM-guided implementation against the vendor plugin contract.
- Auditable PR review checklist, including doc dating and sanitization compliance.
- Nightly DMG build attached to a dedicated GitHub issue for non-author testers, with verbose vendor-debug logging and an in-app feedback affordance.
- Community-validation gate before merge.
- Officialization on the next tagged release (and explicit breaking-change call-out when applicable).

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

Two kinds of work share this lifecycle:

- **`type:new-assistant`** — adding a vendor not yet supported by the app.
- **`type:vendor-evolution`** — modifying an already-supported vendor's connector when its API evolves (qualified by a `kind:enrichment` / `kind:breaking` / `kind:urgent-fix` sub-label).

Both go through the same `phase:*` labels, the same skill family, the same nightly-build pipeline, and the same tester sign-off threshold. The skills branch on issue type only where the work genuinely differs (scaffolding vs in-place refactor; README update on first ship vs none on evolution; release notes wording; whether the urgency escape hatch on the threshold applies).

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A **dedicated GitHub issue** is the system of record for the entire vendor-onboarding lifecycle (request → approval → implementation → review → testing → merge → release). The PR is one artifact referenced by the issue; the issue persists before the PR exists and after it is merged. An issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` enforces the structured fields (vendor name, proposer, known credential sources, known plan variants, links to vendor's API references).
- **Phase progression is gated by a single mutex label** `phase:<step>` on the issue. Only the maintainer can transition phase labels — enforced by a GitHub Action that validates label changes and reverts unauthorized transitions.
- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: trigger event, current label, owning skill, inputs, outputs, exit condition. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill, the issue form, and the PR template reference it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one phase transition. A short meta-skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` (lighter than before — most of the structured information lives on the issue, the PR template only carries technical-review checkboxes and a "Closes #<issue>" link).
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill.
- **Tester-debug build mode**: the nightly DMG attached to a `type:new-assistant` PR is built with verbose logging enabled for the vendor under test (and only that vendor). Payloads are logged in full but sanitized — confidential fields stripped at the logger boundary per the contract from the [vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) epic. Testers attach the resulting log file to their sign-off comment so the maintainer can debug the implementation against real subscriptions without the testers having to hand-redact secrets.
- **In-app tester feedback**: the same nightly DMG ships with a tester-only banner in the popover that opens a feedback sheet, pre-fills the sign-off template (build SHA, vendor slug, macOS version, checklist), and on submission opens the GitHub issue in the browser with the comment body URL-encoded plus reveals the connector log in Finder so the tester can drag-attach it. No GitHub authentication required. The banner is invisible in stable releases by construction (it depends on Info.plist keys only present on tester-debug builds). The legacy manual-comment path stays documented as a fallback.
- **Build provenance everywhere**: the commit SHA is threaded through the DMG filename (`AI-Usages-Tracker-<vendor-slug>-<sha8>.dmg`), baked into the bundle's Info.plist (`AITrackerBuildCommit`, full SHA), called out in the sticky build comment, and required in the tester sign-off so a confirmation cannot be silently mis-attributed to a different build.

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
- Custom GitHub issue types (Enterprise feature). The issue form + `type:new-assistant` label cover the same need on every plan.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens on the issue and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists, describes every phase including its trigger event, the `phase:*` label transition, and the owning skill, and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- The issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` exists, applies `type:new-assistant` and `phase:proposed` automatically, and gates submission on the required fields.
- A GitHub Actions workflow validates every `phase:*` label change on `type:new-assistant` issues against a maintainers list (CODEOWNERS or an explicit whitelist). Unauthorized changes are reverted automatically.
- The skill family is in place under `.claude/skills/`, each skill scoped to one phase transition (triage, implementation, review, tester follow-up, merge, release/officialization, re-verification). Each skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label, performs its work, and applies the next phase label via `gh issue edit`.
- A meta-skill `new-assistant` reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill rather than acting itself.
- A push on a PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue triggers a workflow that builds the DMG **with the tester-debug flag set to the vendor's slug, the commit SHA baked into the bundle's Info.plist, and the issue URL baked alongside it for the in-app feedback banner**. The DMG filename includes both the vendor slug and the short SHA. The workflow posts (or updates) a sticky comment **on the issue** (and a sibling reviewer comment on the PR) with the download link, the SHA-256, the full commit SHA, the connector log path, and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push.
- `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` exists and walks a non-developer reader through cloning the PR branch, building locally, and running the app — short enough that a skeptical tester can follow it without prior Swift experience.
- Every PR for a new assistant uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and starts with `Closes #<issue>` so closing the PR feeds back into the issue lifecycle.
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) that covers contract compliance, doc clarity (including the dating discipline from the contract epic), test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness on the issue.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated by the release skill when a new assistant ships, transitioning the issue to `phase:released` and closing it.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: phase-label lifecycle, per-phase skill split, issue form + gating action, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, risks.
- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate enforced by the maintainer-only label transition.
- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the issue's current `phase:*` label and the linked PR state.
- The issue is the durable contract; the PR is a transient artifact that comes and goes within the issue's lifecycle.

- A push on a PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue triggers a workflow that builds the DMG **with the tester-debug flag set to the vendor's slug, the commit SHA baked into the bundle's Info.plist, and the issue URL baked alongside it for the in-app feedback banner**. The DMG filename includes both the vendor slug and the short SHA. The workflow posts (or updates) a sticky comment **on the issue** (and a sibling reviewer comment on the PR) with the download link plus the SHA-256, the full commit SHA, the connector log path, and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push.

content.content:
1	# Assistant onboarding and evolution workflow
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Industrialize the end-to-end process of **shipping any vendor-scoped change** in AI Usages Tracker — both adding support for a new AI assistant **and** evolving the integration of an existing one when the vendor's API drifts (additive enrichments, backward-incompatible refactors, or urgent emergency fixes when the vendor unilaterally breaks compatibility).
6	
7	The workflow covers, end-to-end:
8	
9	- LLM-guided implementation against the vendor plugin contract.
10	- Auditable PR review checklist, including doc dating and sanitization compliance.
11	- Nightly DMG build attached to a dedicated GitHub issue for non-author testers, with verbose vendor-debug logging and an in-app feedback affordance.
12	- Community-validation gate before merge.
13	- Officialization on the next tagged release (and explicit breaking-change call-out when applicable).
14	
15	This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.
16	
17	Two kinds of work share this lifecycle:
18	
19	- **`type:new-assistant`** — adding a vendor not yet supported by the app.
20	- **`type:vendor-evolution`** — modifying an already-supported vendor's connector when its API evolves (qualified by a `kind:enrichment` / `kind:breaking` / `kind:urgent-fix` sub-label).
21	
22	Both go through the same `phase:*` labels, the same skill family, the same nightly-build pipeline, and the same tester sign-off threshold. The skills branch on issue type only where the work genuinely differs (scaffolding vs in-place refactor; README update on first ship vs none on evolution; release notes wording; whether the urgency escape hatch on the threshold applies).
23	
24	## Dependencies
25	
26	- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.
27	
28	## Scope
29	
30	- A **dedicated GitHub issue** is the system of record for the entire vendor-scoped lifecycle (request → approval → implementation → review → testing → merge → release). The PR is one artifact referenced by the issue; the issue persists before the PR exists and after it is merged.
31	- **Two issue forms** under `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`:
32	  - `new-assistant-request.yml` for new vendors (vendor name, proposer, known credential sources, known plan variants, links to vendor's API references).
33	  - `vendor-evolution-request.yml` for changes to an existing vendor (vendor slug, kind of change, observed drift, app-version-bump impact).
34	- **Phase progression is gated by a single mutex label** `phase:<step>` on the issue, identical for both kinds. Only the maintainer can transition phase labels — enforced by a GitHub Action that validates label changes and reverts unauthorized transitions.
35	- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: trigger event, current label, owning skill, inputs, outputs, exit condition. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill, the issue form, and the PR template reference it.
36	- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one phase transition. Skills are named `assistant-*` (not `new-assistant-*`) because they handle both kinds of work; the implementation skill in particular branches on issue type to either scaffold a new vendor or refactor an existing one. A short meta-skill reads the issue's `type:*` and `phase:*` labels and routes the user to the right sub-skill.
37	- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/assistant-change.md` (lighter than before — most of the structured information lives on the issue, the PR template only carries technical-review checkboxes and a "Closes #<issue>" link).
38	- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.
39	- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
40	- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; default threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill. An **emergency escape hatch** applies for `kind:urgent-fix` issues (vendor unilaterally broke compat, the connector is currently broken in production): a single non-author confirmation is acceptable to merge, with the issue staying open for follow-up confirmations after release.
41	- **Tester-debug build mode**: the nightly DMG attached to a `type:new-assistant` PR is built with verbose logging enabled for the vendor under test (and only that vendor). Payloads are logged in full but sanitized — confidential fields stripped at the logger boundary per the contract from the [vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) epic. Testers attach the resulting log file to their sign-off comment so the maintainer can debug the implementation against real subscriptions without the testers having to hand-redact secrets.
42	- **In-app tester feedback**: the same nightly DMG ships with a tester-only banner in the popover that opens a feedback sheet, pre-fills the sign-off template (build SHA, vendor slug, macOS version, checklist), and on submission opens the GitHub issue in the browser with the comment body URL-encoded plus reveals the connector log in Finder so the tester can drag-attach it. No GitHub authentication required. The banner is invisible in stable releases by construction (it depends on Info.plist keys only present on tester-debug builds). The legacy manual-comment path stays documented as a fallback.
43	- **Build provenance everywhere**: the commit SHA is threaded through the DMG filename (`AI-Usages-Tracker-<vendor-slug>-<sha8>.dmg`), baked into the bundle's Info.plist (`AITrackerBuildCommit`, full SHA), called out in the sticky build comment, and required in the tester sign-off so a confirmation cannot be silently mis-attributed to a different build.
44	
45	**Out of scope**
46	
47	- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
48	- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
49	- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
50	- Custom GitHub issue types (Enterprise feature). The issue form + `type:new-assistant` label cover the same need on every plan.
51	- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
52	- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
53	- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens on the issue and any community channel the maintainer chooses.
54	
55	## Acceptance criteria
56	
57	- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists, describes every phase including its trigger event, the `phase:*` label transition, and the owning skill, and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
58	- The issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` exists, applies `type:new-assistant` and `phase:proposed` automatically, and gates submission on the required fields.
59	- A GitHub Actions workflow validates every `phase:*` label change on `type:new-assistant` issues against a maintainers list (CODEOWNERS or an explicit whitelist). Unauthorized changes are reverted automatically.
60	- The skill family is in place under `.claude/skills/`, each skill scoped to one phase transition (triage, implementation, review, tester follow-up, merge, release/officialization, re-verification). Each skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label, performs its work, and applies the next phase label via `gh issue edit`.
61	- A meta-skill `new-assistant` reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill rather than acting itself.
62	- A push on a PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue triggers a workflow that builds the DMG **with the tester-debug flag set to the vendor's slug, the commit SHA baked into the bundle's Info.plist, and the issue URL baked alongside it for the in-app feedback banner**. The DMG filename includes both the vendor slug and the short SHA. The workflow posts (or updates) a sticky comment **on the issue** (and a sibling reviewer comment on the PR) with the download link, the SHA-256, the full commit SHA, the connector log path, and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push.
63	- `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` exists and walks a non-developer reader through cloning the PR branch, building locally, and running the app — short enough that a skeptical tester can follow it without prior Swift experience.
64	- Every PR for a new assistant uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and starts with `Closes #<issue>` so closing the PR feeds back into the issue lifecycle.
65	- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) that covers contract compliance, doc clarity (including the dating discipline from the contract epic), test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness on the issue.
66	- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated by the release skill when a new assistant ships, transitioning the issue to `phase:released` and closing it.
67	
68	## Notes
69	
70	- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: phase-label lifecycle, per-phase skill split, issue form + gating action, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, risks.
71	- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate enforced by the maintainer-only label transition.
72	- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the issue's current `phase:*` label and the linked PR state.
73	- The issue is the durable contract; the PR is a transient artifact that comes and goes within the issue's lifecycle.
74

# Assistant onboarding and evolution workflow

## Goal

Industrialize the end-to-end process of **shipping any vendor-scoped change** in AI Usages Tracker — both adding support for a new AI assistant **and** evolving the integration of an existing one when the vendor's API drifts (additive enrichments, backward-incompatible refactors, or urgent emergency fixes when the vendor unilaterally breaks compatibility).

The workflow covers, end-to-end:

- LLM-guided implementation against the vendor plugin contract.
- Auditable PR review checklist, including doc dating and sanitization compliance.
- Nightly DMG build attached to a dedicated GitHub issue for non-author testers, with verbose vendor-debug logging and an in-app feedback affordance.
- Community-validation gate before merge.
- Officialization on the next tagged release (and explicit breaking-change call-out when applicable).

This workflow spans **days to weeks of wall-clock time**, not a single LLM session. Implementation, build, tester recruitment, validation, and merge each happen at different moments and frequently in different sessions or even different machines. The skill design must reflect that — there is no single prompt that drives the whole thing.

Two kinds of work share this lifecycle:

- **`type:new-assistant`** — adding a vendor not yet supported by the app.
- **`type:vendor-evolution`** — modifying an already-supported vendor's connector when its API evolves (qualified by a `kind:enrichment` / `kind:breaking` / `kind:urgent-fix` sub-label).

Both go through the same `phase:*` labels, the same skill family, the same nightly-build pipeline, and the same tester sign-off threshold. The skills branch on issue type only where the work genuinely differs (scaffolding vs in-place refactor; README update on first ship vs none on evolution; release notes wording; whether the urgency escape hatch on the threshold applies).

## Dependencies

- [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — the contract this workflow enforces.

## Scope

- A **dedicated GitHub issue** is the system of record for the entire vendor-scoped lifecycle (request → approval → implementation → review → testing → merge → release). The PR is one artifact referenced by the issue; the issue persists before the PR exists and after it is merged.
- **Two issue forms** under `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`:
  - `new-assistant-request.yml` for new vendors (vendor name, proposer, known credential sources, known plan variants, links to vendor's API references).
  - `vendor-evolution-request.yml` for changes to an existing vendor (vendor slug, kind of change, observed drift, app-version-bump impact).
- **Phase progression is gated by a single mutex label** `phase:<step>` on the issue, identical for both kinds. Only the maintainer can transition phase labels — enforced by a GitHub Action that validates label changes and reverts unauthorized transitions.
- A `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` spec describing every phase: trigger event, current label, owning skill, inputs, outputs, exit condition. The spec is the single source of truth; every skill, the issue form, and the PR template reference it.
- A **family of complementary skills** — not a single monolithic skill — each scoped to one phase transition. Skills are named `assistant-*` (not `new-assistant-*`) because they handle both kinds of work; the implementation skill in particular branches on issue type to either scaffold a new vendor or refactor an existing one. A short meta-skill reads the issue's `type:*` and `phase:*` labels and routes the user to the right sub-skill.
- A reusable PR template at `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/assistant-change.md` (lighter than before — most of the structured information lives on the issue, the PR template only carries technical-review checkboxes and a "Closes #<issue>" link).
- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.
- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.
- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; default threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill. An **emergency escape hatch** applies for `kind:urgent-fix` issues (vendor unilaterally broke compat, the connector is currently broken in production): a single non-author confirmation is acceptable to merge, with the issue staying open for follow-up confirmations after release.
- **Tester-debug build mode**: the nightly DMG attached to a `type:new-assistant` PR is built with verbose logging enabled for the vendor under test (and only that vendor). Payloads are logged in full but sanitized — confidential fields stripped at the logger boundary per the contract from the [vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) epic. Testers attach the resulting log file to their sign-off comment so the maintainer can debug the implementation against real subscriptions without the testers having to hand-redact secrets.
- **In-app tester feedback**: the same nightly DMG ships with a tester-only banner in the popover that opens a feedback sheet, pre-fills the sign-off template (build SHA, vendor slug, macOS version, checklist), and on submission opens the GitHub issue in the browser with the comment body URL-encoded plus reveals the connector log in Finder so the tester can drag-attach it. No GitHub authentication required. The banner is invisible in stable releases by construction (it depends on Info.plist keys only present on tester-debug builds). The legacy manual-comment path stays documented as a fallback.
- **Build provenance everywhere**: the commit SHA is threaded through the DMG filename (`AI-Usages-Tracker-<vendor-slug>-<sha8>.dmg`), baked into the bundle's Info.plist (`AITrackerBuildCommit`, full SHA), called out in the sticky build comment, and required in the tester sign-off so a confirmation cannot be silently mis-attributed to a different build.

**Out of scope**

- Apple Developer ID signing or notarization. Builds remain ad-hoc signed; the build-from-source path is the trust escape hatch.
- Shipping a ZIP alongside the DMG. The DMG is the only prebuilt artifact; everything else is "clone and build".
- A scaffold shell script. Skills do the scaffolding directly.
- Custom GitHub issue types (Enterprise feature). The issue form + `type:new-assistant` label cover the same need on every plan.
- Automated payment-tier detection. Testers self-declare their plan in their feedback comment.
- A merge bot that auto-merges once the threshold is met. Merge stays manual; the workflow only surfaces readiness.
- A separate forum / chat for tester recruitment — recruitment happens on the issue and any community channel the maintainer chooses.

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` exists, describes every phase including its trigger event, the `phase:*` label transition, and the owning skill, and is referenced from the lazy-loaded section of `CLAUDE.md`.
- The issue form at `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/new-assistant-request.yml` exists, applies `type:new-assistant` and `phase:proposed` automatically, and gates submission on the required fields.
- A GitHub Actions workflow validates every `phase:*` label change on `type:new-assistant` issues against a maintainers list (CODEOWNERS or an explicit whitelist). Unauthorized changes are reverted automatically.
- The skill family is in place under `.claude/skills/`, each skill scoped to one phase transition (triage, implementation, review, tester follow-up, merge, release/officialization, re-verification). Each skill reads the issue's current `phase:*` label, performs its work, and applies the next phase label via `gh issue edit`.
- A meta-skill `new-assistant` reads the issue's current `phase:*` label and routes the user to the right sub-skill rather than acting itself.
- A push on a PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue triggers a workflow that builds the DMG **with the tester-debug flag set to the vendor's slug, the commit SHA baked into the bundle's Info.plist, and the issue URL baked alongside it for the in-app feedback banner**. The DMG filename includes both the vendor slug and the short SHA. The workflow posts (or updates) a sticky comment **on the issue** (and a sibling reviewer comment on the PR) with the download link, the SHA-256, the full commit SHA, the connector log path, and a link to `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md`. The sticky comment is updated on every push.
- `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` exists and walks a non-developer reader through cloning the PR branch, building locally, and running the app — short enough that a skeptical tester can follow it without prior Swift experience.
- Every PR for a new assistant uses `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/new-assistant.md` and starts with `Closes #<issue>` so closing the PR feeds back into the issue lifecycle.
- A reviewer can audit a PR against a single checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) that covers contract compliance, doc clarity (including the dating discipline from the contract epic), test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness on the issue.
- The README's "Supported assistants" section is updated by the release skill when a new assistant ships, transitioning the issue to `phase:released` and closing it.

## Notes

- See `new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.plan.md` for deep research: phase-label lifecycle, per-phase skill split, issue form + gating action, build-pipeline design, tester sign-off mechanics, risks.
- Skills orchestrate but never bypass review — the threshold of human ✅ confirmations remains a hard gate enforced by the maintainer-only label transition.
- The temporality is the central design constraint: each skill must be invocable cold, with no in-memory state from previous sessions, by re-reading the issue's current `phase:*` label and the linked PR state.
- The issue is the durable contract; the PR is a transient artifact that comes and goes within the issue's lifecycle.

-- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to a `type:new-assistant` issue (or carrying the matching label), and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.

-- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-NEW-ASSISTANT.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, and tester-feedback completeness.

+- A GitHub Actions workflow that builds an ad-hoc-signed **DMG** for any PR linked to either a `type:new-assistant` or a `type:vendor-evolution` issue, and posts the build pointer **on the issue** so testers don't have to dig through PR comments. The PR also gets a build comment for reviewers. Skeptical readers follow `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` to clone and build locally — no ZIP.

+- A reviewer checklist (`docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-ASSISTANT-CHANGE.md`) covering documentation clarity (including "is the dating discipline applied?"), contract compliance, test coverage, tester-feedback completeness, and per-issue-type addenda (new-vendor: branding asset and registry registration; vendor-evolution: Change-log entry, superseded samples preserved, app-version bump if breaking).

- A documented tester-validation protocol: testers comment on the **issue** (not the PR) using a magic sentinel; default threshold **≥ 2 distinct non-author testers** ✅; the issue body keeps a running tally maintained by the follow-up skill. An **emergency escape hatch** applies for `kind:urgent-fix` issues (vendor unilaterally broke compat, the connector is currently broken in production): a single non-author confirmation is acceptable to merge, with the issue staying open for follow-up confirmations after release.

- **Tester-debug build mode**: the nightly DMG attached to a `type:new-assistant` PR is built with verbose logging enabled for the vendor under test (and only that vendor). Payloads are logged in full but sanitized — confidential fields stripped at the logger boundary per the contract from the [vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) epic. Testers attach the resulting log file to their sign-off comment so the maintainer can debug the implementation against real subscriptions without the testers having to hand-redact secrets.

- **In-app tester feedback**: the same nightly DMG ships with a tester-only banner in the popover that opens a feedback sheet, pre-fills the sign-off template (build SHA, vendor slug, macOS version, checklist), and on submission opens the GitHub issue in the browser with the comment body URL-encoded plus reveals the connector log in Finder so the tester can drag-attach it. No GitHub authentication required. The banner is invisible in stable releases by construction (it depends on Info.plist keys only present on tester-debug builds). The legacy manual-comment path stays documented as a fallback.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Roadmap

Open epics for the app. This file is the single source of truth for the features still to implement and their inter-dependencies. Completed features are removed from this index and their files deleted.

Status values: `planned` | `in-progress`.

See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the process and file conventions.

## Dependency graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    buy-me-a-coffee["Buy me a coffee"]
    vendor-plugin-framework["Vendor plugin framework"]
    new-assistant-onboarding-workflow["New assistant onboarding workflow"]
    vendor-plugin-framework --> new-assistant-onboarding-workflow
```

## Epics

1. `planned` — [Buy me a coffee](buy-me-a-coffee.md) — add a discreet donation button in the popover footer that opens the developer's donation page in the browser.
2. `planned` — [Vendor plugin framework](vendor-plugin-framework.md) — define a contract-based plugin framework so adding a new AI assistant follows a single, well-documented pattern across auth, usage fetching, persistence, branding, status, and account monitoring.
3. `planned` — [New assistant onboarding workflow](new-assistant-onboarding-workflow.md) — formalize the LLM-guided implementation, PR review, nightly-build attachment, tester validation, and merge gate for shipping a new assistant.

lines: Decoupled from the issue lifecycle — the issue is closed at this point.
- **Trigger**: contributor or maintainer suspects API drift (metric value looks wrong; connector errors out; `Last verified` in the vendor doc is months old).
-- **Skill**: `new-assistant-reverify` (see §3.7).
+- **Skill**: `assistant-reverify` (see §3.7).

- **Outputs**: updated `docs/vendors/<vendor>.md` with bumped `Last verified`, new dated samples, change-log entry; a follow-up PR for any code adjustment forced by the drift. No new onboarding issue is opened — drift fixes go through the regular PR flow.
## 3. Skill family
listing the testers.
-### 3.7 `new-assistant-reverify`
+### 3.7 `assistant-reverify`
Decoupled from the issue lifecycle — operates on the live vendor doc.
-name: new-assistant-reverify
+name: assistant-reverify

description: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, append a Change log entry, and stage a follow-up PR if needed. Use when API drift is suspected.
model: opus

- **Tester recruitment latency.** Some vendors have small audiences; getting two confirmations may take days/weeks. The workflow does not pressure-merge — the issue simply waits at `phase:testing`. The README should call this out so contributors know the timeline up front.

- **Plan-variant coverage gap.** A tester on Free plan does not validate Paid behavior. The threshold of ≥ 2 testers is a floor; the reviewer checklist asks the maintainer to weigh whether all documented "verified" plan variants are covered before transitioning to `phase:merge-ready`. "Known unknowns" in the vendor doc capture variants left unverified.

- **Sticky-comment duplication on PR + issue.** Two posting points means twice the room for race conditions. Mitigated by `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true` on the build workflow and the sentinel-driven find-or-create pattern.

-- **Vendor doc maintenance over time.** Mitigation: the contract-side rule "if you touch the connector, you bump `Last verified` and append a Change log entry" + the optional `new-assistant-reverify` skill.

+- **Vendor doc maintenance over time.** Mitigation: the contract-side rule "if you touch the connector, you bump `Last verified` and append a Change log entry" + the optional `assistant-reverify` skill.

- **Build reproducibility.** Swift release builds aren't strictly bit-for-bit reproducible. The "verify SHA-256" step in `docs/CHECKOUT-AND-BUILD.md` is documented as best-effort. The trust path is "you read the source and you built it"; SHA matching is a bonus.

- **Tester accidentally pastes raw log into the issue.** Even with sanitization at the source, a tester might quote a snippet that happens to contain a borderline value the sanitizer missed. The follow-up skill replies inline with `<redacted>` in any quoted secret pattern when posting its audit comment, but the original tester comment is still public. Mitigation: the issue template up front + the sticky build comment both explicitly tell the tester to attach the log as a *file* (not paste it), and to flag anything that looks confidential rather than quote it. If a leak does land in a comment, the maintainer edits or deletes the comment and bumps `Last verified` after fixing the sanitizer.

- **Verbose mode noise.** A connector logging every HTTP exchange in tester-debug mode produces large files quickly. Mitigated by per-vendor scoping (only the vendor under test) + existing 5 MB rotation + the short-lived nature of `phase:testing` (days, not months).

input.new_string:
### 3.0 `assistant` (meta router)

```
---
name: assistant
description: Routes to the correct sub-skill of the assistant family based on the issue's type:* and phase:* labels. Use when the user mentions a vendor onboarding or evolution and is unsure which skill to run.
model: sonnet
---

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the type ↔ phase ↔ skill map.

Argument: issue number.

Read the issue. Identify both:
  - issue type: `type:new-assistant` vs `type:vendor-evolution`
  - phase: `phase:*` label

Name the matching sub-skill (every sub-skill handles both issue types — they
read the type themselves and adapt — so the router is type-agnostic at this
level). Stop. Do not perform work yourself. The user invokes the named
sub-skill explicitly.
```

content.text: Maintenant les ajustements sémantiques importants — le déclenchement du workflow, la phase 2.1, le coverage des deux types dans les phases.

input.old_string: A single mutex label `phase:<step>` lives on every `type:new-assistant` issue. Exactly one phase label is set at any time. Transitions are unidirectional except for explicit rollback (e.g., `phase:review` back to `phase:implementing` if the review demands rework).
input.new_string:
A single mutex label `phase:<step>` lives on every issue carrying `type:new-assistant` or `type:vendor-evolution`. Exactly one phase label is set at any time. Transitions are unidirectional except for explicit rollback (e.g., `phase:review` back to `phase:implementing` if the review demands rework).

The phase set is identical for both issue types — the lifecycle does not branch at the label level. What branches is the **work performed at each phase**, controlled by the issue's type and (for vendor-evolution issues) its `kind:*` qualifier:

- `kind:enrichment` — backward-compatible additions (new fields, new metrics surfaced). Standard threshold (≥ 2 testers); no app version bump required.
- `kind:breaking` — backward-incompatible API change. Standard threshold; the next tagged release is bumped accordingly and the release notes carry an explicit "Breaking change for `<vendor>`" callout.
- `kind:urgent-fix` — vendor unilaterally broke compat, the connector is currently broken in the field. **Emergency escape hatch**: a single non-author tester confirmation on the latest build SHA is acceptable to merge; the issue stays open after release in `phase:released-pending-confirmations` (or simply at `phase:released` with a note) so additional tester ✅s arriving post-release backfill the validation. The release notes call out the urgency.
